topics.title: Classification of Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
In ocean wave hydrodynamics, **Classification of Waves** refers to the systematic categorization of water waves based on distinct physical criteria such as the restoring force, wave period (frequency), relative water depth, wave-breaking characteristics, and mode of generation. This classification is the foundational first step in selecting the appropriate linear or non-linear wave theory for analyzing real ocean behavior in coastal and offshore engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 lays the conceptual foundation. Students are expected to identify wave types by their governing physics (gravity, capillary, tidal) and by the ratio of water depth to wavelength, since this directly dictates which mathematical model (Airy, Stokes, Cnoidal, or Solitary) is valid.

### Primary Restoration-Based Classification (The Core Taxonomy)
The most fundamental classification in coastal engineering is based on the **restoring force** that brings the disturbed water surface back to equilibrium:

| Wave Type | Restoring Force | Typical Period (T) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Capillary Waves** | Surface Tension | $T < 0.1$ sec |
| **Gravity Waves (Short)** | Gravity | $0.1 < T < 30$ sec |
| **Gravity Waves (Long)** | Gravity + Coriolis | $30 \text{ sec} < T < 12 \text{ hr}$ |
| **Tidal Waves** | Gravity + Astronomical | $12 \text{ hr} < T < 24 \text{ hr}$ |
| **Trans-Tidal / Tsunamis** | Gravity (Seismic) | Minutes to hours |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine throwing a pebble into a pond. You immediately see tiny ripples on the surface ŌĆö these are **capillary waves**, held together like a stretched elastic membrane by surface tension. The bigger swells that roll outward are **gravity waves**, where Earth's gravity is the "anchor" pulling the water back down. If you wait for ocean tides that rise and fall over half a day, those are driven by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun. In short: every ocean wave is the sea's way of "righting itself" ŌĆö the *force doing the righting* defines its class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The relative depth parameter $d/L$ (depth-to-wavelength ratio) is the single most important non-dimensional number in coastal hydrodynamics. It determines wave speed, kinematics, and energy distribution with depth.

---

### Secondary Classification Criteria (Quick Overview)
1. **Based on Relative Depth ($d/L$):** Deep, Transitional, Shallow.
2. **Based on Wave Breaking:** Non-breaking, Spilling, Plunging, Surging, Collapsing.
3. **Based on Wave Form:** Sinusoidal, Trochoidal, Cnoidal, Solitary.
4. **Based on Propagation:** Progressive (Traveling) and Standing (Clapotis).
5. **Based on Particle Motion:** Oscillatory and Translatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification by Relative Depth (Most Exam-Relevant)
The parameter $d/L$ (where $d$ = water depth, $L$ = wavelength) divides ocean waves into three regimes:

- **Deep Water Waves:** $d/L > 1/2$ (equivalently, $d > L/2$)
  - The seabed has negligible influence on surface motion.
  - Particle orbits are nearly perfect circles that decay exponentially with depth.
  - Wave celerity: $C = C_0 = \sqrt{gL/(2\pi)}$ ŌĆö depends only on $L$, not $d$.

- **Transitional (Intermediate) Water Waves:** $1/20 < d/L < 1/2$
  - Both gravity and depth effects are significant.
  - Wave celerity: $C = \sqrt{(gL/2\pi)\tanh(kd)}$ ŌĆö depends on both $L$ and $d$.

- **Shallow Water Waves:** $d/L < 1/20$
  - The seabed strongly governs wave behavior.
  - Wave celerity: $C = C_s = \sqrt{gd}$ ŌĆö depends only on depth.

### 2.2 Classification by Period (Detailed Bands)
Following the standard oceanographic spectrum:

- **Capillary waves** ($T < 0.1$ s): Wavelengths $\lambda < 1.7$ cm; dominated by surface tension.
- **Ultra-gravity waves** ($0.1 \text{ s} < T < 1$ s): Ripples transitioning to gravity waves.
- **Gravity (wind) waves** ($1 \text{ s} < T < 30$ s): Sea, swell ŌĆö most important for ship and coastal engineering.
- **Infragravity waves** ($30 \text{ s} < T < 5$ min): Bound and free long waves generated by wave groups.
- **Long-period waves** ($5$ min $< T < 12$ hr): Tsunamis, storm surges, harbor seiches.
- **Tidal waves** ($12$ hr $< T < 24$ hr): Astronomical forcing from Sun and Moon.

### 2.3 Classification by Wave Breaking (McCowan / Galvin Criteria)
Wave breaking occurs when the local wave steepness $H/L$ exceeds a depth-limited threshold. Galvin's classic 1968 classification:

- **Spilling:** Foam spills gently down the front face; occurs on gentle slopes ($m < 1/50$).
- **Plunging:** Crest curls forward and falls violently; steepest breaker; $1/50 < m < 1/10$.
- **Surging:** Wave does not break but surges up the beach; $m > 1/10$.
- **Collapsing:** A hybrid ŌĆö the lower face collapses while the crest remains intact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breaker Criterion (McCowan, 1894):** In shallow water, a wave breaks when $H_b / d_b \approx 0.78$. This is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.4 Classification by Wave Form (Theoretical Models)
| Wave Theory | Applicable Range | Key Limitation |
|-------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Airy (Linear)** | Small $H/L$, all depths | Fails for steep waves |
| **Stokes 2nd / 3rd / 5th Order** | Deep to intermediate, finite steepness | Breaks down in very shallow water |
| **Cnoidal** | Shallow water, long waves | Cannot model solitary limit |
| **Solitary** | Shallow water, single crest | No dispersion, no trough |
| **Trochoidal** | Historical, finite amplitude deep water | Overestimates crest, fails at breaking |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|--------------------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | $C = L/T$ | Celerity, Wavelength, Period | Universal definition |
| 2 | $C_0 = \sqrt{gL_0/(2\pi)}$ | Deep-water celerity | Deep-water regime |
| 3 | $C = \sqrt{(gL/2\pi)\tanh(kd)}$ | General celerity | All depths |
| 4 | $C_s = \sqrt{gd}$ | Shallow-water celerity | $d/L < 1/20$ |
| 5 | $L_0 = (gT^2)/(2\pi)$ | Deep-water wavelength | Open ocean |
| 6 | $L = L_0 \tanh(kd)$ | Dispersion relation | All depths |
| 7 | $H_b / d_b \approx 0.78$ | Breaker index | Wave breaking |
| 8 | $H_{max}/L_0 = 1/7$ | Maximum steepness (Miche) | Deep-water breaking |
| 9 | $C_g = (C/2)[1 + 2kd/\sinh(2kd)]$ | Group velocity | Wave energy transport |
| 10 | $C_{g,0} = C_0/2$ | Deep-water group velocity | Half of phase speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These classifications directly govern the design of breakwaters, offshore platforms, ship hulls, and coastal protection works. Selecting the wrong wave theory from a wrong classification can yield errors exceeding 40% in force or runup predictions.

### 2.6 Classification by Particle Motion
- **Oscillatory Waves:** Water particles move in closed orbits (circular/elliptical); no net mass transport beyond the second-order Stokes drift.
- **Translatory Waves:** Net horizontal mass transport occurs (e.g., breaking waves, surges, translation solitary waves).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Dispersion Relation (Foundation of Classification)
The dispersion relation is the **mathematical heart** of wave classification by depth. Its derivation begins with the Laplace equation for an incompressible, inviscid fluid and irrotational flow.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Governing Equations.**
Continuity (incompressibility) gives the Laplace equation:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

where $\phi(x,z,t)$ is the velocity potential.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Boundary Conditions (BCs).**
- **(a) Kinematic free-surface BC** (at $z = \eta$):

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$$

- **(b) Dynamic free-surface BC** (at $z = \eta$):

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{2}\vert \nabla\phi \vert^2 + g\eta = 0$$

- **(c) Seabed BC** (at $z = -d$, no flow through bottom):

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linearization.**
Linearize for small amplitude ($H \ll L$, $H \ll d$), and combine (a) and (b) at $z = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Separable Solution.**
Assume:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = f(z)\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Substituting into Laplace equation:

$$\frac{d^2 f}{dz^2} - k^2 f = 0 \implies f(z) = A\cosh(kz) + B\sinh(kz)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply Seabed BC.**
At $z = -d$: $f'(z) = -Ak\sinh(-kd) + Bk\cosh(-kd) = 0 \implies B = A\tanh(kd)$.

So:

$$f(z) = A[\cosh(kz) + \tanh(kd)\sinh(kz)] = \frac{A\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Free-Surface BC.**

$$-\omega^2 \cdot f(0) + g \cdot f'(0) = 0$$

Evaluating:

$$f(0) = A, \quad f'(0) = Ak\tanh(kd)$$

Thus:

$$-\omega^2 A + g A k \tanh(kd) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Convert to Engineering Variables.**
Using $\omega = 2\pi/T$ and $k = 2\pi/L$, and $C = \omega/k = L/T$:

$$C^2 = \frac{g}{k}\tanh(kd) = \frac{gL}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$$

This is the **General Dispersion Relation** governing all linear wave classification.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Depth Regimes from the Dispersion Relation

| Regime | Condition on $kd$ | $\tanh(kd)$ Approx. | Resulting $C$ |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Deep Water | $kd \geq \pi$ | $\tanh(kd) \approx 1$ | $C_0 = \sqrt{gL_0/2\pi}$ |
| Shallow Water | $kd \leq \pi/10$ | $\tanh(kd) \approx kd$ | $C_s = \sqrt{gd}$ |
| Intermediate | $\pi/10 < kd < \pi$ | Full $\tanh(kd)$ | $C = \sqrt{gL/2\pi \cdot \tanh(kd)}$ |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
**Problem:** A wave with period $T = 8$ s propagates in water depth $d = 10$ m. Classify the wave and compute its celerity.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Deep-Water Wavelength:**

$$L_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 64}{2\pi} = \frac{627.84}{6.2832} \approx 99.93 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Initial Trial Ratio:**

$$\frac{d}{L_0} = \frac{10}{99.93} \approx 0.10$$

Since $0.05 < d/L_0 < 0.5$, it lies in the **intermediate regime**. We must iterate.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the Dispersion Relation Iteratively.**
We need $L$ such that:

$$L = L_0 \tanh(2\pi d/L)$$

Trial 1: Assume $L = L_0 = 99.93$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 99.93) = \tanh(0.6283) = 0.5571$
$L_1 = 99.93 \times 0.5571 = 55.68$ m

Trial 2: $L = 55.68$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 55.68) = \tanh(1.128) = 0.8113$
$L_2 = 99.93 \times 0.8113 = 81.08$ m

Trial 3: $L = 81.08$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 81.08) = \tanh(0.7748) = 0.6496$
$L_3 = 99.93 \times 0.6496 = 64.92$ m

Trial 4: $L = 64.92$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 64.92) = \tanh(0.9677) = 0.7462$
$L_4 = 99.93 \times 0.7462 = 74.57$ m

Trial 5: $L = 74.57$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 74.57) = \tanh(0.8424) = 0.6863$
$L_5 = 99.93 \times 0.6863 = 68.59$ m

Trial 6: $L = 68.59$ m:
$\tanh(2\pi \cdot 10 / 68.59) = \tanh(0.9158) = 0.7224$
$L_6 = 99.93 \times 0.7224 = 72.20$ m

Convergence to **$L \approx 72$ m**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Classify:**
$d/L = 10/72 \approx 0.139$.
Since $0.05 < 0.139 < 0.5$, confirmed **Intermediate Water Wave**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Celerity:**

$$C = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{72}{8} = 9.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Verification with general formula:**
$C = \sqrt{(gL/2\pi)\tanh(2\pi d/L)} = \sqrt{(9.81 \times 72/2\pi) \times 0.7224}$
$C = \sqrt{112.4 \times 0.7224} = \sqrt{81.20} = 9.01$ m/s. Ō£ō

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)
The following architecture diagram maps all classifications discussed in Sections 1 and 2. Use this as a single-look mental map before any examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[OCEAN WAVES]:::root

    ROOT --> A[By Restoring Force]:::branch
    ROOT --> B[By Relative Depth d slash L]:::branch
    ROOT --> C[By Wave Breaking]:::branch
    ROOT --> D[By Wave Form]:::branch
    ROOT --> E[By Particle Motion]:::branch
    ROOT --> F[By Propagation Mode]:::branch

    A --> A1[Capillary T less than 0.1 s]:::leaf
    A --> A2[Gravity Wind Waves 1 to 30 s]:::leaf
    A --> A3[Infragravity 30 s to 5 min]:::leaf
    A --> A4[Long Period Tsunami Surge]:::leaf
    A --> A5[Tidal 12 to 24 hr]:::leaf

    B --> B1[Deep d slash L greater than 0.5]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Intermediate 0.05 to 0.5]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Shallow d slash L less than 0.05]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Non Breaking]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Spilling]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Plunging]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Surging]:::leaf
    C --> C5[Collapsing]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Airy Linear]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Stokes 2nd 3rd 5th]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Cnoidal]:::leaf
    D --> D4[Solitary]:::leaf
    D --> D5[Trochoidal]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Oscillatory Closed Orbits]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Translatory Net Mass Transport]:::leaf

    F --> F1[Progressive Travelling]:::leaf
    F --> F2[Standing Clapotis]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#0B3D91,stroke:#062B6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold
    classDef branch fill:#F5C518,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000,font-weight:bold
    classDef leaf fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#0B3D91,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B3D91
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Selecting a Wave Theory
Use this decision tree when solving numerical problems ŌĆö examiners often test whether you can justify theory selection from classification alone.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Given H L d]:::root --> Q1{Is d slash L greater than 0.5}:::branch
    Q1 -- Yes --> D1[Use Deep Water Airy]:::leaf
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is d slash L less than 0.05}:::branch
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is H slash d greater than 0.78}:::branch
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is H slash L greater than 0.05}:::branch
    Q3 -- Yes --> D2[Wave is Breaking]:::leaf
    Q3 -- No --> D3[Use Cnoidal or Solitary]:::leaf
    Q4 -- Yes --> D4[Use Stokes Higher Order]:::leaf
    Q4 -- No --> D5[Use Linear Airy]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#0B3D91,stroke:#062B6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef branch fill:#F5C518,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    classDef leaf fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#0B3D91,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B3D91
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Classify ocean waves based on relative depth $d/L$. State the dispersion relation valid for each regime.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Based on the ratio of water depth $d$ to wavelength $L$:
1. **Deep water waves** ŌĆö $d/L > 1/2$: Celerity $C_0 = \sqrt{gL_0/2\pi}$, independent of $d$.
2. **Intermediate water waves** ŌĆö $1/20 < d/L < 1/2$: Celerity $C = \sqrt{(gL/2\pi)\tanh(kd)}$, depends on both.
3. **Shallow water waves** ŌĆö $d/L < 1/20$: Celerity $C_s = \sqrt{gd}$, depends only on depth.

*(1 mark per regime with its dispersion relation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
*Distinguish between spilling, plunging, and surging breakers. What parameter controls this classification?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **beach slope ($m$)** and **wave steepness ($H/L$)** control breaker type (Galvin, 1968):
- **Spilling breakers:** Occur on mild slopes ($m < 1/50$); foam gradually spills down the front face.
- **Plunging breakers:** Occur on moderate slopes ($1/50 < m < 1/10$); crest curls forward and falls violently ŌĆö most energetic.
- **Surging breakers:** Occur on steep slopes ($m > 1/10$); wave does not break but surges up the beach.

*(1 mark per type + identification of slope as controlling parameter.)*

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Depth Classification & Dispersion
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify ocean waves based on the relative depth parameter $d/L$. For each classification, derive the corresponding dispersion relation and celerity expression from the general dispersion equation:

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$$

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A wave of period $T = 10$ s and height $H = 2$ m propagates in a coastal region where the depth $d = 25$ m. Classify the wave and compute (i) its wavelength, (ii) its phase celerity, and (iii) its group velocity.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the general dispersion relation:

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd) \quad \text{and} \quad C = \frac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\tanh(kd)} = \sqrt{\frac{gL}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)}$$

**Case 1 ŌĆö Deep water ($d/L > 1/2$, i.e., $kd > \pi$):**
For large $kd$, $\tanh(kd) \to 1$. Substituting:

$$C_0 = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{gL_0}{2\pi}}$$

Wavelength is governed only by period: $L_0 = gT^2/2\pi$. Depth $d$ does not appear.

**Case 2 ŌĆö Shallow water ($d/L < 1/20$, i.e., $kd < \pi/10$):**
For small $kd$, $\tanh(kd) \approx kd$. Substituting:

$$C_s = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k} \cdot kd} = \sqrt{gd}$$

Celerity depends only on depth. Wavelength $L = T\sqrt{gd}$.

**Case 3 ŌĆö Intermediate ($1/20 < d/L < 1/2$):**
No approximation possible; full $\tanh(kd)$ must be retained, and iteration is required.

*(2 marks per regime derivation = 6 marks; 1 mark for clear regime boundaries.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step (i) ŌĆö Deep-water wavelength:**
$L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = (9.81 \times 100)/(6.2832) = 156.13$ m.

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Initial ratio:**
$d/L_0 = 25/156.13 = 0.160$ ŌåÆ Intermediate regime. Iterate the dispersion relation $L = L_0 \tanh(2\pi d / L)$.

Trial 1 ($L = 156.13$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 25/156.13) = \tanh(1.006) = 0.7660$; $L_1 = 119.6$ m.
Trial 2 ($L = 119.6$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 25/119.6) = \tanh(1.313) = 0.8653$; $L_2 = 135.1$ m.
Trial 3 ($L = 135.1$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 25/135.1) = \tanh(1.162) = 0.8239$; $L_3 = 128.6$ m.
Trial 4 ($L = 128.6$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 25/128.6) = \tanh(1.221) = 0.8404$; $L_4 = 131.2$ m.
Trial 5 ($L = 131.2$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 25/131.2) = \tanh(1.197) = 0.8338$; $L_5 = 130.2$ m.

**Converged value: $L \approx 130$ m.**

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Phase celerity:**
$C = L/T = 130/10 = 13.0$ m/s.

**Step (iv) ŌĆö Group velocity:**
Using $C_g = (C/2)[1 + 2kd/\sinh(2kd)]$ with $kd = 2\pi \cdot 25/130 = 1.208$:
$\sinh(2.416) = 5.594$
$C_g = (13/2)[1 + 2 \times 1.208/5.594] = 6.5 \times [1 + 0.432] = 6.5 \times 1.432 = 9.31$ m/s.

*(2 marks classification + 3 marks L + 1 mark C + 1 mark Cg = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Generation & Breaking Classification
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify ocean waves according to their generating forces. Give two representative examples for each category with typical period ranges.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A wave train approaches a beach with bottom slope $m = 1/30$. The deep-water wave height $H_0 = 1.8$ m and deep-water wavelength $L_0 = 60$ m. Determine the breaker type (Spilling/Plunging/Surging) and estimate the breaker height and breaker depth using the McCowan and Galvin criteria.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification by Generation (7 marks):**

1. **Wind-generated waves** (period 1ŌĆō30 s): Sea (under active wind forcing) and swell (after wind dies). Example: Storm waves in the Arabian Sea.
2. **Tidal waves** (period 12ŌĆō24 hr): Driven by gravitational pull of Moon and Sun. Example: Semi-diurnal tide along Kerala coast.
3. **Tsunamis** (period 5ŌĆō60 min): Generated by submarine earthquakes, landslides, or volcanic eruptions. Example: 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami.
4. **Storm surges** (period hours): Wind setup and atmospheric pressure drop during cyclones. Example: 2023 Cyclone Biparjoy surge.
5. **Seiches** (period minutes to hours): Standing oscillations in enclosed/semi-enclosed basins. Example: Harbor oscillation at Vizhinjam.
6. **Ship wakes** (period 2ŌĆō10 s): Kelvin wake pattern from vessel hulls.
7. **Landslide-generated impulse waves** (period seconds): Coastal cliff failures.

*(7 marks for complete enumeration ŌĆö 1 mark per category with example + period.)*

**(b) Breaker Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify slope regime:**
$m = 1/30 = 0.0333$. Since $1/50 = 0.02 < 0.0333 < 1/10 = 0.10$, the slope is **moderate**, suggesting a **Plunging breaker** (Galvin classification).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute deep-water steepness:**
$H_0/L_0 = 1.8/60 = 0.03$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Miche's criterion for maximum deep-water steepness:**
$H_0/L_0 = 0.03 < 0.142$ (Miche limit $1/7$). Wave is not yet at deep-water breaking limit ŌĆö will break in shallow water.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply McCowan's breaker index:**
$H_b/d_b = 0.78 \implies H_b = 0.78 d_b$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply energy flux conservation from deep to breaking point:**
Using linear shoaling: $H_b = H_0 \cdot K_s$, where the shoaling coefficient for moderate slopes is approximated as $K_s \approx 1.0$ to $1.2$. Take $K_s = 1.1$:
$H_b = 1.8 \times 1.1 = 1.98$ m.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute breaker depth:**
$d_b = H_b / 0.78 = 1.98 / 0.78 = 2.54$ m.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final classification:**
The breaker is **Plunging** based on slope regime ($1/50 < m < 1/10$), with estimated $H_b \approx 2.0$ m at depth $d_b \approx 2.5$ m.

*(2 marks slope identification + 2 marks McCowan application + 2 marks breaker height/depth + 1 mark final classification.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Classification Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing regimes:** Do NOT classify a wave as "deep" merely because the ocean looks deep. The correct check is $d/L > 1/2$, NOT $d$ being large in absolute terms. A 100 m depth with $L = 400$ m is intermediate, not deep.
> 2. **Forgetting to iterate:** When $d/L$ falls in the intermediate range, you MUST iterate $L = L_0 \tanh(2\pi d/L)$ until convergence. Using $L_0$ directly will lose 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing breaking slope with beach slope:** The breaker type depends on the **local bottom slope near breaking**, not the average beach slope.
> 4. **McCowan vs. Miche confusion:** McCowan ($H_b/d_b = 0.78$) applies in shallow water. Miche ($H_{max}/L_0 = 1/7$) applies in deep water. Examiners deduct marks for mixing them up.
> 5. **Units in celerity problems:** Always show $C = L/T$ in m/s; do not leave answer in $L/T$ symbolic form when numerical values are given.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Restoring force** is the master key ŌĆö gravity for ocean engineering; capillary only for ripples.
- **Relative depth $d/L$** determines the mathematical model. Memorize: $> 1/2$ deep, $< 1/20$ shallow, in between transitional.
- **General dispersion relation:** $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ ŌĆö the universal equation from which all depth regimes are derived.
- **Celerity rules:** Deep water $C_0 = \sqrt{gL_0/2\pi}$; Shallow water $C_s = \sqrt{gd}$.
- **Wavelength shortcut in shallow water:** $L = T\sqrt{gd}$.
- **Group velocity in deep water is half the phase velocity:** $C_{g,0} = C_0/2$.
- **Breaker types** are governed by **beach slope** (Galvin): Spilling ($m < 1/50$), Plunging ($1/50 < m < 1/10$), Surging ($m > 1/10$), Collapsing (hybrid).
- **McCowan's index:** $H_b/d_b \approx 0.78$ ŌĆö shallow-water breaker criterion.
- **Miche's limit:** $H_{max}/L_0 = 1/7$ ŌĆö deep-water breaking steepness.
- **Particle motion:** Oscillatory (closed orbits) vs. Translatory (net transport).
- **Wave theories by form:** Airy (linear), Stokes (finite steepness), Cnoidal (long shallow), Solitary (single crest), Trochoidal (historical).
- **Always classify first**, then select the appropriate theory ŌĆö this is the KTU examiner's expected workflow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of the Velocity Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Velocity Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Potential ($\phi$)** is a scalar field whose gradient at any point in an incompressible, irrotational fluid flow field gives the velocity vector at that point. Mathematically, the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ is related to the velocity potential by:
> $$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$
> where $\nabla$ is the gradient operator. For a three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system, this yields the three velocity components:
> $$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

The concept of velocity potential is valid only when the flow is **irrotational**, meaning the vorticity vector $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ everywhere in the flow field. This condition allows the existence of a single-valued scalar potential function $\phi$.

For an incompressible fluid (where the density $\rho$ is constant, typically **1025 kg/m┬│** for seawater), the continuity equation combined with the irrotationality condition yields **Laplace's Equation**, which governs the spatial behaviour of the velocity potential in ocean wave hydrodynamics:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hiking on a mountainous terrain, and you have a topographic map that shows elevation contours. The velocity potential $\phi$ is like this "elevation map" for fluid flow. Just as water flows from high elevation to low elevation (downhill) along the steepest path (the gradient), fluid particles flow in the direction of steepest descent of $\phi$. The velocity of flow at any point is determined by **how steeply $\phi$ changes** in space, not by the absolute value of $\phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Think of $\phi$ as the "pressure of motion" ŌĆö even though $\phi$ itself has no direct physical meaning, its spatial variations dictate how every fluid particle in the ocean moves. For ocean waves, this potential-based formulation drastically simplifies otherwise complex vector calculus into a single elegant scalar equation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of fresh water | $\rho$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The derivation of the velocity potential relies on **two fundamental assumptions** in classical ocean wave theory (Airy / Linear Wave Theory framework):

1. **Incompressibility:** The fluid density $\rho$ is constant and does not change with time or position.
2. **Irrotationality:** The flow has zero vorticity, meaning each fluid element undergoes no net rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions are central to KTU Module 1. Most ocean engineering problems (wave forces on offshore structures, ship hydrodynamics, coastal sediment transport) begin with this potential flow framework.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vorticity Definition:**
Vorticity $\vec{\omega}$ measures the local rotation rate of fluid elements and is defined as the curl of the velocity field:
$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Irrotational Condition:**
If $\vec{\omega} = 0$, then $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, meaning the curl of the velocity vector vanishes everywhere.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Vector Identity:**
By a fundamental vector identity, any vector field with zero curl can be expressed as the gradient of a scalar potential:
$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mass Conservation:**
For an incompressible fluid, the continuity equation simplifies to the **divergence-free** condition:
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Combining the Conditions:**
Substituting $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ into the continuity equation yields:
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

This final result is Laplace's equation, a linear, second-order **partial differential equation (PDE)** that is the cornerstone of linear wave theory.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Velocity from potential | $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ | Horizontal velocity component |
| Velocity from potential | $w = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$ | Vertical velocity component |
| Irrotationality (3D) | $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0$ | Zero y-axis rotation |
| Irrotationality (2D) | $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$ | Zero z-axis rotation |
| Continuity (incompressible) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Mass conservation |
| Governing PDE | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Laplace's Equation |
| Bernoulli's Equation | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \dfrac{P}{\rho} + gz + \dfrac{1}{2}\vert\vec{V}\vert^2 = f(t)$ | Pressure-velocity coupling |
| Stream function relation | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad w = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Cauchy-Riemann analogue |
| Cauchy-Riemann equations | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | 2D potential-flow coupling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The velocity potential framework is the mathematical backbone of:

- **Offshore structure design** ŌĆö predicting wave-induced pressures on oil rigs, monopiles, and floating platforms.
- **Ship hydrodynamics** ŌĆö computing wave resistance, diffraction, and radiation forces.
- **Coastal engineering** ŌĆö analyzing harbour oscillations, wave run-up, and breakwater design.
- **Renewable energy** ŌĆö estimating power extraction from oscillating water column (OWC) devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can justify the assumptions (incompressibility and irrotationality) before stating Laplace's equation. Always state both assumptions explicitly in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Velocity Potential from First Principles

We begin with the **continuity equation** for a fluid element and the **irrotationality condition** for potential flow.

#### Step A: The Continuity Equation

For a fluid with constant density $\rho$, conservation of mass applied to an infinitesimal fluid element yields the incompressible continuity equation. In Cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

This equation states that the net volumetric flux into any control volume must equal zero ŌĆö fluid neither accumulates nor disappears within the element.

#### Step B: The Irrotationality Condition

The vorticity vector $\vec{\omega}$ is defined as:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

For irrotational flow, every component of this vector equals zero. Expanding the curl in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\vec{\omega} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

This produces three independent scalar equations (one for each component direction):

$$\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each of these equations states that the angular velocity of a fluid element about the corresponding axis is zero ŌĆö fluid particles translate without spinning.

#### Step C: Existence of the Velocity Potential

A fundamental theorem of vector calculus states: **a vector field whose curl vanishes everywhere in a simply connected region can be expressed as the gradient of a scalar potential.** Since our flow satisfies $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, such a scalar $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ must exist such that:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

In Cartesian components, this becomes:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$$

$$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

$$w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

#### Step D: Deriving Laplace's Equation

Now substitute the velocity potential expressions into the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

Rearranging the order of differentiation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

In compact vector notation:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

This is **Laplace's Equation** ŌĆö the governing PDE for the velocity potential in an incompressible, irrotational fluid flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The notation $\nabla^2$ is the **Laplacian operator**. It is not a vector but a scalar differential operator. Always write it without ambiguity as $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ or $\sum \partial^2 \phi / \partial x_i^2 = 0$.

### 3.2 Boundary Conditions on the Velocity Potential

For ocean wave problems, the velocity potential $\phi$ must satisfy **four essential boundary conditions** to fully determine the flow field. These are high-yield items for KTU Part B questions.

#### Boundary Condition 1: Kinematic Free Surface Condition

The free surface $z = \eta(x, t)$ is a material surface ŌĆö fluid particles on the surface remain on the surface. Linearised at $z = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

#### Boundary Condition 2: Dynamic Free Surface Condition

Combining Bernoulli's equation (linearised) with the kinematic condition produces:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

#### Boundary Condition 3: Seabed (Bottom) Condition

The seabed at $z = -h$ is impermeable, so no fluid penetrates it:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = -h$$

#### Boundary Condition 4: Lateral / Radiation Condition

For waves propagating away from a region (e.g., past an offshore structure):

$$\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} \left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \pm \frac{1}{c}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right) = 0$$

where $c$ is the wave celerity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Summary of the Derivation Chain

| Stage | Starting Equation | Mathematical Operation | Resulting Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Continuity | Definition for incompressible fluid | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ |
| 2 | Irrotationality | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | Curl-free condition |
| 3 | Vector Identity | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | Velocity potential exists |
| 4 | Substitution | $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ into continuity | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ |
| 5 | Solution | Apply boundary conditions | Unique $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram ŌĆö From Physical Assumptions to Governing Equation

The following Mermaid diagram captures the logical pipeline from the two fundamental physical assumptions (incompressibility and irrotationality) through to the final Laplace equation that governs the velocity potential.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START Physical Flow Setup"] --> nodeA["Assumption 1 INCOMPRESSIBILITY Fluid density rho constant"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Continuity Equation div of V equals zero"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Assumption 2 IRROTATIONALITY Vorticity omega equals zero"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Curl of V equals zero everywhere in flow"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Vector Identity Apply Stokes theorem"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Velocity V equals gradient of scalar phi"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Substitute V into continuity equation"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["LAPLACE EQUATION nabla squared phi equals zero"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Apply four boundary conditions"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["UNIQUE VELOCITY POTENTIAL phi xyz t"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Boundary Condition Coupling

This block diagram shows how the Laplace equation connects with the four boundary conditions that fully determine $\phi$ in ocean wave problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLap["Central PDE nabla squared phi equals zero"] --> nodeBC1["BC1 Kinematic Free Surface at z equals zero"]
    nodeLap --> nodeBC2["BC2 Dynamic Free Surface linearised Bernoulli"]
    nodeLap --> nodeBC3["BC3 Seabed at z equals minus h"]
    nodeLap --> nodeBC4["BC4 Radiation at infinity"]
    nodeBC1 --> nodeSol["Solved phi Field"]
    nodeBC2 --> nodeSol
    nodeBC3 --> nodeSol
    nodeBC4 --> nodeSol
    nodeSol --> nodeOutput["Output Velocity u v w and Pressure P"]
```

### 4.3 Mathematical Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM1["Continuity Equation"] --> nodeM4["Final Result nabla squared phi equals zero"]
    nodeM2["Irrotationality curl V equals zero"] --> nodeM3["Existence of phi via Stokes theorem"]
    nodeM3 --> nodeM4
    nodeM4 --> nodeM5["Bernoulli Coupling pressure velocity"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeM6["Boundary Condition System 4 BCs"]
    nodeM6 --> nodeM7["Solution Methods Separation of Variables Eigenfunction Expansion"]
    nodeM5 --> nodeM7
    nodeM7 --> nodeM8["Engineering Outputs Wave Forces Structure Loads"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define velocity potential and state the conditions under which it exists in a fluid flow field.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The velocity potential $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ is a scalar field whose gradient gives the velocity vector at every point in the flow. Mathematically:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

The velocity potential exists only when **two essential conditions** are satisfied:
1. The flow must be **irrotational**, i.e., $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, which implies zero vorticity everywhere.
2. The flow region must be **simply connected** (no holes or obstructions), ensuring the scalar potential is single-valued.

When these conditions hold and the fluid is incompressible ($\rho = $ constant), $\phi$ satisfies Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$, the governing PDE of linear wave theory. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
**State Laplace's equation for velocity potential and explain its significance in ocean wave hydrodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Laplace's equation for the velocity potential $\phi$ in an incompressible, irrotational fluid is:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **linear, homogeneous, second-order PDE**, allowing superposition of solutions.
- Combined with the linearised free-surface conditions, it forms the basis of **Airy (linear) wave theory**.
- It reduces the problem of finding a 3D vector velocity field to solving a single scalar equation, drastically simplifying analysis of ocean wave-induced motions and forces on offshore structures. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Style]**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Starting from the continuity equation and the irrotationality condition, derive the velocity potential equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ for an incompressible fluid flow.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Continuity Equation for Incompressible Fluid (1 mark):**
For a fluid with constant density, conservation of mass gives:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$
In vector notation: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Irrotationality Condition (1 mark):**
The vorticity vector is defined as $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$. For irrotational flow, $\vec{\omega} = 0$, so:
$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Existence of Scalar Potential (1 mark):**
Since the curl of $\vec{V}$ is zero everywhere, by the fundamental theorem of vector calculus, there exists a scalar potential $\phi$ such that:
$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Component Form (1 mark):**
$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitution into Continuity (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Laplace Equation (2 marks):**
$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is the **velocity potential equation** governing incompressible, irrotational fluid flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**For a 2D progressive wave propagating in the $x$-$z$ plane, write the Laplace equation and state all four boundary conditions required to determine the velocity potential uniquely. Clearly identify where each boundary condition is applied.**

**Model Solution:**

**Governing Equation (2 marks):**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Boundary Conditions (5 marks ŌĆö 1.25 marks each):**

| BC | Location | Equation | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| BC1 | Free surface $z = 0$ (kinematic) | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial t}$ | Particles on surface stay on surface |
| BC2 | Free surface $z = 0$ (dynamic) | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$ | Pressure equals atmospheric pressure |
| BC3 | Seabed $z = -h$ | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$ | No flow through impermeable bottom |
| BC4 | Radiation at $\vert x \vert \to \infty$ | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \pm \dfrac{1}{c}\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = 0$ | Waves propagate outward, no reflection from infinity |

These four boundary conditions, together with the Laplace equation, completely determine the velocity potential $\phi(x, z, t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**With the help of the Euler equation of motion, show that for an irrotational flow, the velocity components can be expressed as gradients of a scalar function $\phi$. Hence obtain the continuity equation in terms of $\phi$ for an incompressible fluid.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Euler's Equation (1 mark):**
For an inviscid, incompressible fluid under gravity, Euler's equation in vector form is:
$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla P - \rho g \hat{k}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expanding the Material Derivative (1 mark):**
$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right) = -\nabla P - \rho g \hat{k}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Using the Vector Identity (1 mark):**
The convective acceleration can be rewritten using the identity:
$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\frac{\vert\vec{V}\vert^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V} \times (\nabla \times \vec{V})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Irrotationality Simplification (1 mark):**
Since the flow is irrotational, $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, so:
$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\frac{\vert\vec{V}\vert^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Defining Velocity Potential (1 mark):**
For a curl-free field, $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$, which gives the velocity components:
$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substituting into Continuity (2 marks):**
For an incompressible fluid, $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, so:
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \phi) = \nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is the **Laplace equation** for the velocity potential. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Derive the dynamic free-surface boundary condition $\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$ from Bernoulli's equation and the kinematic free-surface condition.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Bernoulli's Equation for Unsteady Flow (1.5 marks):**
For an irrotational, incompressible flow under gravity:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \frac{P}{\rho} + gz + \frac{1}{2}\vert\vec{V}\vert^2 = f(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Pressure Condition at Free Surface (1 mark):**
At the free surface $z = \eta(x, t)$, the pressure equals atmospheric pressure $P_a$ (taken as zero gauge):
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + g\eta + \frac{1}{2}\vert\vec{V}\vert^2 = 0 \quad \text{at } z = \eta$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linearisation (1 mark):**
For small-amplitude waves, $\vert\vec{V}\vert^2$ is of second order (negligible). Also, $\eta$ is small, so we evaluate the linearised condition at $z = 0$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + g\eta = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$
This yields:
$$\eta = -\frac{1}{g}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Kinematic Free-Surface Condition (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiating Step 3 with Respect to Time (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{g}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Combining Both Equations (1.5 marks):**
Equating the two expressions for $\partial \eta / \partial t$:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = -\frac{1}{g}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2}$$

Rearranging gives the **dynamic free-surface boundary condition**:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

This equation is one of the four boundary conditions that uniquely determine the velocity potential in linear wave theory. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting assumptions** ŌĆö Failing to state incompressibility and irrotationality before deriving Laplace's equation (loses 1ŌĆō2 marks).
> 2. **Sign errors in Bernoulli** ŌĆö Mixing up the gravitational potential term $gz$ (must use $+gz$ with $+z$ pointing upward).
> 3. **Confusing kinematic vs dynamic BC** ŌĆö Many students swap the free-surface conditions or write them at $z = \eta$ instead of linearising at $z = 0$.
> 4. **Incomplete justification for $\phi$** ŌĆö Writing $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ without citing the curl-free condition explicitly.
> 5. **Missing the bottom BC** ŌĆö Always include $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$ at $z = -h$ for finite-depth ocean problems.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Velocity potential $\phi$** is a scalar function defined such that $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$, valid only for irrotational flows.
- **Two foundational assumptions:** incompressibility ($\rho = $ constant) and irrotationality ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$).
- **Governing PDE** for ocean wave problems: $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ (Laplace's equation) ŌĆö linear, homogeneous, second-order.
- **Continuity equation** for incompressible flow: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- **Irrotationality** means $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, equivalent to $\omega_x = \omega_y = \omega_z = 0$.
- **Bernoulli's equation** couples velocity potential to pressure: $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \dfrac{P}{\rho} + gz + \dfrac{1}{2}\vert\vec{V}\vert^2 = f(t)$.
- **Four essential boundary conditions:**
  1. Kinematic free surface at $z = 0$
  2. Dynamic free surface at $z = 0$
  3. Seabed (no-penetration) at $z = -h$
  4. Radiation / lateral boundary at $\vert x \vert \to \infty$
- **Stream function $\psi$** is a complementary scalar satisfying CauchyŌĆōRiemann relations with $\phi$.
- **Laplace's equation is linear** ŌĆö solutions can be superposed, enabling Fourier and eigenfunction methods.
- **Linearisation is essential** ŌĆö all products like $\vert\vec{V}\vert^2$ are dropped in small-amplitude (Airy) theory.
- **Standard seawater density** $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ are the default numerical values for KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispersion Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dispersion Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Dispersion Relationship** (also called the dispersion equation or frequency-wavelength relation) is the fundamental kinematic equation that governs the propagation of small-amplitude, linear (Airy) gravity waves on the surface of a fluid of finite depth. It mathematically couples the angular frequency **Žē** (rad/s) of a wave to its wavenumber **k** (rad/m), the gravitational acceleration **g** (Ōēł **9.81 m/s┬▓**), and the still water depth **h** (m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST751, Module 1):**
> "The dispersion relation defines the functional dependence of the angular frequency **Žē** on the wavenumber **k** for linear progressive surface gravity waves propagating over a horizontal bed of uniform depth."

The canonical form derived from linear potential wave theory is:

$$\omega^{2} = g\,k\,\tanh(kh)$$

This equation classifies surface gravity waves as **dispersive waves** ŌĆö different frequency components travel at different phase speeds, causing an initially localized wave packet to spread out as it propagates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a mixed crowd of runners of vastly different heights competing in a long-distance race. The tall runners have a long stride and a slower cadence (low frequency), while the short runners take quick, rapid steps (high frequency). Because the race track has a fixed length and a uniform surface, each runner's speed is strictly tied to their stride length ŌĆö that binding between **"stride length" (wavelength)** and **"speed" (phase velocity)** is exactly analogous to the dispersion relationship.

In ocean wave terms:

| Analogy Element | Wave Physics Equivalent |
|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Stride length | Wavelength **╬╗** = 2ŽĆ/k |
| Cadence | Angular frequency **Žē** |
| Track surface friction | Water depth **h** |
| Gravitational pull | Acceleration due to gravity **g** |
| Runner's speed | Phase velocity **c = Žē/k** |

A single, well-defined rule (the dispersion relation) decides how fast each "runner" (frequency component) is allowed to travel. Hence, a stormy wave group containing many frequencies gradually **disperses**, with the long-period swell outrunning the choppy short waves.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **g = 9.81 m/s┬▓** (acceleration due to gravity at mean sea level)
- **Žü = 1025 kg/m┬│** (density of seawater at standard ocean conditions)
- **Water depth regimes:**
  - **Deep water:** h > ╬╗/2 ŌåÆ tanh(kh) Ōēł 1
  - **Transitional:** 0.05 < h/╬╗ < 0.5
  - **Shallow water:** h < ╬╗/20 ŌåÆ tanh(kh) Ōēł kh

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio **kh** (wavenumber ├Ś depth) ŌĆö known as the **relative depth parameter** ŌĆö is the single most important dimensionless number that selects the asymptotic form of the dispersion relation. Always compute **kh = 2ŽĆh/╬╗** before applying any simplifying limit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework ŌĆö Linear Potential Wave Theory (Airy Theory)

The dispersion relation emerges from solving a **boundary value problem** governed by:

1. **Laplace's equation** (continuity for incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow)
2. **Kinematic free-surface boundary condition** (FSBC)
3. **Dynamic free-surface boundary condition** (DFSBC)
4. **Kinematic bottom boundary condition** (KBBC)

Each condition contributes a constraint that, when combined, yields the dispersion relation.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 ŌĆö Governing PDE (Laplace Equation):**
The velocity potential **Žå(x, z, t)** in an inviscid, incompressible, irrotational fluid satisfies Laplace's equation.

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bottom Boundary Condition:**
At the impermeable seabed **z = ŌłÆh**, no flow crosses the bottom, so the vertical velocity vanishes.

$$\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=-h} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linearized Kinematic Free-Surface Condition:**
At the mean water level **z = 0**, the surface elevation **╬Ę(x, t)** satisfies a kinematic relation. Linearizing around **z = 0** yields:

$$\frac{\partial\eta}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Linearized Dynamic Free-Surface Condition:**
The pressure at the free surface equals atmospheric pressure (gauge = 0). Linearizing Bernoulli's equation gives:

$$\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial t} + g\eta = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Combine FSBCs to Eliminate ╬Ę:**
Differentiating Step 4 with respect to **t** and substituting Step 3 gives the **combined free-surface condition**:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial t^{2}} + g\,\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Assume Progressive-Wave Form:**
Propose a separable solution propagating in +x direction:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = f(z)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Substitution into Laplace's equation yields an ODE in **z**, whose bottom-bounded solution is:

$$f(z) = A\,\cosh\bigl[k(z+h)\bigr]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Enforce Combined FSBC:**
Inserting the bounded velocity potential into Step 5 produces the dispersion relation:

$$\omega^{2} = g\,k\,\tanh(kh)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Notes / Limiting Form |
|---|----------|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Dispersion Relation (General)** | Žē┬▓ = gk tanh(kh) | Exact for all depths | Valid only for linear waves |
| 2 | Phase Velocity | c = Žē/k | c = ŌłÜ[(g/k) tanh(kh)] | m/s |
| 3 | Wavelength (iterative) | ╬╗ = (g/2ŽĆ) T┬▓ tanh(kh) | Implicit; iterate on ╬╗ | Used in Hindcasting |
| 4 | Group Velocity | cg = ŌłéŽē/Ōłék | cg = (c/2)[1 + 2kh/sinh(2kh)] | m/s |
| 5 | Group Velocity (deep) | cg,Ōł× = g/(2Žē) = c/2 | h ŌåÆ Ōł× | Energy travels at half phase speed |
| 6 | Group Velocity (shallow) | cg,shallow = c = ŌłÜ(gh) | kh ŌåÆ 0 | Non-dispersive limit |
| 7 | Deep-water limit | Žē┬▓ = gk | tanh(kh) ŌåÆ 1 | h > ╬╗/2 |
| 8 | Shallow-water limit | Žē┬▓ = gk┬▓h | tanh(kh) ŌåÆ kh | h < ╬╗/20 |
| 9 | Relative depth parameter | kh = 2ŽĆh/╬╗ | Dimensionless | Selects the regime |
| 10 | Wave celerity (shallow) | c = ŌłÜ(gh) | Independent of ╬╗ | Tsunami-like behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "derive or state the dispersion relation," always write the **general form first**, then quote **both asymptotic limits** (deep and shallow). Examiners reward students who mention the regime boundaries explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dispersion relation is the cornerstone of:

- **Oceanographic hindcasting & forecasting** (SWAN, WAM, Mike21 spectral models)
- **Coastal & offshore structural design** (jackets, FPSOs, wave energy converters)
- **Tsunami modeling** ŌĆö long waves satisfy the **shallow-water (non-dispersive)** form
- **Ship-motion and seakeeping analysis**
- **Radar altimeter and SAR ocean-wave retrieval** (inverting the dispersion relation in the presence of surface currents)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the General Dispersion Relation

### Step 1 ŌĆö Governing Equation
For an inviscid, incompressible, irrotational fluid, the velocity potential **Žå(x, z, t)** satisfies the 2-D Laplace equation in the fluid domain **(x, ŌłÆh Ōēż z Ōēż 0)**.

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = 0$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Bottom Boundary Condition (No Penetration)
The seabed is rigid and impermeable, so the vertical velocity vanishes at **z = ŌłÆh**.

$$\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=-h} = 0$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Assume a Separable Progressive Wave
Propose a solution that is periodic in **x** and **t**:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = f(z)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Why this form?** Linear surface gravity waves are sinusoidal in horizontal space and time; **k = 2ŽĆ/╬╗** and **Žē = 2ŽĆ/T** encode the spatial and temporal scales.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into Laplace
Compute second derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} = -k^{2}\,f(z)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = f''(z)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Adding:

$$\bigl[f''(z) - k^{2}f(z)\bigr]\,\sin(kx - \omega t) = 0$$

Cancel the sine term (non-trivial solution):

$$f''(z) - k^{2}f(z) = 0$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the ODE
The general solution is a linear combination of hyperbolic functions:

$$f(z) = A\,\cosh(kz) + B\,\sinh(kz)$$

Apply the bottom BC:

$$f'(-h) = A\,k\,\sinh(-kh) + B\,k\,\cosh(-kh) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow -A\,\sinh(kh) + B\,\cosh(kh) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = A\,\tanh(kh)$$

Substituting back:

$$f(z) = A\bigl[\cosh(kz) + \tanh(kh)\,\sinh(kz)\bigr]$$

Using the identity **cosh(a + b) = cosh(a) cosh(b) + sinh(a) sinh(b)**, this simplifies to:

$$f(z) = A\,\cosh\bigl[k(z+h)\bigr]$$

Thus the velocity potential becomes:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = A\,\cosh\bigl[k(z+h)\bigr]\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the Linearized Combined FSBC
At **z = 0**, the combined free-surface condition reads:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial t^{2}} + g\,\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = 0$$

Compute the partial derivatives at **z = 0**:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial t^{2}} = -\omega^{2}\,A\,\cosh(kh)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = A\,k\,\sinh(kh)\,\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

(Note the cosine arises because differentiation w.r.t. **z** flips the sine to cosine-hyperbolic sine pair.)

### Step 7 ŌĆö Enforce the Combined BC
Substitute both expressions:

$$-\omega^{2}\,A\,\cosh(kh)\,\sin(kx - \omega t) + g\,A\,k\,\sinh(kh)\,\cos(kx - \omega t) = 0$$

For this to hold for **all x and t**, both the sine and cosine coefficients must independently vanish ŌĆö but they cannot both be zero simultaneously unless the coefficients match in magnitude. Dividing the cosine coefficient by the sine coefficient and using the identity:

$$\frac{\sin(kx - \omega t)}{\cos(kx - \omega t)} = \tan(kx - \omega t)$$

Actually, the rigorous resolution is: rewrite the second term with a 90┬░ phase shift, or equivalently, the relation holds for **all** t only when the cosine and sine pieces are out of phase by ŽĆ/2. The compatibility condition gives:

$$\omega^{2}\,\cosh(kh) = g\,k\,\sinh(kh)$$

Divide both sides by **cosh(kh)**:

$$\boxed{\;\omega^{2} = g\,k\,\tanh(kh)\;}$$

This is the **general dispersion relation for linear progressive gravity waves**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Phase Velocity c(k, h)

By definition:

$$c = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

From the dispersion relation:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\,\tanh(kh)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Group Velocity cg

Group velocity is defined as:

$$c_{g} = \frac{\partial\omega}{\partial k}$$

From **Žē┬▓ = gk tanh(kh)**:

$$2\omega\,\frac{d\omega}{dk} = g\,\bigl[\tanh(kh) + kh\,\text{sech}^{2}(kh)\bigr]$$

$$\frac{d\omega}{dk} = \frac{g}{2\omega}\,\bigl[\tanh(kh) + kh\,\text{sech}^{2}(kh)\bigr]$$

Using **sech┬▓(kh) = 1/cosh┬▓(kh)** and the identity **2 tanh(kh) ŌłÆ tanh(2kh) = 2kh/cosh┬▓(2kh)**, the bracket simplifies elegantly to:

$$\tanh(kh) + kh\,\text{sech}^{2}(kh) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\tanh(kh) \cdot 2 + \text{residual}\bigr] \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c_{g} = \frac{c}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2kh}{\sinh(2kh)}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check (deep water, kh ŌåÆ Ōł×):** sinh(2kh) ŌåÆ Ōł×, so the bracket ŌåÆ 1, hence **cg = c/2**. This is the famous "deep-water swell travels at half the phase speed" result.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Limiting Forms

| Regime | Condition | tanh(kh) | Dispersion Relation | Phase Velocity c | Group Velocity cg |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Deep** | h/╬╗ > 0.5 (kh > ŽĆ) | 1 | Žē┬▓ = gk | ŌłÜ(g/k) | c/2 |
| **Transitional** | 0.05 < h/╬╗ < 0.5 | tanh(kh) | gk tanh(kh) | ŌłÜ[(g/k) tanh(kh)] | (c/2)[1 + 2kh/sinh(2kh)] |
| **Shallow** | h/╬╗ < 0.05 (kh < ŽĆ/10) | kh | Žē┬▓ = gk┬▓h | ŌłÜ(gh) | ŌłÜ(gh) = c |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A wave with period **T = 8 s** propagates over a depth **h = 20 m**. Compute wavelength, phase velocity, and group velocity.

**Solution:**

Iteration 1 (deep-water guess): **╬╗ŌéĆ = (g T┬▓)/(2ŽĆ) = 9.81 ├Ś 64 / 6.2832 Ōēł 100 m**

Relative depth: **kh = 2ŽĆ ├Ś 20 / 100 = 1.257**, so **tanh(1.257) = 0.852**.

Iteration 2: **╬╗Ōéü = 100 ├Ś 0.852 Ōēł 85.2 m** ŌåÆ recompute **kh = 2ŽĆ ├Ś 20 / 85.2 = 1.475** ŌåÆ **tanh(1.475) = 0.901**

Iteration 3: **╬╗Ōéé = 100 ├Ś 0.901 Ōēł 90.1 m** ŌåÆ **kh = 1.394** ŌåÆ **tanh(1.394) = 0.883**

Iteration 4: **╬╗Ōéā Ōēł 88.3 m** ŌåÆ **kh Ōēł 1.422** ŌåÆ **tanh Ōēł 0.889** ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéä Ōēł 88.9 m** (converged)

Final: **╬╗ Ōēł 89 m**, **c = ╬╗/T Ōēł 11.1 m/s**, **cg = (c/2)[1 + 2(1.42)/sinh(2.84)] Ōēł (11.1/2)[1 + 2.84/8.49] Ōēł 7.41 m/s**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Derivation Flow of the Dispersion Relation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incompressible, Inviscid, Irrotational Flow] --> B[Define Velocity Potential phi x,z,t]
    B --> C[Laplace Equation: del squared phi = 0]
    C --> D[Assume Progressive Wave: phi = f of z times sin kx minus omega t]
    D --> E[Substitute into Laplace yields ODE f double prime minus k squared f = 0]
    E --> F[Solve ODE: f of z = A cosh k z plus B sinh k z]
    F --> G[Apply Bottom BC: df/dz at z equals minus h is zero]
    G --> H[Vertical structure: f of z = A cosh k times z plus h]
    H --> I[Linearized Combined FSBC at z equals 0]
    I --> J[Eliminate surface eta: d2 phi/dt2 plus g dphi/dz equals 0]
    J --> K[Substitute bounded phi solution]
    K --> L[Match sine and cosine coefficients]
    L --> M[omega squared equals g k tanh k h]
    M --> N[Phase Velocity c equals omega over k]
    M --> O[Group Velocity cg equals d omega over d k]
    N --> P[Deep Limit: omega squared equals g k]
    N --> Q[Shallow Limit: omega squared equals g k squared h]
    O --> R[Deep cg equals c over 2]
    O --> S[Shallow cg equals c equals sqrt g h]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Physical Significance & Energy Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wave Packet at Origin x = 0] --> P2[Contains Range of Frequencies]
    P2 --> P3[Dispersion Relation Selects Phase Speed per Frequency]
    P3 --> P4[Long Waves Travel Faster in Deep Water]
    P3 --> P5[Short Waves Travel Slower in Deep Water]
    P4 --> P6[Wave Packet Spreads Energy at Group Velocity cg]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[cg = c/2 in Deep Water]
    P6 --> P8[cg = c in Shallow Water]
    P7 --> P9[Swell Dominance: Distant Storms Produce Long Period Waves]
    P8 --> P10[Tsunami Behavior: Long Waves Travel as Shallow Water Waves]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Depth Regimes & Engineering Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Given Wave Period T and Depth h] --> R2[Compute kh equals 2 pi h over lambda]
    R2 --> S1{kh greater than pi}
    S1 -- Yes --> D1[Deep Water Regime]
    S1 -- No --> S2{kh less than pi over 10}
    S2 -- Yes --> D2[Shallow Water Regime]
    S2 -- No --> D3[Transitional Regime]
    D1 --> U1[Use omega squared equals g k]
    D2 --> U2[Use omega squared equals g k squared h]
    D3 --> U3[Use omega squared equals g k tanh k h]
    U1 --> A1[Cg equals c over 2]
    U2 --> A2[Cg equals c]
    U3 --> A3[Iterative Solution Required]
    A1 --> E1[Offshore Structure Design]
    A2 --> E2[Coastal and Port Engineering]
    A3 --> E3[Spectral Wave Modeling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the dispersion relation for linear gravity waves in water of finite depth. Why is it called a "dispersion" relation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dispersion relation for linear (Airy) progressive gravity waves in water of uniform finite depth **h** is:

$$\omega^{2} = g\,k\,\tanh(kh)$$

where **Žē** is the angular frequency, **k** the wavenumber, and **g = 9.81 m/s┬▓**.

It is called a "dispersion" relation because the phase velocity **c = Žē/k = ŌłÜ[(g/k) tanh(kh)]** depends on **k** (or wavelength). Consequently, each frequency component travels at a different speed, and a wave packet containing a range of frequencies gradually spreads out ŌĆö i.e., it **disperses** ŌĆö as it propagates.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between deep-water and shallow-water forms of the dispersion relation. State the regime criteria.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Deep Water | Shallow Water |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| Criterion | h > ╬╗/2 (or h/╬╗ > 0.5) | h < ╬╗/20 (or h/╬╗ < 0.05) |
| tanh(kh) | ŌåÆ 1 | ŌåÆ kh |
| Dispersion Relation | Žē┬▓ = gk | Žē┬▓ = gk┬▓h |
| Phase Velocity | c = ŌłÜ(g/k) | c = ŌłÜ(gh) |
| Group Velocity | cg = c/2 | cg = c |
| Nature | Dispersive | Non-dispersive |

Between these limits lies the **transitional regime**, where the full hyperbolic-tangent form must be retained and solutions are typically obtained by iteration on wavelength.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Starting from the Laplace equation and the appropriate boundary conditions, **derive the dispersion relation** for linear progressive gravity waves in water of finite depth **h**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wave has period **T = 10 s** and propagates over water of depth **h = 15 m**. **Determine the wavelength, phase velocity, and group velocity** using the finite-depth dispersion relation. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Governing PDE:**
For incompressible, irrotational flow:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial z^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bottom BC at z = ŌłÆh:**

$$\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=-h} = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combined Free-Surface BC at z = 0:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial t^{2}} + g\,\frac{\partial\phi}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assume Progressive-Wave Solution:**

$$\phi(x, z, t) = f(z)\,\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Vertical Structure (ODE):**
Substitution yields **fŌĆ│ ŌłÆ k┬▓f = 0**, whose bounded solution is **f(z) = A cosh[k(z+h)]**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Combined FSBC:**

$$\omega^{2}\,\cosh(kh) = g\,k\,\sinh(kh)$$

$$\boxed{\;\omega^{2} = g\,k\,\tanh(kh)\;}$$

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A (b)

**Iteration:** Use **╬╗ŌéĆ = (g T┬▓)/(2ŽĆ) = (9.81 ├Ś 100)/6.2832 Ōēł 156.1 m**.

- Iteration 1: kh = 2ŽĆ(15)/156.1 = 0.604, tanh(0.604) = 0.539 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéü = 156.1 ├Ś 0.539 Ōēł 84.1 m**
- Iteration 2: kh = 2ŽĆ(15)/84.1 = 1.121, tanh(1.121) = 0.808 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéé = 156.1 ├Ś 0.808 Ōēł 126.1 m**
- Iteration 3: kh = 2ŽĆ(15)/126.1 = 0.747, tanh(0.747) = 0.635 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéā Ōēł 99.1 m**
- Iteration 4: kh = 0.951, tanh = 0.740 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéä Ōēł 115.5 m**
- Iteration 5: kh = 0.816, tanh = 0.672 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéģ Ōēł 104.9 m**
- Iteration 6: kh = 0.898, tanh = 0.716 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéå Ōēł 111.8 m** (converging)
- Iteration 7: kh = 0.843, tanh = 0.687 ŌåÆ **╬╗Ōéć Ōēł 107.2 m** Ō£ō

**Final Results (accept ╬╗ Ōēł 108 m):**

- Wavelength **╬╗ Ōēł 108 m**
- Phase velocity **c = ╬╗/T Ōēł 108/10 = 10.8 m/s**
- Group velocity **cg = (c/2)[1 + 2kh/sinh(2kh)]**
  - kh Ōēł 0.873 ŌåÆ sinh(1.746) Ōēł 2.751
  - **cg = 5.4 ├Ś [1 + 1.746/2.751] = 5.4 ├Ś 1.634 Ōēł 8.83 m/s**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** **Derive expressions for the phase velocity and group velocity** of linear gravity waves from the general dispersion relation. Discuss their behavior in the deep-water and shallow-water limits. (7 marks)

**(b)** A tsunami wave is modeled as a shallow-water gravity wave with period **T = 1800 s** in the deep ocean (**h = 4000 m**). Calculate its wavelength, phase velocity, and the time taken to travel **1500 km** from the generation zone to the coast. State the relevant dispersion-relation limit used. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B (a)

**Phase Velocity:**

$$c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\,\tanh(kh)}$$

**Group Velocity:**

$$c_{g} = \frac{\partial\omega}{\partial k} = \frac{c}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2kh}{\sinh(2kh)}\right]$$

**Deep-water limit (kh ŌåÆ Ōł×):**
- tanh(kh) ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ **c = ŌłÜ(g/k) = g/Žē**
- sinh(2kh) ŌåÆ Ōł× ŌåÆ bracket ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ **cg = c/2**

**Shallow-water limit (kh ŌåÆ 0):**
- tanh(kh) ŌåÆ kh ŌåÆ **c = ŌłÜ(gh)**
- sinh(2kh) Ōēł 2kh ŌåÆ bracket ŌåÆ [1 + 1] = 2 ŌåÆ **cg = (c/2)(2) = c = ŌłÜ(gh)**

In shallow water, both phase and group velocities are **independent of wavelength**, so waves are **non-dispersive**. In deep water, long waves travel faster than short waves, causing the wave packet to spread ŌĆö the system is **dispersive**.

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B (b)

**Check regime:** For T = 1800 s, deep-water wavelength estimate ╬╗ŌéĆ = gT┬▓/(2ŽĆ) = 9.81 ├Ś (1800)┬▓/6.2832 Ōēł **5.05 ├Ś 10ŌüČ m = 5050 km**. Since **h = 4 km Ōē¬ ╬╗/2**, the shallow-water limit applies.

**Shallow-water dispersion limit:**

$$\omega^{2} = g\,k^{2}\,h \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = \sqrt{g\,h} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 4000} \approx \sqrt{39240} \approx 198 \text{ m/s}$$

**Phase velocity c Ōēł 198 m/s**

**Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = c\,T = 198 \times 1800 \approx 356{,}400 \text{ m} \approx 356 \text{ km}$$

**Travel time for 1500 km:**

$$t = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{198} \approx 7575 \text{ s} \approx 2.10 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Tsunamis are extremely fast (~720 km/h) in the open ocean, yet their amplitude is tiny (< 1 m) due to the enormous wavelength. They only become destructive as they enter shallow coastal waters, where the wavelength compresses and the amplitude grows.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the hyperbolic-tangent form.** Writing only Žē┬▓ = gk without qualifying the depth regime is marked as **incomplete** (lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Sign errors in BCs.** The bottom BC is **ŌłéŽå/Ōłéz = 0 at z = ŌłÆh**, NOT at z = 0. Mixing up the sign of the depth leads to a wrong vertical structure function.
> 3. **Confusing phase and group velocities.** Phase velocity **c** is the speed of a single wave crest; group velocity **cg** is the speed of the wave packet envelope (i.e., energy). KTU expects this distinction explicitly stated.
> 4. **Skipping the combined FSBC step.** Examiners look for the elimination of **╬Ę** between the kinematic and dynamic FSBCs to form the **combined condition**. Omitting this intermediate step costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 5. **No iteration check.** When asked to compute wavelength for transitional depth, simply substituting a deep-water estimate once and stopping is treated as a non-rigorous solution (lose 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **dispersion relation** is **Žē┬▓ = gk tanh(kh)** ŌĆö the central kinematic equation of linear water-wave theory.
- It emerges from solving **Laplace's equation** subject to: **(i)** bottom BC, **(ii)** combined linearized FSBC.
- Three depth regimes govern the simplification: **deep (tanh kh Ōēł 1)**, **shallow (tanh kh Ōēł kh)**, **transitional (full form + iteration)**.
- **Deep-water limits:** Žē┬▓ = gk, c = ŌłÜ(g/k), cg = c/2.
- **Shallow-water limits:** Žē┬▓ = gk┬▓h, c = ŌłÜ(gh), cg = c (non-dispersive).
- **Phase velocity** = speed of individual wave crest; **Group velocity** = speed of wave energy/packet envelope.
- The **relative depth parameter kh = 2ŽĆh/╬╗** decides the regime ŌĆö always compute it before applying any limiting form.
- **Engineering utilities:** wave hindcasting, spectral modeling (SWAN/WAM), offshore structural design, tsunami prediction, and SAR ocean-wave retrieval.
- **Dispersive** systems spread wave packets; **non-dispersive** (shallow-water) systems preserve wave shape.
- A common **KTU examiner trick** is to give a problem at a depth that is neither deep nor shallow, forcing students to either iterate or argue the regime carefully ŌĆö practice both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Celerity in Different Water Depth Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Celerity in Different Water Depth Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Celerity (Phase Velocity) in Ocean Wave Mechanics**
> In the context of linear wave theory (Airy wave theory), **celerity (C)** is defined as the *phase velocity* ŌĆö the speed at which a specific phase of a wave (e.g., the crest, trough, or any fixed point on the waveform) propagates across the water surface. Unlike group velocity, celerity is the speed of an individual wave form, not the energy packet.

Mathematically, celerity is the ratio of one wavelength to one wave period:

$$C = \frac{L}{T}$$

Where:
- **C** = Phase velocity or celerity (m/s)
- **L** = Wavelength (m)
- **T** = Wave period (s)

The general expression for celerity in water of *arbitrary* depth is obtained from the linear dispersion relationship of Airy wave theory:

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{gL}{2\pi}\tanh\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)}$$

Where:
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s┬▓**
- **d** = Still water depth (m)
- **L** = Wavelength (m)
- **T** = Wave period (s)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Conveyor Belt vs. Marching Soldiers Analogy**
> Imagine a line of soldiers marching in formation. The "wave" is the rhythmic up-and-down motion of each soldier's hand as they march. The *celerity* is the speed at which a single arm-raise pattern travels down the line. The water particles themselves only move in small orbital paths ŌĆö they do not travel horizontally with the wave. The wave **shape** moves; the **water** mostly stays in place (except in breaking waves or very shallow water where there is mass transport).
>
> Now, the *speed* of that pattern depends on whether the soldiers are marching on a **narrow plank** (deep water ŌĆö each soldier's motion is unaffected by the plank's edges), a **wide stadium track** (shallow water ŌĆö the boundary effects of the ground slow the motion), or somewhere in between. This is exactly how water depth changes wave celerity.

## 1.3 Why Celerity Depends on Depth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In deep water, water particles move in circular orbits and the seabed is too far below to influence the surface wave. In shallow water, the orbits become flattened ellipses, and the seabed "drags" on the wave, slowing it down. The ratio **d/L** (relative depth) determines which regime dominates.

## 1.4 Classification of Water Depth Regimes

| Regime | Relative Depth (d/L) | Hyperbolic Function Approximation | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deep Water** | $d/L \geq 0.5$ | $\tanh(kd) \approx 1$ | Open ocean, offshore structures |
| **Transitional (Intermediate) Water** | $0.05 < d/L < 0.5$ | $\tanh(kd) \approx \tanh(kd)$ (full form required) | Nearshore zones, harbors |
| **Shallow Water** | $d/L \leq 0.05$ | $\tanh(kd) \approx kd$ | Surf zone, coastal engineering |

Where **k = 2ŽĆ/L** is the wave number.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dispersion Relation Foundation

The linear dispersion relation in Airy (linear) wave theory is:

$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$$

Where:
- **Žē** = Angular frequency = $2\pi/T$ (rad/s)
- **k** = Wave number = $2\pi/L$ (rad/m)

Phase velocity emerges as:

$$C = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh(kd)$$

This is the **master equation**. All other formulas in the three depth regimes are simplifications of this single relationship.

## 2.2 Case 1: Deep Water Celerity ($d/L \geq 0.5$)

When $kd \geq \pi$ (i.e., $d \geq L/2$):
- $\tanh(kd) \approx 1$
- The seabed has negligible effect on the surface wave motion.
- Water particle orbits are perfect circles with diameter that decays exponentially with depth.

The deep-water celerity becomes:

$$C_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{gL_0}{2\pi}$$

Where $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$ is the deep-water wavelength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In deep water, celerity is governed *purely* by the wave period T. This is a critical engineering result ŌĆö once you measure the period of a swell, you can immediately compute its deep-water speed.

## 2.3 Case 2: Shallow Water Celerity ($d/L \leq 0.05$)

When $kd \leq 0.314$ (i.e., $d \leq L/20$):
- $\tanh(kd) \approx kd$
- The wave "feels" the bottom strongly; orbits are flat horizontal ellipses.

The shallow-water celerity becomes:

$$C = \sqrt{gd}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In shallow water, celerity depends *only* on water depth! It is independent of wavelength and period. This is why tsunami waves slow down as they approach the coast but their height grows enormously ŌĆö the energy flux must be conserved, and as speed decreases, amplitude must increase.

## 2.4 Case 3: Transitional Water Celerity (General Form)

For $0.05 < d/L < 0.5$, no simplification is valid. The full dispersion relation must be used:

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$$

Since C depends on L (and L depends on C, since $L = C \cdot T$), this is an **implicit equation**. It must be solved iteratively using:

1. Initial guess: $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$ (deep water)
2. Update: $L_{n+1} = (gT^2/2\pi)\tanh(2\pi d/L_n)$
3. Repeat until convergence.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of Celerity Knowledge**
> 1. **Tsunami warning systems** ŌĆö Travel time of tsunami = distance / C, where C = ŌłÜ(gd) in shallow water.
