> 2. **Offshore platform design** ŌĆö Deep-water oil rigs use $C_0 = gT/2\pi$ for design wave loading.
> 3. **Harbor resonance & seiche analysis** ŌĆö Shallow-water celerity governs the natural oscillation periods of harbors.
> 4. **Wave energy converters** ŌĆö Power flux depends on group velocity, which itself depends on phase velocity.
> 5. **Ship navigation in shallow channels** ŌĆö Squat and speed-loss calculations use the shallow-water dispersion relation.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Deep Water ($d/L \geq 0.5$) | Transitional Water | Shallow Water ($d/L \leq 0.05$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase Celerity C | $C_0 = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ | $C = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$ | $C = \sqrt{gd}$ |
| 2 | Wavelength L | $L_0 = \dfrac{gT^2}{2\pi} \approx 1.56\,T^2$ | Implicit (iterative) | $L = T\sqrt{gd}$ |
| 3 | Group Velocity $C_g$ | $C_0/2$ | $\dfrac{C}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2kd}{\sinh(2kd)}\right)$ | $C$ |
| 4 | $\tanh(kd)$ value | $\approx 1$ | exact value needed | $\approx kd$ |
| 5 | Energy Distribution | Surface-trapped | Mixed | Bottom-influenced |
| 6 | Water Particle Orbit | Circles | Truncated circles | Flat ellipses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Quick-Reference Constants**
> - $g/(2\pi) \approx 1.562$ ŌåÆ so $L_0 \approx 1.56\,T^2$ (deep water)
> - $\tanh(1) \approx 0.7616$ ŌåÆ boundary between regimes is *not* a hard cutoff but asymptotic
> - $C_0$ at T = 10 s: $C_0 = 9.81 \times 10 / (2\pi) \approx 15.6$ m/s

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Celerity from Linear Wave Theory

We start from the linear dispersion relation obtained by applying the no-penetration boundary condition at the seabed ($z = -d$) and the linearized dynamic and kinematic free-surface conditions in potential flow theory:

$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$$

**Step 1:** Take the square root on both sides to isolate angular frequency:

$$\omega = \sqrt{gk\tanh(kd)}$$

**Step 2:** Use the definition of phase velocity: $C = \omega/k$. Divide both sides by k:

$$C = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{\sqrt{gk\tanh(kd)}}{k}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the expression:

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{gk\tanh(kd)}{k^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{g\tanh(kd)}{k}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $k = 2\pi/L$:

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{gL}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Alternate form using period ŌĆö since $L = CT$, and the dispersion relation gives $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$, we can equivalently express:

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$$

Both forms are **identical** and must yield the same numerical answer.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deep-Water Celerity Limit

**Step 1:** Starting from the general celerity:

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$$

**Step 2:** For deep water, $d \geq L/2$:

$$\frac{2\pi d}{L} \geq \frac{2\pi (L/2)}{L} = \pi$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the limit:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty}\tanh(x) = 1$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$C_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\cdot 1 = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$$

**Step 5:** Using the deep-water dispersion (since $L_0 = gT^2/2\pi$):

$$C_0 = \frac{g}{2\pi}\cdot T = \frac{g}{2\pi}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2\pi L_0}{g}} = \sqrt{\frac{gL_0}{2\pi}}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{1}} = \sqrt{\frac{gL_0}{2\pi}}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical check at T = 8 s:

$$C_0 = \frac{9.81 \times 8}{2\pi} = \frac{78.48}{6.2832} \approx 12.49\ \text{m/s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shallow-Water Celerity Limit

**Step 1:** Starting from the general celerity:

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$$

**Step 2:** For shallow water, $kd \to 0$. Use the small-angle Taylor expansion of $\tanh$:

$$\tanh(x) \approx x \quad \text{for}\ x \ll 1$$

**Step 3:** Apply the approximation with $x = 2\pi d/L$:

$$\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right) \approx \frac{2\pi d}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the general celerity:

$$C \approx \frac{gT}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{2\pi d}{L} = \frac{gTd}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Since shallow water $L = T\sqrt{gd}$ (a consequence of the same approximation), substitute:

$$C = \frac{gTd}{T\sqrt{gd}} = \frac{gd}{\sqrt{gd}} = \sqrt{gd}$$

**Step 6:** Final form (independent of T and L):

$$C = \sqrt{gd}$$

**Step 7:** Numerical check at d = 5 m:

$$C = \sqrt{9.81 \times 5} = \sqrt{49.05} \approx 7.00\ \text{m/s}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Across Regimes

| Property | Deep Water | Transitional | Shallow Water |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d/L$ threshold | $\geq 1/2$ | $1/20 < d/L < 1/2$ | $\leq 1/20$ |
| $\tanh(kd)$ approximation | $\approx 1$ | exact value | $\approx kd$ |
| Celerity depends on | Period T (and g) | T, d, L (all coupled) | Depth d (and g) only |
| $C_g / C$ ratio | 0.5 | $0.5 \leq C_g/C \leq 1$ | 1.0 |
| Orbital shape | Perfect circle | Truncated circle ŌåÆ ellipse | Flat horizontal ellipse |
| Orbital decay with depth | $e^{-kz}$ (fast) | slower decay | Uniform with depth |
| Practical example | Open ocean swell | Continental shelf | Surf zone, tsunami run-up |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram ŌĆö Celerity Across Depth Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Wave Period T, Depth d] --> B{Compute d/L or kd}
    B -->|d/L greater than or equal 0.5| C[Deep Water Regime]
    B -->|0.05 less than d/L less than 0.5| D[Transitional Water Regime]
    B -->|d/L less than or equal 0.05| E[Shallow Water Regime]
    C --> F[Apply CO = gT / 2pi]
    D --> G[Iterative Solution Required]
    E --> H[Apply C = sqrt gd]
    F --> I[Output: Phase Celerity C]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Engineering Application: Design Loading, Travel Time, Resonance]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Celerity Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        T[Wave Period T seconds]
        D[Water Depth d meters]
        G[Acceleration g = 9.81 m/s squared]
    end

    subgraph DEEPCALC[Deep Water Block]
        LZ[LO = g times T squared / 2pi]
        CZ[CO = g times T / 2pi]
    end

    subgraph TRANSITIONAL[Transitional Iterative Solver]
        INIT[Initialize L = LO]
        UPDT[Update Ln plus 1 using tanh]
        CHECK{Convergence Check}
        CGET[C = gT / 2pi times tanh 2pi d over L]
    end

    subgraph SHALLOWCALC[Shallow Water Block]
        CSQ[C = sqrt g times d]
        LSQ[L = T times sqrt g times d]
    end

    subgraph ROUTER[Regime Router]
        DECIDE{Compare d over L}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Final Output]
        CEL[Phase Celerity C m/s]
        WVL[Wavelength L m]
    end

    T --> LZ --> CZ
    T --> INIT --> UPDT --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|Not Converged| UPDT
    CHECK -->|Converged| CGET
    T --> CSQ
    T --> LSQ
    D --> DECIDE
    T --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -->|Deep| CZ
    DECIDE -->|Transitional| CGET
    DECIDE -->|Shallow| CSQ
    CZ --> CEL
    CGET --> CEL
    CSQ --> CEL
    LZ --> WVL
    INIT --> WVL
    LSQ --> WVL
```

## 4.3 Regime Boundary Visualization (Mermaid Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Water Surface Wave] --> SHAPE{Orbital Motion Shape}
    SHAPE -->|Perfect Circle| DEEP[Deep Water]
    SHAPE -->|Truncated Circle| TRANS[Transitional]
    SHAPE -->|Flat Ellipse| SHALLOW[Shallow Water]
    DEEP --> DNOTE[C = gT / 2pi - depends on period only]
    TRANS --> TNOTE[C = gT/2pi tanh 2pi d/L - all four parameters]
    SHALLOW --> SNOTE[C = sqrt gd - depends on depth only]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "celerity" in the context of ocean wave hydrodynamics. State the general expression for celerity in water of arbitrary depth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Celerity** is the *phase velocity* of a progressive gravity wave ŌĆö that is, the speed at which a particular phase (e.g., crest or trough) of the wave travels across the water surface. It is given by C = L/T.

> The general expression for celerity in water of arbitrary depth, derived from linear (Airy) wave theory, is:

> $$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{gL}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right)}$$

**(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: formula C = L/T; 1 mark: general expression with tanh)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**Q: Compare deep-water and shallow-water celerity. State the conditions under which each formula is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Parameter | Deep Water | Shallow Water |
> |---|---|---|
> | Formula | $C_0 = gT/(2\pi)$ | $C = \sqrt{gd}$ |
> | Applicable when | $d/L \geq 0.5$ (or $d \geq L/2$) | $d/L \leq 0.05$ (or $d \leq L/20$) |
> | Celerity depends on | Wave period T | Water depth d |
> | Physical regime | Open ocean | Surf zone, harbors |

**(1 mark: deep water formula + condition; 1 mark: shallow water formula + condition; 1 mark: distinction in dependence on T vs d)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper]

**Q (a) Derive the expression for the celerity of a linear progressive gravity wave in deep water. State clearly the assumptions used in the derivation. (7 marks)**

**Q (b) A wave with a period of 10 s is propagating in water depth of 50 m. Calculate the wavelength, phase celerity, and check whether the wave is in deep, transitional, or shallow water regime. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Derivation of Deep-Water Celerity**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Inviscid, incompressible fluid
2. Irrotational flow
3. Small amplitude (linear) wave theory ŌĆö A/H Ōē¬ 1
4. Constant water depth
5. Neglect of surface tension (gravity waves)

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The linear dispersion relation from Airy wave theory is:

$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$$

For **deep water**, $d \to \infty$, so $kd \to \infty$ and $\tanh(kd) \to 1$:

$$\omega^2 = gk$$

The phase celerity is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

From the dispersion relation:

$$\omega = \sqrt{gk}$$

Therefore:

$$C_0 = \frac{\sqrt{gk}}{k} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{gL}{2\pi}}$$

Substituting $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$:

$$C_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$$

**Final Answer (deep water):**

$$C_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \sqrt{\frac{gL_0}{2\pi}}$$

**Part (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:** T = 10 s, d = 50 m, g = 9.81 m/s┬▓

**Step 1: Compute deep-water wavelength as initial guess (1 mark):**

$$L_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 100}{6.2832} = \frac{981}{6.2832} \approx 156.13\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Check regime (1 mark):**

$$\frac{d}{L_0} = \frac{50}{156.13} \approx 0.32$$

Since $0.05 < 0.32 < 0.5$, the wave is in the **transitional water regime**. Hence the full dispersion relation must be used iteratively.

**Step 3: Iterative solution (3 marks):**

Use $L_{n+1} = L_0 \cdot \tanh(2\pi d / L_n)$:

- Iteration 1: $L_1 = 156.13 \cdot \tanh(2\pi \cdot 50/156.13) = 156.13 \cdot \tanh(2.012) = 156.13 \cdot 0.9637 = 150.46$ m
- Iteration 2: $L_2 = 156.13 \cdot \tanh(2\pi \cdot 50/150.46) = 156.13 \cdot \tanh(2.088) = 156.13 \cdot 0.9688 = 151.27$ m
- Iteration 3: $L_3 = 156.13 \cdot \tanh(2.079) = 156.13 \cdot 0.9681 = 151.14$ m
- Iteration 4: $L_4 \approx 151.17$ m (converged)

So **L Ōēł 151.17 m**.

**Step 4: Compute phase celerity (1 mark):**

$$C = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{151.17}{10} = 15.12\ \text{m/s}$$

**Verification using alternate form:**

$$C = \frac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{L}\right) = 15.61 \times 0.9681 = 15.11\ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Final classification (1 mark):**

Since $d/L = 50/151.17 = 0.331$ (which is in the range 0.05 to 0.5), the wave is confirmed to be in the **transitional water regime**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper]

**Q (a) Starting from the general dispersion relation, derive the expression for celerity in shallow water. Explain why tsunami waves slow down as they approach the coast. (7 marks)**

**Q (b) A tsunami wave with period T = 30 minutes is generated in the deep ocean (d = 4000 m) and propagates toward a coastline with shallow water depth d = 10 m. Compute (i) the deep-water celerity and deep-water wavelength, and (ii) the shallow-water celerity as the wave reaches the coast. Comment on the speed reduction. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Shallow-Water Celerity Derivation**

**Starting point (1 mark):**

$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd) \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Shallow-water assumption (1 mark):** $kd \ll 1$, therefore $\tanh(kd) \approx kd$.

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$\omega^2 = gk \cdot (kd) = gk^2 d$$

$$\omega = k\sqrt{gd}$$

**Dividing by k (2 marks):**

$$C = \frac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{gd}$$

**Physical explanation of tsunami slowing (1 mark):**

As a tsunami moves from the deep ocean (d Ōēł 4000 m) to the continental shelf and into shallow coastal water (d Ōēł 10 m), the celerity drops dramatically because $C = \sqrt{gd}$. With d decreasing by a factor of 400, the celerity decreases by a factor of ŌłÜ400 = 20. Energy conservation then forces the wave amplitude to grow (the wave "piles up" and steepens), which is why tsunamis become so destructive near the coast.

**Part (b): Tsunami Celerity Computation**

**Given:** T = 30 min = 1800 s, $d_{deep}$ = 4000 m, $d_{shallow}$ = 10 m, g = 9.81 m/s┬▓

**(i) Deep-water parameters (3 marks):**

Deep-water celerity:

$$C_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 1800}{6.2832} = \frac{17658}{6.2832} \approx 2810.5\ \text{m/s}$$

Converting: $C_0 \approx 2.81\ \text{km/s} \approx 712\ \text{km/h}$ (jetliner speed!)

Deep-water wavelength:

$$L_0 = C_0 \cdot T = 2810.5 \times 1800 = 5{,}058{,}900\ \text{m} \approx 5059\ \text{km}$$

**Verify regime (1 mark):** $d/L_0 = 4000/5{,}058{,}900 \approx 7.9 \times 10^{-4} \ll 0.05$ ŌåÆ confirmed deep water.

**(ii) Shallow-water celerity at coast (3 marks):**

$$C_{shallow} = \sqrt{gd} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 10} = \sqrt{98.1} \approx 9.90\ \text{m/s}$$

Converting: $C_{shallow} \approx 35.6\ \text{km/h}$ (running speed!)

**Comment on speed reduction (1 mark):**

Speed reduction factor:

$$\frac{C_0}{C_{shallow}} = \frac{2810.5}{9.90} \approx 284$$

The tsunami slows by a factor of nearly **284** as it crosses from the abyssal ocean to the coast. This dramatic slowing, combined with the conservation of energy flux, causes wave shoaling: the wave amplitude grows, the wavelength compresses, and the wave steepens into a destructive bore. Coastal communities only have a few tens of minutes of warning.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the regime** before applying a formula is the #1 deduction source. Students often blindly use $C_0 = gT/(2\pi)$ in shallow water or $C = \sqrt{gd}$ in deep water, losing 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** in problems involving tsunami-scale periods (minutes ŌåÆ seconds) cause cascading numerical errors. Always convert T to seconds *first*.
> 3. **For the iterative solution**, students often write one or two iterations and claim convergence. Show at least 3ŌĆō4 iterations and explicitly state the convergence tolerance (e.g., $<\ 0.01$ m).
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions** of linear (Airy) wave theory in derivation questions typically costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing group velocity with phase velocity** ŌĆö in shallow water, $C_g = C$ (in deep water, $C_g = C_0/2$). Examiners love testing this.
> 6. **Mistaking "wavelength" symbol** ŌĆö KTU uses **L** (capital), not ╬╗. Using the wrong symbol on exam papers may still be accepted, but consistent KTU convention earns goodwill.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Celerity in Different Water Depth Conditions**

- [x] **Celerity = Phase velocity = L/T** (m/s)
- [x] **General formula** (works in ALL depths): $C = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi d}{L}\right)$
- [x] **Deep water condition:** $d/L \geq 0.5$ ŌåÆ $C_0 = gT/(2\pi) \approx 1.56\,T$
- [x] **Deep water depends ONLY on T** (not on d or L)
- [x] **Shallow water condition:** $d/L \leq 0.05$ ŌåÆ $C = \sqrt{gd}$
- [x] **Shallow water depends ONLY on d** (not on T or L)
- [x] **Transitional regime:** use full dispersion relation + iterative solver
- [x] **Linear wave theory assumptions:** inviscid, incompressible, irrotational, small amplitude, constant depth
- [x] **Always check d/L FIRST** before choosing a celerity formula
- [x] **Tsunami physics:** deep ocean d Ōēł 4 km ŌåÆ C Ōēł 700 km/h; shallow coast d Ōēł 10 m ŌåÆ C Ōēł 36 km/h (20├ŚŌĆō280├Ś reduction)
- [x] **Group vs Phase:** $C_g = C/2$ in deep water, $C_g = C$ in shallow water
- [x] **Iterative formula for L:** $L_{n+1} = L_0 \cdot \tanh(2\pi d / L_n)$ with $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$
- [x] **g = 9.81 m/s┬▓** (standard value used throughout)
- [x] **Units discipline:** T in seconds, d and L in meters, C in m/s
- [x] **Engineering applications:** tsunami travel time, offshore platform design, harbor resonance, wave energy

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Fluid Particle Velocities and Acceleration Under Progressive Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Local Fluid Particle Velocities and Acceleration Under Progressive Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Local Fluid Particle Velocities and Acceleration Under Progressive Waves

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
>
> Under **Airy Linear Wave Theory** (also called Small Amplitude Wave Theory), the **local fluid particle velocity** is defined as the instantaneous velocity of an individual water parcel at a fixed spatial location $(x, z)$ and time $t$, as the wave propagates across the sea surface. For a two-dimensional progressive wave travelling in the $+x$ direction, the velocity field is obtained as the spatial gradient of the **velocity potential function** $\phi(x, z, t)$.

Mathematically, the velocity components are:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \qquad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

The corresponding **local fluid particle accelerations** are the time derivatives of these velocities:

$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}, \qquad a_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a stadium "wave" in a fluid medium.** When fans stand up and sit down sequentially, each fan (fluid particle) only moves **vertically a tiny bit** and the disturbance travels horizontally ŌĆö but the *people themselves* return to their original seat. Ocean waves behave identically: the wave *shape* travels thousands of kilometres, yet each water molecule only oscillates in a tiny **circular or elliptical orbit** near its mean position.
>
> - The **horizontal velocity $u$** = how fast the particle slides back-and-forth.
> - The **vertical velocity $w$** = how fast it bobs up-and-down.
> - The **accelerations $a_x, a_z$** = the rate of change of these velocities (important for designing offshore platforms, pipelines, and breakwaters).
>
> Near the **surface**, orbits are nearly circular; near the **seabed**, they become flat ellipses (horizontal motion only). The wave *energy* moves forward, but the *water particles* mostly stay in their neighbourhood.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (standard KTU value)
- **Wave angular frequency:** $\sigma = 2\pi/T$ where $T$ is the wave period (in seconds)
- **Wave number:** $k = 2\pi/L$ where $L$ is the wavelength (in metres)
- **Deep-water limit:** $d/L > 0.5$ ; **Shallow-water limit:** $d/L < 0.05$

> [!TIP]
> Always confirm whether the problem specifies **deep, intermediate, or shallow water** ŌĆö the hyperbolic functions in the kinematic equations reduce dramatically in deep water (e.g., $\cosh kd \approx \sinh kd \approx \frac{1}{2}e^{kd}$), which simplifies exam calculations considerably.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Starting Point: The Velocity Potential

Airy Wave Theory assumes an **ideal, incompressible, inviscid fluid** undergoing **irrotational flow**. Under these assumptions, a velocity potential $\phi(x, z, t)$ exists such that:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace's Equation)}$$

The general solution for a two-dimensional progressive wave advancing in the $+x$ direction, satisfying Laplace's equation and all kinematic and dynamic boundary conditions, is:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{g H}{2 \sigma} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z + d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Where:**
- $H$ = wave height (m)
- $d$ = still-water depth (m)
- $z$ = vertical coordinate measured from the **seabed** upward (so the SWL is at $z = 0$ and surface at $z = d + \eta$)
- The phase term $(kx - \sigma t)$ ensures the wave propagates in the $+x$ direction.

## 2.2 Why Particle Velocities Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why KTU tests this heavily:**
>
> 1. **Morison's Equation** for wave forces on slender cylinders uses $u$ and $a_x$ directly.
> 2. **Sediment transport** calculations under waves rely on near-bed velocities $u_{z=0}$.
> 3. **Small craft harbour design** requires the maximum velocity under design waves.
> 4. **Mooring line and riser dynamics** on offshore platforms need the full velocity and acceleration time-history.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Component

1. **Horizontal velocity $u$** ŌåÆ obtained by partial differentiation of $\phi$ w.r.t. $x$. The hyperbolic factor $\frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd}$ tells us how velocity decays with depth.
2. **Vertical velocity $w$** ŌåÆ obtained by partial differentiation of $\phi$ w.r.t. $z$. The hyperbolic factor $\frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd}$ peaks at the surface and vanishes at the seabed (a no-flow-through boundary).
3. **Acceleration** ŌåÆ obtained by time differentiation. Note that $u$ and $w$ are out-of-phase with each other (cosine vs. sine), which is the hallmark of orbital motion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet ŌĆö Particle Kinematics Under Airy Progressive Waves**

| # | Quantity | General (Finite Depth) Expression | Deep-Water Approximation $(d/L \to \infty)$ | Shallow-Water Approximation $(d/L \to 0)$ | Units |
|---|----------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Horizontal Velocity $u$ | $u = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ | $u = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ | $u = \dfrac{H}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ | m/s |
| 2 | Vertical Velocity $w$ | $w = -\dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | $w = -\dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | $w = -\dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{(z+d)}{d} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | m/s |
| 3 | Horizontal Acceleration $a_x$ | $a_x = \dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | $a_x = \dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | $a_x = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | m/s┬▓ |
| 4 | Vertical Acceleration $a_z$ | $a_z = -\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ | $a_z = -\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$ | $a_z = -\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{(z+d)}{d} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ | m/s┬▓ |
| 5 | Dispersion Relation | $\sigma^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ | $\sigma^2 = gk$ | $\sigma^2 = g k^2 d$ | ŌĆö |
| 6 | Surface Maxima ($\cos/\sin = 1$) | $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi H}{T \tanh(kd)}$ | $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi H}{T}$ | $u_{\max} = \dfrac{H}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}}$ | m/s |
| 7 | Seabed Maxima ($z=-d$) | $u_{b,\max} = \dfrac{\pi H}{T \sinh(kd)}$ | $u_{b,\max} \to 0$ | $u_{b,\max} = \dfrac{H}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}}$ | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The horizontal and vertical velocity components are always **90┬░ out of phase in time** ($u \propto \cos$, $w \propto \sin$). This phase lag is precisely what produces the **closed orbital trajectories** characteristic of Airy waves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Potential $\phi(x, z, t)$

We assume a 2-D progressive wave travelling in the $+x$ direction over a horizontal seabed at $z = -d$. The free surface is at $z = \eta(x, t)$ above the still-water level (SWL, $z = 0$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume a separable solution of Laplace's equation.**
Try a product of an $x$-$t$ travelling wave and a depth-dependent function:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = A(z) \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into Laplace's equation.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = -k^2 A(z) \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = A''(z) \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the resulting ODE for $A(z)$.**

$$A''(z) - k^2 A(z) = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$A(z) = C_1 \cosh(kz) + C_2 \sinh(kz)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the seabed boundary condition (no vertical flow through bottom).**

At $z = -d$:

$$w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k C_1 \sinh(-kd) + k C_2 \cosh(-kd) = 0$$

Since $\sinh(-kd) = -\sinh(kd)$ and $\cosh(-kd) = \cosh(kd)$:

$$-C_1 \sinh(kd) + C_2 \cosh(kd) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = C_2 \frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express the velocity potential.**

Substituting $C_1$ back, letting $C_2 \to B$:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = B \left[ \frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} \cosh(kz) + \sinh(kz) \right] \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

Using the identity $\cosh k(z+d) = \cosh(kd)\cosh(kz) + \sinh(kd)\sinh(kz)$:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{B}{\sinh(kd)} \cosh k(z+d) \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the dynamic free-surface boundary condition (linearised Bernoulli).**

At the linearised surface $z = 0$:

$$\eta = -\frac{1}{g} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0} = \frac{B \sigma}{g \sinh(kd)} \cosh(kd) \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

Also, by definition of Airy wave: $\eta = \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \sigma t)$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for constant $B$.**

Equating coefficients of $\cos(kx - \sigma t)$:

$$\frac{B \sigma \cosh(kd)}{g \sinh(kd)} = \frac{H}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \frac{g H}{2 \sigma} \cdot \frac{\sinh(kd)}{\cosh(kd)}$$

Substituting back gives the canonical Airy velocity potential:

$$\boxed{\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{g H}{2 \sigma} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Horizontal Velocity $u$

Differentiate $\phi$ with respect to $x$:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{g H}{2 \sigma} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot k \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

Now use $\sigma = 2\pi/T$, so $g/(2\sigma) \cdot k = gk/(2\sigma)$. Apply the **dispersion relation** $\sigma^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ which gives $gk = \sigma^2 / \tanh(kd)$:

$$\frac{g k}{2 \sigma} = \frac{\sigma}{2 \tanh(kd)} = \frac{\pi}{T \tanh(kd)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Vertical Velocity $w$

Differentiate $\phi$ with respect to $z$:

$$w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{g H}{2 \sigma} \cdot \frac{k \sinh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

Applying $gk/(2\sigma) = \pi/[T \tanh(kd)]$ and noting $1/\cosh(kd) = \tanh(kd)/\sinh(kd)$:

$$\boxed{w = -\frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)}$$

(The minus sign arises because increasing $z$ toward the surface corresponds to $\sin \to -\sin$ due to the conventional sign convention of the velocity potential gradient direction.)

## 3.4 Derivation of Accelerations

**Horizontal acceleration:**

$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sigma \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

Substituting $\sigma = 2\pi/T$:

$$\boxed{a_x = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)}$$

**Vertical acceleration:**

$$a_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = -\frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot (-\sigma) \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$\boxed{a_z = -\frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** A wave in $d = 10$ m water depth has $H = 2$ m and $T = 8$ s. Compute the maximum horizontal velocity and maximum horizontal acceleration at the seabed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute wavelength $L$.** Using the dispersion relation iteratively. First guess (deep water):

$$L_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 64}{6.2832} = 99.9 \text{ m}$$

Check $d/L_0 = 10/99.9 = 0.10$ ŌåÆ intermediate depth. Iterate using Fenton's or Newton's method on:

$$\sigma^2 = g k \tanh(kd) \Rightarrow L = L_0 \tanh(kd)$$

Iteration gives $L \approx 58.0$ m, so $k = 2\pi/58.0 = 0.1083$ rad/m.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute hyperbolic factors at the seabed $(z = -d)$:**

$$\cosh k(z+d) = \cosh(0) = 1$$

$$\sinh(kd) = \sinh(0.1083 \times 10) = \sinh(1.083) = 1.316$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum horizontal velocity at seabed:**

$$u_{b,\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T \sinh(kd)} = \frac{\pi \times 2}{8 \times 1.316} = \frac{6.283}{10.528} = 0.597 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum horizontal acceleration at seabed:**

$$a_{x,\max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2 \sinh(kd)} = \frac{2 \times 9.87 \times 2}{64 \times 1.316} = \frac{39.48}{84.22} = 0.469 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Allocation for such a problem:** 2 marks for setting up dispersion; 3 marks for hyperbolic evaluation; 3 marks each for $u$ and $a_x$ final answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö From Wave Parameters to Particle Kinematics

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical chain of relationships between wave parameters, hyperbolic depth-factors, and the resulting velocity/acceleration fields.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wave Input Parameters H, T, d"] --> B["Solve Dispersion Relation sigma squared equals gk tanh kd"]
    B --> C["Derive Wave Number k and Wavelength L"]
    C --> D["Compute Velocity Potential phi"]
    D --> E1["Horizontal Velocity u via partial phi by partial x"]
    D --> E2["Vertical Velocity w via partial phi by partial z"]
    E1 --> F1["Horizontal Acceleration a_x via partial u by partial t"]
    E2 --> F2["Vertical Acceleration a_z via partial w by partial t"]
    F1 --> G["Engineering Outputs Morison Force, Sediment Transport, Mooring Loads"]
    F2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    B --> H{"Depth Regime Check d by L"}
    H -->|Deep > 0.5| I["Use e to power kz simplification"]
    H -->|Shallow < 0.05| J["Use linear in z simplification"]
    H -->|Intermediate| K["Keep full hyperbolic form"]
```

## 4.2 Orbital Trajectory Schematic ŌĆö Depth Variation

The Mermaid diagram below visualises how the **horizontal velocity amplitude** varies with depth $z/d$ (where $z = -d$ is the seabed and $z = 0$ is SWL). This is the **classic KTU board diagram** examiners expect students to be able to sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surface_Zone["Surface zone z near 0"]
        S1["Orbit nearly circular"] --> S2["u_max and w_max both large"]
    end
    subgraph Mid_Depth["Mid depth z equals minus d by 2"]
        M1["Orbit elliptical"] --> M2["w reduces more than u"]
    end
    subgraph Seabed_Zone["Seabed zone z equals minus d"]
        B1["Orbit flat horizontal line"] --> B2["Only u exists, w equals 0"]
    end
    Surface_Zone --> Mid_Depth
    Mid_Depth --> Seabed_Zone
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Particle Path Diagram (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0["Particle at Mean Position"] --> P1["Move forward and up to crest"]
    P1 --> P2["Reverse direction, move down at crest"]
    P2 --> P3["Move backward and down to trough"]
    P3 --> P4["Reverse direction, move up at trough"]
    P4 --> P0
    style P0 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The particle traces a **closed clockwise orbit** under a wave moving in the $+x$ direction. The orbit's eccentricity depends on $kd$. In deep water, orbits are circular; in shallow water, they flatten into back-and-forth horizontal motion at the seabed.

## 4.4 Decay of Velocity with Depth ŌĆö Schematic Values Table

| Depth $z/d$ | $\dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd}$ (deep) | $\dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd}$ (deep) | Orbit Shape |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.0 (surface) | 1.000 | 1.000 | Circle |
| -0.25 | 0.607 | 0.487 | Slight ellipse |
| -0.50 | 0.369 | 0.217 | Flat ellipse |
| -0.75 | 0.223 | 0.097 | Very flat |
| -1.00 (seabed) | 0.135 | 0.000 | Straight line |

> [!TIP]
> This table is highly useful for **KTU short-answer and viva questions**. Memorise that at the seabed, $w = 0$ always and $u_{\text{seabed}} = u_{\text{surface}} / \cosh kd$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model]** 
State the expressions for horizontal and vertical fluid particle velocities under a linear progressive wave in water of finite depth $d$. Explain how these expressions simplify in deep water.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a 2-D linear (Airy) progressive wave of height $H$, period $T$, in finite depth $d$, the velocity components at location $(x, z)$ and time $t$ are:

$$u = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$w = -\frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**In deep water** ($d/L > 0.5$, i.e., $kd \to \infty$): the hyperbolic functions simplify using $\cosh k(z+d) \approx \sinh k(z+d) \approx \frac{1}{2}e^{k(z+d)}$, giving:

$$u_{\text{deep}} = \frac{\pi H}{T} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \sigma t), \quad w_{\text{deep}} = -\frac{\pi H}{T} e^{kz} \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

The exponential factor $e^{kz}$ shows that velocities **decay rapidly with depth** ŌĆö at one wavelength depth, the velocity is reduced by a factor of $e^{-2\pi} \approx 1/535$. **(1 mark for expression, 1 mark for finite-depth form, 1 mark for deep-water simplification.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]**
Why is the **horizontal fluid particle acceleration** $a_x$ used in Morison's equation for computing wave forces on a vertical pile, rather than the velocity alone?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Morison's equation splits the total horizontal wave force per unit length of a slender cylinder into two parts:

$$f = f_D + f_I = \frac{1}{2} C_D \rho D \vert u \vert u + C_M \rho \frac{\pi D^2}{4} a_x$$

- The **drag component** $f_D$ depends on the velocity $u$ (since drag is a viscous effect proportional to dynamic pressure).
- The **inertia component** $f_I$ depends on the **acceleration $a_x$** (since inertial force equals mass $\times$ acceleration; the virtual mass coefficient $C_M$ accounts for the displaced water).

Therefore both velocity AND acceleration are needed: velocity for drag, acceleration for inertia. For small-diameter cylinders (where inertia dominates), $a_x$ is the primary parameter. For typical offshore piles ($D/L < 0.2$), both terms must be evaluated ŌĆö hence $a_x$ is indispensable. **(1 mark drag, 1 mark inertia, 1 mark justification.)**

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dispersion relation**: A common mistake is computing $k$ from deep-water formula $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi)$ in finite depth. Always iterate the dispersion relation $\sigma^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$.
> 2. **Sign convention errors**: The SWL is at $z = 0$ and the seabed is at $z = -d$. Reversing this costs **2ŌĆō3 marks instantly**.
> 3. **Forgetting to substitute $\sigma = 2\pi/T$**: Many students leave the answer in terms of $\sigma$ when the question asks for a numerical answer ŌĆö **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Phase mix-up**: $u \propto \cos(kx - \sigma t)$ and $w \propto \sin(kx - \sigma t)$. If a question asks "maximum at the wave crest", remember crest is at $kx - \sigma t = 0$ where $\cos = 1$ (so $u$ is max) but $\sin = 0$ (so $w = 0$). Draw the orbit to verify.
> 5. **Units**: Always show units in the final answer ŌĆö missing units lose marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model]**

A linear progressive wave with height $H = 3$ m and period $T = 10$ s propagates in water depth $d = 15$ m.

#### (a) Determine the wavelength, wave number, and phase velocity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Use the dispersion relation:**

$$\sigma^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$$

With $\sigma = 2\pi/T = 2\pi/10 = 0.6283$ rad/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓, $d = 15$ m.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Guess deep-water wavelength:**

$$L_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 100}{6.2832} = 156.1 \text{ m}$$

Check $d/L_0 = 15/156.1 = 0.0961$ ŌåÆ finite-depth wave.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the iterative Fenton-McKee equation** $L = L_0 \tanh(2\pi d / L)$:

- Iteration 1: $L_1 = 156.1 \times \tanh(2\pi \times 15 / 156.1) = 156.1 \times \tanh(0.604) = 156.1 \times 0.5394 = 84.20$ m
- Iteration 2: $L_2 = 156.1 \times \tanh(2\pi \times 15 / 84.20) = 156.1 \times \tanh(1.1196) = 156.1 \times 0.8087 = 126.24$ m
- Iteration 3: $L_3 = 156.1 \times \tanh(2\pi \times 15 / 126.24) = 156.1 \times \tanh(0.7467) = 156.1 \times 0.6340 = 98.97$ m
- Iteration 4: $L_4 = 156.1 \times \tanh(0.9521) = 156.1 \times 0.7403 = 115.58$ m
- Iteration 5: $L_5 = 156.1 \times \tanh(0.8153) = 156.1 \times 0.6736 = 105.15$ m
- Iteration 6: $L_6 = 156.1 \times \tanh(0.8962) = 156.1 \times 0.7138 = 111.43$ m
- Converging to $L \approx 108.5$ m.

**Answer (after convergence):** $L \approx 108.5$ m, hence $k = 2\pi/L = 0.0579$ rad/m, and $C = L/T = 10.85$ m/s.

**Mark split:** (1 mark setup, 3 marks iteration, 1 mark each for $L$, $k$, $C$).

#### (b) Compute the maximum horizontal velocity and maximum horizontal acceleration at a point located 5 m below the still water level. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Locate the point:** $z = -5$ m (5 m below SWL, with seabed at $z = -15$ m).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the depth argument:** $k(z + d) = 0.0579 \times (-5 + 15) = 0.0579 \times 10 = 0.579$ rad.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate hyperbolic functions:**

$$\cosh(0.579) = 1.173, \quad \sinh(0.579) = 0.612$$

$$\sinh(kd) = \sinh(0.0579 \times 15) = \sinh(0.8685) = 0.9673$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum horizontal velocity** (when $\cos(kx - \sigma t) = 1$):

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T \cdot \sinh(kd)} \cdot \cosh[k(z+d)] = \frac{\pi \times 3}{10 \times 0.9673} \times 1.173$$

$$u_{\max} = \frac{9.425}{9.673} \times 1.173 = 0.9744 \times 1.173 = 1.143 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum horizontal acceleration** (when $\sin(kx - \sigma t) = 1$):

$$a_{x,\max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2 \cdot \sinh(kd)} \cdot \cosh[k(z+d)] = \frac{2\pi^2 \times 3}{100 \times 0.9673} \times 1.173$$

$$a_{x,\max} = \frac{59.22}{96.73} \times 1.173 = 0.6123 \times 1.173 = 0.718 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark each for $k(z+d)$, $\cosh$, $\sinh(kd)$; 2 marks for $u_{\max}$; 2 marks for $a_{x,\max}$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]**

For a linear progressive wave of height $H = 1.5$ m and period $T = 6$ s in water depth $d = 8$ m:

#### (a) Derive the expressions for horizontal and vertical particle velocities and accelerations under finite depth, starting from the velocity potential. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the velocity potential for a 2-D progressive wave** satisfying Laplace's equation and seabed boundary condition:

$$\phi(x, z, t) = \frac{gH}{2\sigma} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Derive horizontal velocity** $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$:

$$u = \frac{gH}{2\sigma} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot k \cos(kx - \sigma t)$$

Apply the dispersion relation $gk = \sigma^2 / \tanh(kd)$, so $gk/(2\sigma) = \sigma/(2\tanh kd) = \pi/(T \tanh kd)$. Since $1/\cosh kd = \tanh kd / \sinh kd$:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Derive vertical velocity** $w = \partial \phi / \partial z$:

$$w = \frac{gH}{2\sigma} \cdot \frac{k \sinh k(z+d)}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$$

$$\boxed{w = -\frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derive horizontal acceleration** $a_x = \partial u / \partial t$:

$$\boxed{a_x = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive vertical acceleration** $a_z = \partial w / \partial t$:

$$\boxed{a_z = -\frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)}$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark $\phi$ statement; 2 marks $u$ derivation; 2 marks $w$ derivation; 1 mark $a_x$; 1 mark $a_z$).

#### (b) Calculate the maximum horizontal velocity at the seabed and at the surface. Compare with the values predicted by deep-water theory. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Solve dispersion relation for $k$.** With $T = 6$ s, $d = 8$ m, $\sigma = 2\pi/6 = 1.0472$ rad/s.

$L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 36 / 6.2832 = 56.21$ m. $d/L_0 = 8/56.21 = 0.1423$ ŌåÆ finite depth.

Iterate: $L \to$ converges near $L \approx 46.8$ m, so $k = 2\pi/46.8 = 0.1343$ rad/m, $\sinh(kd) = \sinh(0.1343 \times 8) = \sinh(1.0744) = 1.296$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum at the surface** ($z = 0$, $\cosh(kd) = \cosh(1.0744) = 1.658$):

$$u_{s,\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T \sinh(kd)} \cdot \cosh(kd) = \frac{\pi \times 1.5}{6 \times 1.296} \times 1.658$$

$$u_{s,\max} = 0.6059 \times 1.658 = 1.005 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum at the seabed** ($z = -d$, $\cosh 0 = 1$):

$$u_{b,\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T \sinh(kd)} \cdot 1 = 0.6059 \times 1 = 0.606 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Deep-water comparison** (using $L_0 = 56.21$ m):

Surface value: $u_{s,\max,\text{deep}} = \pi H / T = \pi \times 1.5 / 6 = 0.7854$ m/s (lower than finite-depth result).

Seabed in deep water: theoretically zero ŌĆö but here we get 0.606 m/s because this is **not** truly deep water.

**Discussion:** The finite-depth surface velocity (1.005 m/s) exceeds the deep-water estimate (0.785 m/s) due to wave shoaling effects. The seabed velocity (0.606 m/s) is **non-zero**, demonstrating that in finite depth the wave motion reaches the bottom ŌĆö important for sediment transport.

**Mark split:** (2 marks dispersion; 1 mark each for surface and seabed maxima; 2 marks comparison with deep-water theory; 1 mark engineering interpretation).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Local Fluid Particle Kinematics Under Progressive Waves**

- **Velocity potential:** $\phi = \dfrac{gH}{2\sigma} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$
- **Horizontal velocity $u$**: $u = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$
- **Vertical velocity $w$**: $w = -\dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$
- **Horizontal acceleration $a_x$**: $a_x = \dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \sigma t)$
- **Vertical acceleration $a_z$**: $a_z = -\dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \cos(kx - \sigma t)$
- **Dispersion relation:** $\sigma^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ ŌĆö **must be iterated** in finite depth.
- **Phase relationship:** $u \propto \cos$, $w \propto \sin$ ŌåÆ **90┬░ out of phase** ŌåÆ circular/elliptical orbits.
- **Seabed condition:** $w = 0$ at $z = -d$ always; $u$ is maximum there in shallow water and minimum (zero) in deep water.
- **Surface condition:** Both $u$ and $w$ attain their maximum amplitudes at the surface.
- **Deep-water shortcut:** Replace $\cosh/\sinh kd$ by $e^{kz}$.
- **Shallow-water shortcut:** Replace $\cosh k(z+d)/\sinh kd \approx 1$ and $\sinh k(z+d)/\sinh kd \approx (z+d)/d$.
- **Engineering applications:** Morison's equation, sediment transport, mooring dynamics, breakwater stability.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** sign convention on $z$, forgetting to iterate dispersion, leaving answers in terms of $\sigma$ instead of $T$.
- **Units reminder:** $H$ in m, $T$ in s, $d$ in m, $z$ in m, $u$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s┬▓, $g$ in m/s┬▓, $k$ in rad/m, $\sigma$ in rad/s.
- **Quick sanity check:** For a 1 m wave with 8 s period in 10 m depth, surface $u_{\max}$ should be of the order of **1 m/s**, seabed $u_{\max}$ around **0.5ŌĆō0.7 m/s**, and accelerations of order **0.5ŌĆō1 m/s┬▓**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Particle Displacement Under Progressive Waves
Pressure Distribution Under Progressive Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Distribution Under Progressive Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pressure Distribution Under Progressive Waves** refers to the spatial and temporal variation of fluid pressure at any point beneath the free surface of a traveling (progressive) gravity wave in an incompressible, inviscid fluid of finite or infinite depth. Under the framework of **Linear (Airy) Wave Theory**, the total pressure at a point located at horizontal coordinate $x$ and vertical coordinate $z$ (measured positive upward from the still water level, SWL) beneath a wave crest or trough is the superposition of a **hydrostatic component** and a **dynamic (wave-induced) component**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The pressure beneath a progressive wave is given by:
> $$p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g \eta(x,t) \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh}$$
> where $h$ is the mean water depth, $k = 2\pi/L$ is the wave number, and $\eta$ is the instantaneous free surface elevation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing chest-deep in a swimming pool when a gentle wave passes. You feel:
1. A **steady squeeze** around your body ŌĆö this is the **hydrostatic pressure**, equal to the weight of the water column above you. It is there even when the pool is perfectly still.
2. A gentle **oscillating push** that grows stronger when a wave crest passes over you and weakens (or even reverses) when a trough passes. This is the **dynamic pressure** ŌĆö extra pressure due to the wave motion itself.

Now imagine going underwater with a scuba pressure gauge. As a wave passes:
- The gauge reading goes up and down rhythmically.
- The **closer you are to the surface**, the larger the fluctuation.
- The **deeper you go**, the fluctuation becomes smaller. At very great depth, the wave is "invisible" to the gauge ŌĆö only the hydrostatic pressure remains.

This physical intuition matches the math exactly. The dynamic pressure decays **exponentially with depth**, governed by the **cosh-based pressure response factor** $K_p$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m┬│** (typical ocean) |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s┬▓** |
| Wave number | $k$ | $2\pi/L$ rad/m |
| Mean water depth | $h$ | meters (m) |
| Wave height | $H$ | meters (m) |
| Wave amplitude | $a$ | $H/2$ (m) |
| Angular frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Dynamic pressure amplitude | $p_d$ | Pa or N/m┬▓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions of Linear Wave Theory** (essential for validity of the pressure equation):
> 1. Fluid is **incompressible** and **inviscid**.
> 2. Flow is **irrotational** (valid for inviscid fluid from rest).
> 3. Wave amplitude is **small** ($H/L \ll 1$, typically $< 0.06$).
> 4. Fluid is **homogeneous** (constant $\rho$).
> 5. Surface tension is **negligible** (gravity waves).
> 6. Sea bed is **horizontal and impermeable**.
> 7. Pressure at the free surface is **uniform** (atmospheric, taken as zero gauge).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The pressure distribution under progressive waves is derived from the **unsteady Bernoulli equation** applied to an irrotational, inviscid flow:

$$p + \frac{1}{2}\rho \vert \vec{V} \vert^2 + \rho g z + \rho \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = f(t)$$

For linear waves, the **velocity-squared (non-linear) term is neglected** because the velocity itself is of order $O(a)$ and its square is $O(a^2)$, which is second-order small. After dropping this term and applying the **dynamic free surface boundary condition** (linearized), the constant of integration $f(t)$ is evaluated to yield the pressure field.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Velocity Potential of a Linear Progressive Wave**:
   The fundamental solution for a wave traveling in the $+x$ direction is:
   $$\Phi(x,z,t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

2. **Substitute into Linearized Bernoulli**:
   $p/\rho + gz + \partial \Phi/\partial t = 0$ (gauge pressure form, constant absorbed).

3. **Compute the Time Derivative**:
   $\partial \Phi/\partial t = -a g \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \cos(kx - \omega t)$

4. **Use Surface Elevation Identity**:
   Recall $\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$, so $\cos(kx - \omega t) = \eta/a$.

5. **Assemble Final Pressure Expression**:
   $$p = -\rho g z + \rho g \eta \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh}$$

6. **Decompose into Two Physical Components**:
   - **Hydrostatic pressure**: $p_{hs} = -\rho g z$ (the weight of the overlying water column).
   - **Dynamic (wave-induced) pressure**: $p_d = \rho g \eta \cdot K_p(z)$, where $K_p(z) = \cosh k(h+z)/\cosh kh$ is the **pressure response factor**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations, units, and conditions you must memorize for the KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | Linear wave surface profile | m |
| 2 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | Linear dispersion relation | rad┬▓/s┬▓ |
| 3 | $k = 2\pi/L$ | Wave number | rad/m |
| 4 | $c = \omega/k = \sqrt{g \tanh(kh)/k}$ | Wave celerity | m/s |
| 5 | $c_g = c/2 \cdot (1 + 2kh/\sinh(2kh))$ | Group velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g \eta K_p$ | **Total pressure** | Pa |
| 7 | $K_p = \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh}$ | **Pressure response factor** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $K_p(z=0) = 1$ | At free surface (dynamic = hydrostatic from $\eta$) | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $K_p(z=-h) = \frac{1}{\cosh kh}$ | At sea bed | ŌĆö |
| 10 | $p_d \vert_{z=-h} = \frac{\rho g \eta}{\cosh kh}$ | Dynamic pressure at bed | Pa |
| 11 | $p_{total} \vert_{z=-h} = \rho g h + \frac{\rho g \eta}{\cosh kh}$ | Total bed pressure | Pa |
| 12 | $\lim_{h \to \infty} K_p = e^{kz}$ | Deep water limit | ŌĆö |
| 13 | $\lim_{kh \to 0} K_p = 1$ | Shallow water limit | ŌĆö |
| 14 | $\Phi = \frac{ag}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \sin(kx-\omega t)$ | Velocity potential | m┬▓/s |
| 15 | $u = \partial \Phi/\partial x$, $w = \partial \Phi/\partial z$ | Velocity components | m/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore structure design**: Jacket platforms, monopiles, and subsea pipelines experience **cyclic pressure loading** at the seabed, governing fatigue life.
- **Coastal sediment transport**: The bottom pressure gradient drives **oscillatory boundary layer flow**, which mobilizes sand and generates ripples.
- **Wave energy converters**: Pressure differential across oscillating water column (OWC) devices is the **prime mover** of energy extraction.
- **Harbor resonance and seiching**: Pressure-driven long-wave oscillations inside basins.
- **Submarine hydrodynamics**: Submerged bodies feel wave pressure fluctuations that affect **depth-keeping and control**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Pressure Equation

**Starting Point: Linearized Bernoulli Equation for an Inviscid, Irrotational Flow**

For an incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow, the **unsteady Bernoulli equation** along a streamline (and hence throughout the fluid, by irrotationality) is:

$$p + \frac{1}{2}\rho \vert \vec{V} \vert^2 + \rho g z + \rho \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = f(t)$$

where:
- $p$ is the pressure,
- $\vec{V}$ is the velocity vector,
- $\Phi$ is the velocity potential (such that $\vec{V} = \nabla \Phi$),
- $f(t)$ is an arbitrary function of time (since the equation is a spatial gradient being set equal to a function of time only).

**Step 1: Linearization ŌĆö Drop the Convective Term**

For linear (Airy) waves, the velocity components $u$ and $w$ are of order $O(a)$. Therefore:

$$\vert \vec{V} \vert^2 = u^2 + w^2 = O(a^2)$$

Since we are neglecting all terms of order $a^2$ and higher (small-amplitude assumption), we set:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho \vert \vec{V} \vert^2 \approx 0$$

The linearized Bernoulli equation becomes:

$$p + \rho g z + \rho \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = f(t)$$

**Step 2: Express the Velocity Potential of a Progressive Wave**

For a linear wave of amplitude $a$, traveling in the $+x$ direction in water of constant depth $h$, the velocity potential is:

$$\Phi(x,z,t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

> **Why this form?** It is the unique solution of Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$ that satisfies:
> (a) zero normal velocity at the sea bed: $\partial \Phi/\partial z = 0$ at $z = -h$,
> (b) the linearized kinematic and dynamic free-surface boundary conditions at $z = 0$.

**Step 3: Evaluate the Time Derivative of $\Phi$**

Differentiate $\Phi$ with respect to time (treat $x$ and $z$ as constants):

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t) \cdot (-\omega)$$

Simplify by canceling $\omega$:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = -a g \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Linearized Bernoulli Equation**

$$p + \rho g z + \rho \left[ -a g \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \cos(kx - \omega t) \right] = f(t)$$

**Step 5: Determine the Arbitrary Function $f(t)$ Using the Free-Surface Condition**

At the free surface, $z = \eta(x,t)$ and the pressure equals the atmospheric pressure (taken as the gauge-zero reference, $p = 0$):

$$0 + \rho g \eta + \rho \left[ -a g \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh} \cos(kx - \omega t) \right]_{z=\eta} = f(t)$$

Because $\eta$ is small ($O(a)$), we evaluate the cosh term at $z = 0$ (its value at the surface) to first order:

$$\rho g \eta - \rho a g \cos(kx - \omega t) = f(t)$$

But by definition, the surface elevation is:

$$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

Therefore:

$$\rho g \cdot a \cos(kx - \omega t) - \rho a g \cos(kx - \omega t) = f(t) \implies f(t) = 0$$

This elegantly shows that **the arbitrary function vanishes** under the linearized free-surface condition.

**Step 6: Write the Final Pressure Equation**

The pressure at any point $(x,z)$ in the fluid at time $t$ is:

$$p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g a \cos(kx - \omega t) \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh}$$

Recognizing that $a \cos(kx - \omega t) = \eta$, we obtain the **canonical KTU result**:

$$\boxed{\,p(x,z,t) = -\rho g z + \rho g \eta(x,t) \cdot \frac{\cosh k(h+z)}{\cosh kh}\,}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Pressure Response Factor at Key Locations

| Location | Vertical Coordinate $z$ | $K_p$ Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free surface (mean)** | $0$ | $1$ | Dynamic pressure equals $\rho g \eta$ (full strength) |
| **Mid-depth** | $-h/2$ | $\cosh(kh/2)/\cosh(kh)$ | Intermediate decay |
| **Sea bed** | $-h$ | $1/\cosh(kh)$ | Minimum dynamic pressure |
| **Deep water (any $z$)** | $h \to \infty$ | $e^{kz}$ | Pure exponential decay |

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Numerical Evaluation

> **Problem:** A wave of height $H = 2$ m and period $T = 8$ s propagates in water of depth $h = 10$ m. Find the maximum total pressure at the sea bed, given $\rho = 1025$ kg/m┬│ and $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

**Step 1: Compute Wave Amplitude**

$$a = H/2 = 1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute Angular Frequency**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Wave Number $k$ from the Dispersion Relation**

$$\omega^2 = g k \tanh(kh) \implies 0.7854^2 = 9.81 \cdot k \cdot \tanh(10k)$$

Iterate (or use tables):

$$k \approx 0.0946 \text{ rad/m}, \quad L = \frac{2\pi}{k} \approx 66.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate $K_p$ at the Sea Bed**

$$K_p \vert_{z=-h} = \frac{1}{\cosh(kh)} = \frac{1}{\cosh(0.0946 \times 10)} = \frac{1}{\cosh(0.946)} = \frac{1}{1.503} \approx 0.665$$

**Step 5: Compute Maximum Dynamic Pressure at the Bed**

Maximum occurs at wave crest, $\eta = a = 1$ m:

$$p_d^{max} = \rho g a K_p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1 \times 0.665 \approx 6689 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.69 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6: Add Hydrostatic Pressure at the Bed**

$$p_{hs} = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 100{,}553 \text{ Pa} \approx 100.55 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 7: Total Maximum Bed Pressure**

$$p_{total}^{max} = 100.55 + 6.69 = 107.24 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The dynamic contribution at the bed is only **~6.2%** of the hydrostatic pressure in this case. However, this oscillating component is what causes sediment mobilization, pipeline fatigue, and subsea foundation loading cycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Derivation Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Linearized Unsteady Bernoulli Equation"] --> B["Drop Convective Term: 0.5 rho times V squared"]
    B --> C["Use Velocity Potential Phi of Linear Wave"]
    C --> D["Compute Partial Phi by Partial t"]
    D --> E["Apply Free Surface Boundary Condition p equals 0 at z equals eta"]
    E --> F["Arbitrary Function f of t equals 0"]
    F --> G["Final Pressure Equation"]
    G --> H["Hydrostatic Component minus rho g z"]
    G --> I["Dynamic Component rho g eta times Kp"]
    I --> J["Kp equals cosh k times h plus z over cosh k h"]
    J --> K["Evaluate Kp at Sea Bed: 1 over cosh k h"]
    J --> L["Evaluate Kp at Surface: equals 1"]
    H --> M["Total Pressure Field p x z t"]
    I --> M
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pressure Response Factor Decay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Mean Water Level z equals 0"] -->|"Kp equals 1.0"| B["Strongest Dynamic Pressure"]
    B -->|"Decay with depth"| C["Mid Depth Kp smaller"]
    C -->|"Continued exponential decay"| D["Sea Bed z equals minus h"]
    D -->|"Kp equals 1 over cosh kh"| E["Minimum Dynamic Pressure"]
    E -->|"If h is very large"| F["Deep Water: Kp equals e to power kz"]
    F --> G["Pressure Fluctuation Vanishes at Great Depth"]
```

    A["Pressure Distribution Under Progressive Waves"] --> B["Offshore Structures"]
    A --> C["Coastal Sediment Transport"]
    A --> D["Wave Energy Converters"]
    A --> E["Submarine and Subsea Pipelines"]
    B --> B1["Cyclic Loading on Jacket Platforms"]
    B --> B2["Fatigue Analysis of Monopiles"]
    C --> C1["Bed Pressure Gradient Drives Sediment Motion"]
    C --> C2["Ripple Formation on Seabed"]
    D --> D1["Oscillating Water Column Devices"]
    D --> D2["Pressure Differential Energy Harvesting"]
    E --> E1["On-bottom Stability Calculations"]
    E --> E2["Vortex Induced Vibration Analysis"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation: Pressure Field Beneath a Wave Crest

```
       z = ╬Ę (free surface, wave crest)
       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  ŌåÉ atmosphere (p = 0)
        \   |  /
         \  | /      ŌåÉ region of strong dynamic pressure
          \ |/
           \|        ŌåÉ Kp = 1 at surface
   SWL ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ z = 0
            |  \
            |   \     ŌåÉ Kp decays exponentially
            |    \
            |     \
            |______\  ŌåÉ Kp = 1/cosh(kh) at bed
       z = -h (sea bed)
   ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
   p_total = Žügh + Žüg┬Ę╬Ę┬ĘKp
   p_dyn  = Žüg┬Ę╬Ę┬ĘKp  (oscillating part)
   p_hs   = Žügh       (steady part)
