<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** State the Airy dispersion relation and briefly explain why it is called a "dispersion" relation.

**Model Answer:**

$$\omega^{2} \;=\; gk\,\tanh(kd)$$

It is termed a *dispersion* relation because the **phase velocity $C = \omega/k$ depends on the wave number $k$** (or equivalently, the period $T$). Consequently, **long-period waves travel faster than short-period waves** in finite-depth water. In the *deep-water* limit, $C = gT/(2\pi)$ grows linearly with $T$. In the *shallow-water* limit, $C = \sqrt{gd}$ becomes **independent of $T$** (the waves become non-dispersive). **[3 marks: 1 for relation, 1 for k-dependence, 1 for deep vs. shallow contrast]**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** Distinguish between *phase velocity* and *group velocity* in deep water. Why is the distinction important for ship navigation?

**Model Answer:**

- **Phase velocity** $C$: speed of an individual wave crest = $gT/(2\pi)$ in deep water.
- **Group velocity** $C_g$: speed at which wave *energy* (a wave group/packet) travels = $C/2$ in deep water.

For a ship, the **energy** ŌĆö which is what creates resistance, heaving motion, and structural fatigue ŌĆö travels at $C_g$, not $C$. Therefore, a ship navigating through a wave train physically experiences the *group* velocity, not the celerity of individual crests. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Starting from the Laplace equation for an inviscid, incompressible, irrotational fluid, derive the **Airy dispersion relation** $\omega^{2} = gk\,\tanh(kd)$ by applying the seabed and linearized free-surface boundary conditions. State clearly the assumptions made. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A progressive wave in **15 m** water depth has a height of $H = 1.5$ m and period $T = 6$ s. Using Airy theory, determine:
- (i) The wavelength $L$ and celerity $C$.
- (ii) The maximum horizontal velocity at the **surface** and at the **seabed**.
- (iii) The maximum vertical acceleration at the **surface**. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question A ŌĆö Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation

The derivation is identical to **Section 3.1** above. Mark scheme:

| Step | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| State assumptions (inviscid, incompressible, irrotational, 2D, small-amplitude) | 1 |
| Write Laplace equation $\nabla^{2}\Phi = 0$ | 1 |
| Assume separable form $\Phi = f(z)\cos(kx-\omega t)$ | 1 |
| Obtain $f(z) = A\cosh kz + B\sinh kz$ | 1 |
| Apply seabed BC $\to f(z) = \dfrac{A}{\cosh kd}\cosh k(z+d)$ | 1 |
| Apply free-surface BC and simplify to $\omega^{2} = gk\tanh kd$ | 2 |
| **Total** | **7** |

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical

**(i) Wavelength & Celerity.** Deep-water trial: $L_0 = gT^{2}/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 36 / 6.2832 \approx 56.21$ m. $d/L_0 = 15/56.21 = 0.267$ ŌåÆ **transitional**.

Iterate $L = L_0\,\tanh(2\pi d / L)$:

- $L_1 = 56.21 \tanh(2\pi \times 15/56.21) = 56.21 \tanh(1.676) = 56.21 \times 0.9333 = 52.46$ m
- $L_2 = 56.21 \tanh(2\pi \times 15/52.46) = 56.21 \tanh(1.796) = 56.21 \times 0.9463 = 53.19$ m
- $L_3 = 56.21 \tanh(2\pi \times 15/53.19) = 56.21 \tanh(1.772) = 56.21 \times 0.9436 = 53.04$ m
- $L_4 = 56.21 \tanh(2\pi \times 15/53.04) = 56.21 \tanh(1.777) = 56.21 \times 0.9441 = 53.07$ m Ō£ō

$$\boxed{\,L \;\approx\; 53.1 \text{ m}\,}, \quad C = L/T = 53.1/6 \approx 8.85 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Velocity Amplitudes.** $k = 2\pi/53.1 = 0.1183$ rad/m; $kd = 1.775$. $\sinh(1.775) = 2.736$; $\cosh(1.775) = 2.915$.

- Surface: $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.5}{6} \times \dfrac{2.915}{2.736} = 0.785 \times 1.066 \approx 0.836 \text{ m/s}$
- Seabed: $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.5}{6} \times \dfrac{1}{2.736} = 0.785 \times 0.3655 \approx 0.287 \text{ m/s}$

**(iii) Maximum Vertical Acceleration at Surface.** Differentiate $w$ twice in time. Surface amplitude $z=0$:

$$\dot w_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2\pi^{2}H}{T^{2}} \cdot \frac{\sinh kd}{\sinh kd} \;=\; \frac{2\pi^{2}H}{T^{2}}$$

Plug in: $\dot w_{\max} = 2\pi^{2} \times 1.5 / 36 = 29.61/36 \approx 0.822 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

$$\boxed{\,\dot w_{\max} \;\approx\; 0.822 \text{ m/s}^{2}\,}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the terms *celerity*, *wavelength*, *wave number*, and *angular frequency*. Derive the relationship between them for a deep-water wave. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wave with $H = 3$ m and $T = 10$ s propagates in **deep water**. Compute:
- (i) The deep-water wavelength $L_0$ and celerity $C_0$.
- (ii) The maximum horizontal and vertical velocities at $z = -L_0/4$ below the still water level.
- (iii) The total energy density per unit horizontal area. **[7 marks]**

### Question B ŌĆö Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Definitions & Derivation

Definitions (2 marks):

- **Celerity $C$**: speed of an individual wave crest, $C = L/T$.
- **Wavelength $L$**: horizontal spacing between successive crests.
- **Wave number $k$**: $k = 2\pi / L$, angular measure per unit length.
- **Angular frequency $\omega$**: $\omega = 2\pi / T$, angular measure per unit time.

Derivation (5 marks): For deep water ($d \to \infty$), $\tanh(kd) \to 1$, so the dispersion relation reduces to $\omega^{2} = gk$. Hence:

$$\frac{(2\pi/T)^{2}}{2\pi/L} \;=\; g \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_0 \;=\; \frac{\omega}{k} \;=\; \frac{gT}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{g}{2\pi/L_0}$$

with $L_0 = gT^{2}/(2\pi)$.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Deep-Water Numerical

**(i)** $L_0 = 9.81 \times 100/(2\pi) = 156.13$ m; $C_0 = 156.13/10 = 15.61$ m/s.

**(ii)** At $z = -L_0/4$: $kz = 2\pi(-L_0/4)/L_0 = -\pi/2$. In deep water:

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T}\,e^{kz} = \frac{\pi \times 3}{10}\,e^{-\pi/2} = 0.9425 \times 0.2079 \approx 0.196 \text{ m/s}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{\pi H}{T}\,e^{kz} \approx 0.196 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(equal in deep water)}$$

**(iii)** Total energy density per unit area:

$$E \;=\; \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9 \;\approx\; 11{,}313 \text{ J/m}^{2} \;\approx\; 11.3 \text{ kJ/m}^{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign of $z$:** Forgetting whether $z$ is positive up or down. Mismatch gives *wrong exponent* $e^{kz}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to iterate $L$ in transitional water:** Plugging $L_0$ directly into $u$ formulas yields errors of 20ŌĆō50%.
> 3. **Radians vs degrees** in $\sin/\cos/\tanh$ arguments ŌĆö the **#1 numerical mistake** in KTU papers.
> 4. **Using $g = 9.8$ vs $9.81$** inconsistently mid-solution. Pick one and stick with it.
> 5. **Confusing $\sinh$ with $\cosh$** in $u$ vs $w$ formulas (vertical uses $\sinh$, horizontal uses $\cosh$ in the numerator).
> 6. **Forgetting the $\pi$ in $\pi H/T$** ŌĆö many students write $H/T$ and lose 2 marks silently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **MUST-KNOW BULLETS ŌĆö Re-read these 60 seconds before the exam.**

- Ō£ģ The **Airy dispersion relation** is $\omega^{2} = gk\tanh(kd)$ ŌĆö derive it cold.
- Ō£ģ **Deep water** ŌćÆ $L_0 = gT^{2}/(2\pi)$, $C_0 = gT/(2\pi)$, kinematics decay as $e^{kz}$.
- Ō£ģ **Shallow water** ŌćÆ $C = \sqrt{gd}$, $L = T\sqrt{gd}$, kinematics are uniform with depth.
- Ō£ģ **Transitional water** ŌćÆ **iterate** $L$ using $L = L_0 \tanh(2\pi d / L)$; converges in 5ŌĆō7 steps.
- Ō£ģ **Surface horizontal velocity (max)**: $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi H}{T \sinh kd}\cosh kd$.
- Ō£ģ **Seabed velocity (max)**: $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi H}{T \sinh kd}$ ŌĆö *crucial for sediment transport*.
- Ō£ģ **Acceleration (max)**: $\dot u_{\max} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}H}{T^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd}$ (├Ścos term for instantaneous).
- Ō£ģ **Dynamic pressure**: $p_d = \dfrac{\rho g H}{2}\dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd}\cos(kx-\omega t)$.
- Ō£ģ **Total energy density**: $E = \rho g H^{2}/8$ (same in all depths for linear theory).
- Ō£ģ **Group velocity**: $C_g = C/2$ deep, $= C$ shallow.
- Ō£ģ Always **state assumptions** before derivation (inviscid, incompressible, irrotational, small-amplitude).
- Ō£ģ $z$ is **positive upward** with $z = 0$ at MSL; $z = -d$ at the seabed.
- Ō£ģ Standard values: $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓, $\rho_{\text{salt}} = 1025$ kg/m┬│, $\rho_{\text{fresh}} = 1000$ kg/m┬│.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Regimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Force Regimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the hydrodynamics of ocean waveŌĆōstructure interaction, **Force Regimes** refer to the classified domains of wave-induced hydrodynamic loading on a submerged, floating, or partially submerged structure, demarcated by the *relative magnitudes* of the competing physical force components ŌĆö namely **viscous drag**, **fluid inertia**, and **wave diffraction/scattering**. The classification is governed primarily by two non-dimensional hydrodynamic parameters: the **KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number (KC)** and the **Diffraction Parameter (D/L or 2a/L)**.

Mathematically, the total inline horizontal force per unit length on a slender cylindrical member is expressed through the **Morison Equation**:

$$
F_x(t) = \underbrace{F_D}_{\text{Drag}} + \underbrace{F_I}_{\text{Inertia}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \, u \vert u \vert \;+\; \rho C_M \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,\dot{u}
$$

where the *relative dominance* of the first term versus the second defines the force regime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō PENST751, Module 2):**
> Force Regimes form the conceptual backbone linking **linear wave theory** (Module 1) to **Morison's equation and diffraction theory** (Modules 3 & 4). A clear understanding of regimes is mandatory before selecting an appropriate wave-load prediction model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Reed vs. The Boulder's Journey Through Wind:**
>
> Imagine wind blowing across a field of grass vs. blowing against a giant concrete wall.
>
> - A **slender reed** bends with the wind ŌåÆ *wind pushes it directly* (fluid must flow **around** it; viscous drag dominates).
> - A **medium tree trunk** feels both push and the inertia of air masses being shoved aside (a balance of drag and inertia).
> - A **massive concrete wall** creates pressure waves that wrap around it ŌĆö the wall "knows" the wave is coming from far away (wave scattering / diffraction dominates, the body is large relative to wavelength).
>
> The **reed Ōēł drag regime**, the **tree Ōēł inertia-drag regime**, and the **wall Ōēł diffraction regime**. Ocean engineers must recognize which "object" their offshore structure resembles to choose the right equation.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Density of seawater (Žü):** Ōēł **1025 kg/m┬│**
- **Kinematic viscosity of seawater (╬Į):** Ōēł **1.0 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬▓/s**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** = **9.81 m/s┬▓**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Governing Non-Dimensional Parameters

#### (a) KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number (KC)

$$
KC \;=\; \dfrac{U_{m}\,T}{D} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,a}{D}
$$

where $U_m$ = maximum horizontal orbital velocity, $T$ = wave period, $D$ = cylinder diameter, $a$ = horizontal water particle amplitude.

**Physical meaning:** Ratio of the fluid particle displacement length to the body's characteristic dimension. It quantifies *how far* a fluid particle travels during one wave cycle relative to the body's size.

#### (b) Diffraction Parameter (or Scattering Parameter)

$$
\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{D}{L} \qquad \text{or} \qquad k a
$$

where $L$ = wavelength, $k$ = wave number = $2\pi/L$.

**Physical meaning:** Body size relative to wavelength. Small $\lambda$ means the body "doesn't see" the wave coming; large $\lambda$ means the wave is scattered significantly by the body's presence.

### 2.2 The Three Primary Force Regimes

#### Regime I ŌĆö Drag-Dominated Regime
- **KC > 20** (high particle excursion vs. body diameter)
- Drag force $F_D$ dominates; inertia contribution negligible.
- Velocity-squared effects important; flow separation and vortex shedding dominate.
- **Typical applications:** Small-diameter cylinders in long-period waves, e.g., mooring chains, slender risers, subsea cables.

#### Regime II ŌĆö Inertia-Dominated Regime
- **KC < 3** (small particle excursion; body relatively large)
- Inertia force $F_I$ dominates; drag negligible.
- Potential-flow theory applicable; flow essentially attached.
- **Typical applications:** Large caissons, offshore wind monopiles in short-period waves.

#### Regime III ŌĆö Diffraction-Dominated Regime
- **D/L > 0.2** (body large compared to wavelength)
- Morison equation is **invalid**. The body alters the incoming wave field; scattered wave field is comparable to incident field.
- **Linear diffraction theory** (MacCamyŌĆōFuchs solution for vertical cylinders) must be used.
- **Typical applications:** Large offshore structures ŌĆö concrete gravity platforms, large ship hulls, offshore wind jackets.

#### Transition (DragŌĆōInertia) Regime
- **3 < KC < 20**: Both drag and inertia terms comparable.
- Both $C_D$ and $C_M$ must be evaluated from experiments; cross-flow and inline forces become coupled.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula for Force Regimes, formatted strictly to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number | $KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D} = \dfrac{2\pi a}{D}$ | dimensionless | Displacement-based KC |
| 2 | Diffraction Parameter | $\lambda = \dfrac{D}{L}$ | dimensionless | $L$ = wavelength |
| 3 | Wave Number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{L}$ | rad/m | Linear wave theory |
| 4 | Reynolds Number (in waves) | $Re = \dfrac{U_m D}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Flow separation indicator |
| 5 | Morison Drag Force (per unit length) | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\, u \vert u \vert$ | N/m | Velocity-squared |
| 6 | Morison Inertia Force (per unit length) | $F_I = \rho C_M \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,\dot{u}$ | N/m | Acceleration-proportional |
| 7 | Total Inline Force (Morison) | $F_x = F_D + F_I$ | N/m | Valid only when $\lambda < 0.2$ |
| 8 | MacCamyŌĆōFuchs Inertia Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{4}{(kD)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{K_1(kD)\,K_0(kD) + \ldots}{\ldots}$ (Bessel form) | dimensionless | $\to 2.0$ as $kD \to 0$ |
| 9 | Inertia Force Limit (potential flow, $a\to 0$) | $F_I \to \rho \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,\dot{u}$ | N/m | $C_M = 2.0$ |
| 10 | Stokes Drift Velocity (2nd order) | $\bar{u}_s = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{2} c\, k^{2} a \, e^{2kz}$ | m/s | Nonlinear mass transport |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore wind farms:** Predicting monopile fatigue loading ŌåÆ requires correct force regime classification.
- **Subsea pipeline design:** Drag-dominated regime dictates vortex-induced vibration (VIV) analysis.
- **FPSO/TLP hull design:** Diffraction-dominated regime requires expensive diffraction codes (WAMIT, NEMOH).
- **Coastal jetties and breakwaters:** Inertia-dominated regime, with added mass effects dominant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number from Orbital Kinematics

We start with the first-order linear wave (Airy) horizontal velocity at the free surface:

$$
u(x,t) \;=\; a\,\omega\,\cos(kx - \omega t)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the maximum velocity.**

The amplitude of the harmonic velocity is:

$$
U_m \;=\; a\,\omega
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the wave period.**

The angular frequency relates to period by:

$$
\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi}{T}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the fluid particle displacement amplitude $a$.**

Integrating velocity with respect to time:

$$
\xi(t) \;=\; \int u\,dt \;=\; -a\,\sin(kx - \omega t)
$$

Therefore the maximum horizontal displacement equals $a$ itself.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the dimensionless ratio of displacement to body size.**

The natural non-dimensional parameter comparing excursion to body diameter is:

$$
KC \;\equiv\; \dfrac{\text{displacement amplitude}}{\text{characteristic body size}} \;=\; \dfrac{a}{D/2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $a = U_m/\omega$ to get the velocityŌĆōperiod form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KC &= \dfrac{2a}{D} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{D}\cdot\dfrac{U_m}{\omega} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{D}\cdot U_m \cdot \dfrac{T}{2\pi} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{U_m\,T}{\pi D}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note on common convention:** Several textbooks (and the KTU reference) use a $\pi$ factor absorbed differently. The most common engineering definition used in offshore codes (API, DNV) is:

$$
\boxed{\,KC \;=\; \dfrac{U_m\,T}{D}\,}
$$

which differs from the displacement form by a factor of $\pi$. We will adopt the API/DNV convention throughout, but the student should be aware of both.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Regime Classification Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Regime Classification Logic:}\\
\text{Compute:}\quad & KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D}, \quad \lambda = \dfrac{D}{L} \\[6pt]
\text{If } \lambda > 0.2:\quad & \text{DIFFRACTION DOMINATED} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{MacCamyŌĆōFuchs} \\[4pt]
\text{If } \lambda \le 0.2 \text{ and } KC < 3:\quad & \text{INERTIA DOMINATED} \;\;\Rightarrow\; C_M \approx 2.0 \\[4pt]
\text{If } \lambda \le 0.2 \text{ and } 3 \le KC \le 20:\quad & \text{DRAGŌĆōINERTIA TRANSITION} \\[4pt]
\text{If } \lambda \le 0.2 \text{ and } KC > 20:\quad & \text{DRAG DOMINATED} \;\;\Rightarrow\; C_D \sim 1.0 \text{ to } 1.2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Why the Inertia Force Takes the Form $\rho C_M (\pi D^2/4)\dot{u}$

Consider an infinitely long vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ in a fluid of infinite extent, with the fluid otherwise at rest and accelerated horizontally at $\dot{u}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the unsteady Bernoulli equation for potential flow around the cylinder.**

The velocity potential for a cylinder in uniform acceleration is obtained from the doublet solution:

$$
\phi \;=\; -\dfrac{\dot{u}\,a^{2}}{r}\cos\theta \qquad (a = D/2)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate the pressure using unsteady Bernoulli.**

$$
p - p_\infty \;=\; -\rho\,\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \;=\; \rho\,\dot{u}\,\dfrac{a^{2}}{r}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate the pressure over the cylinder surface (projected area per unit length).**

The force per unit length is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_I &= -\int_0^{2\pi} p \, a\,\cos\theta \, d\theta \\[6pt]
&= -\rho\,\dot{u}\,a^{3}\int_0^{2\pi}\dfrac{\cos^{2}\theta}{a^{2}}\,d\theta \\[6pt]
&= -\rho\,\dot{u}\,a \int_0^{2\pi}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta \\[6pt]
&= -\rho\,\dot{u}\,a\cdot\pi \;=\; -\rho\,\dot{u}\,\pi a^{2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{a}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $a = D/2$:

$$
\boxed{\,F_I \;=\; \rho\,(\pi D^{2}/4)\,\dot{u}\,}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the inertia coefficient.**

In real (viscous) flow, the actual inertia force is:

$$
F_I^{\text{real}} \;=\; \rho\,C_M\,(\pi D^{2}/4)\,\dot{u}
$$

Comparing the inviscid result with the empirical form yields the **mass (inertia) coefficient $C_M$**, which is $\mathbf{2.0}$ for an ideal cylinder in potential flow (1.0 from displaced fluid + 1.0 from added-mass identity). For real flows, $C_M$ typically ranges from **1.5 to 2.5**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A vertical cylindrical pile of diameter $D = 1.5$ m is installed in water of depth $d = 20$ m. A wave of height $H = 4$ m and period $T = 8$ s propagates past it. Classify the force regime using linear (Airy) wave theory at the still-water level (SWL).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute wavelength from the dispersion relation.**

$$
L = \dfrac{g T^{2}}{2\pi}\tanh(kd) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{iterate to find } L
$$

Initial guess: $L_0 = gT^{2}/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 64 / 6.2832 \approx 99.92$ m, giving $kd = 2\pi(20)/99.92 \approx 1.257$, $\tanh(1.257) \approx 0.852$.

$$
L \approx 99.92 \times 0.852 \approx 85.13 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute wave number.**

$$
k = \dfrac{2\pi}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi}{85.13} \approx 0.0738 \text{ rad/m}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute surface particle velocity amplitude.**

$$
a = \dfrac{H}{2} = 2.0 \text{ m}, \qquad U_m = a\,\omega = 2.0 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{8} \approx 1.571 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number.**

$$
KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D} = \dfrac{1.571 \times 8}{1.5} \approx 8.38
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute diffraction parameter.**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{D}{L} = \dfrac{1.5}{85.13} \approx 0.0176 \;\;(\ll 0.2)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Classify.**

Since $3 < KC \approx 8.38 < 20$ and $\lambda \ll 0.2$, the flow lies in the **DragŌĆōInertia Transition Regime**. The full Morison equation with both $C_D$ and $C_M$ must be applied.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Force-Regime Classification Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wave-Structure Problem] --> B[Compute KC and lambda]
    B --> C{lambda greater than 0.2?}
    C -->|Yes| D[DIFFRACTION REGIME]
    D --> D1[Use MacCamy-Fuchs or BEM solver]
    C -->|No| E{KC range check}
    E -->|KC less than 3| F[INERTIA REGIME]
    F --> F1[C_M around 2.0 dominates]
    E -->|3 to 20| G[DRAG-INERTIA TRANSITION]
    G --> G1[Full Morison equation required]
    E -->|KC greater than 20| H[DRAG REGIME]
    H --> H1[C_D dominates, VIV possible]
    D1 --> Z[End]
    F1 --> Z
    G1 --> Z
    H1 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Force-Regime Map (Conceptual 2D Parameter Space)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[KC axis horizontal] --> P2[lambda axis vertical]
    subgraph MAP
        DRAG[Drag Dominated - Top right]
        TRANS[Transition - Middle band]
        INERT[Inertia Dominated - Bottom left]
        DIFF[Diffraction Dominated - Upper region]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Force-Regime Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Wave Input H T d] --> MOD[Linear Wave Kinematics Module]
    BODY[Body Geometry D Lsub] --> MOD
    MOD --> KCOUT[Compute KC]
    MOD --> LAMOUT[Compute lambda]
    KCOUT --> REG[Regime Classifier]
    LAMOUT --> REG
    REG --> OUT1[Regime Label]
    REG --> OUT2[Recommended Equation Morison or Diffraction]
    REG --> OUT3[Coefficient Guidance C_D and C_M]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
*Define the KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number and state its physical significance in classifying wave force regimes.*

**Model Answer:**
The KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number is defined as $KC = U_m T / D$, the ratio of the horizontal distance a fluid particle travels in one wave cycle to the body's characteristic diameter. Physically, it measures how far the fluid moves relative to the body. A high $KC$ (large displacement) implies the body experiences strong flow separation ŌåÆ **drag-dominated regime**. A low $KC$ (small displacement) implies attached potential flow ŌåÆ **inertia-dominated regime**. The transition band is typically $3 < KC < 20$.

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
*Why is the Morison equation invalid for large offshore structures such as concrete gravity platforms?*

**Model Answer:**
The Morison equation assumes that the wave field is *undisturbed* by the presence of the body ŌĆö i.e., it sums drag (local velocity-squared) and inertia (local acceleration) forces using the *undisturbed* incident wave kinematics. This assumption fails when the body's diffraction parameter $D/L > 0.2$, because the body scatters the incoming wave field significantly. The incident and scattered wave fields become comparable in magnitude, and the body experiences an additional **diffraction force** that Morison's equation cannot capture. For such large bodies, **linear diffraction theory** (MacCamyŌĆōFuchs for vertical cylinders, or full boundary-element methods) must be employed.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number from first-order wave kinematics. Show both the velocity-period form and the displacement-diameter form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a vertical circular cylinder of diameter $D = 2.0$ m in water depth $d = 15$ m subjected to a wave of height $H = 3$ m and period $T = 7$ s, classify the force regime at the still-water level using linear wave theory. Use $C_D = 1.2$ and $C_M = 2.0$, and compute the maximum horizontal force per unit length on the cylinder. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of KC**

Step 1 ŌĆö From linear (Airy) wave theory, the horizontal velocity at SWL is:
$$
u(t) = a\,\omega\,\cos(\omega t - kx)
$$

Step 2 ŌĆö The maximum velocity amplitude is $U_m = a\omega = 2\pi a / T$.

Step 3 ŌĆö The fluid particle displacement amplitude is found by integrating velocity:
$$
\xi(t) = \int u\,dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \xi_{\max} = a
$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Define KC as the ratio of total displacement per cycle to body diameter:
$$
KC_{\text{disp}} = \dfrac{2\,\xi_{\max}}{D} = \dfrac{2a}{D}
$$

Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $a = U_m T / (2\pi)$:
$$
KC_{\text{disp}} = \dfrac{2}{D}\cdot\dfrac{U_m T}{2\pi} = \dfrac{U_m T}{\pi D}
$$

Step 6 ŌĆö The standard engineering (API/DNV) form absorbs the $\pi$ by redefining KC over a half-cycle-equivalent distance, giving the widely used form:
$$
KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Classification and Force Computation**

Step 1 ŌĆö Wavelength from dispersion: iterate with $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = 75.18$ m, $kd \approx 1.254$, $\tanh(kd) \approx 0.851$:
$$
L \approx 75.18 \times 0.851 \approx 64.0 \text{ m}
$$

Step 2 ŌĆö $k = 2\pi/L \approx 0.0982$ rad/m; $\omega = 2\pi/7 \approx 0.898$ rad/s.

Step 3 ŌĆö $a = H/2 = 1.5$ m; $U_m = a\omega = 1.5 \times 0.898 = 1.347$ m/s.

Step 4 ŌĆö Compute KC:
$$
KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D} = \dfrac{1.347 \times 7}{2.0} \approx 4.71
$$

Step 5 ŌĆö Compute diffraction parameter:
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{D}{L} = \dfrac{2.0}{64.0} \approx 0.031
$$

Step 6 ŌĆö Since $\lambda \ll 0.2$ and $3 < KC = 4.71 < 20$: **DragŌĆōInertia Transition Regime**. Morison equation with both terms required.

Step 7 ŌĆö Maximum acceleration at SWL: $\dot{u}_{\max} = a\omega^2 = 1.5 \times 0.898^2 \approx 1.210$ m/s┬▓.

Step 8 ŌĆö Compute drag force per unit length (occurs when $u = U_m$):
$$
F_{D,\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\,U_m^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 \times (1.347)^{2} \approx 2232 \text{ N/m}
$$

Step 9 ŌĆö Compute inertia force per unit length (occurs when $\dot{u} = \dot{u}_{\max}$):
$$
F_{I,\max} = \rho C_M \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,\dot{u}_{\max} = 1025 \times 2.0 \times \tfrac{\pi(2.0)^{2}}{4} \times 1.210 \approx 7790 \text{ N/m}
$$

Step 10 ŌĆö Note that drag and inertia maxima are 90┬░ out of phase, so they are **not added directly** for total maximum. However, the inertia term clearly dominates by a factor of ~3.5, confirming that this case lies near the **inertia side** of the transition regime.

---

#### **Question B-B (Internal Choice Alternative).** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Pattern, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain, with sketches, the three primary force regimes ŌĆö drag, inertia, and diffraction ŌĆö for a vertical cylinder in regular waves. Identify the governing non-dimensional parameters and their typical threshold values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A large offshore concrete gravity base structure has an effective cylindrical cross-section of diameter $D = 30$ m in water depth $d = 50$ m. A storm wave has $H = 12$ m and $T = 14$ s. Show that the Morison equation is **not applicable**, and outline the appropriate alternative theoretical framework. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Three Force Regimes**

[Student should draw three labelled sketches showing cylinder size relative to wavelength, and discuss:]
1. **Drag-dominated** ($KC > 20$): slender body, large displacement, vortex shedding and flow separation, $C_D$ dominates.
2. **Inertia-dominated** ($KC < 3$): relatively larger body, attached flow, potential-flow solution with $C_M = 2.0$.
3. **Diffraction-dominated** ($D/L > 0.2$): body comparable to wavelength, wave scattering, Morison invalid ŌåÆ MacCamyŌĆōFuchs or BEM.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Applicability Check**

Step 1 ŌĆö Wavelength: $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 196 / 6.2832 \approx 306$ m; $kd = 2\pi(50)/306 \approx 1.027$, $\tanh(kd) \approx 0.773$. So $L \approx 306 \times 0.773 \approx 236.6$ m.

Step 2 ŌĆö Diffraction parameter:
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{D}{L} = \dfrac{30}{236.6} \approx 0.127
$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Although $\lambda \approx 0.127 < 0.2$, it is close to the boundary. Computing $U_m$ and $KC$:
$$
a = 6 \text{ m},\;\; \omega = 2\pi/14 \approx 0.449 \text{ rad/s},\;\; U_m = 2.694 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
KC = \dfrac{U_m T}{D} = \dfrac{2.694 \times 14}{30} \approx 1.26 \;\;(< 3,\; \text{inertia side})
$$

Step 4 ŌĆö However, for such a **massive structure**, the wave field is significantly disturbed even at $\lambda = 0.127$. The wave length is only ~8 times the diameter. A standard engineering practice (API RP 2A) prescribes that **Morison is invalid** whenever $D/L > 0.05$ for conservative design of large structures. Hence we must use **linear diffraction theory**.

Step 5 ŌĆö Appropriate alternative: **MacCamyŌĆōFuchs solution** for the inertia force on a large vertical cylinder:
$$
F_I = \rho g \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,H\,A(kD)
$$
where $A(kD)$ is a Bessel-function-based correction factor that equals $1.0$ as $kD \to 0$ and decays for large $kD$. For full 3D wave-structure interaction (added mass, damping, run-up), the recommended tool is a **Boundary Element Method (BEM)** code such as **WAMIT, NEMOH, or HydroSTAR**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls ŌĆö Force Regimes:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the two KC conventions** ŌĆö Some texts use $KC = U_m T / (\pi D)$, others $KC = U_m T / D$. State explicitly which convention you are using; KTU examiners expect the API/DNV form $KC = U_m T / D$.
> 2. **Forgetting the phase difference** between drag (velocity-squared, max at $u = U_m$) and inertia (acceleration, max at $\dot{u} = \dot{u}_{\max}$). They are 90┬░ out of phase in linear waves. Do **not** simply add their maxima to get the "maximum total force."
> 3. **Applying Morison to large bodies.** A common error is using Morison for $D > 5$ŌĆō$10$ m cylinders. Always check $D/L$ first.
> 4. **Confusing $C_D$ and $C_M$ with $C_f$ and $C_a$.** $C_f$ is a friction coefficient (boundary layer); $C_a = C_M - 1$ is the added-mass coefficient only.
> 5. **Using surface values throughout the depth.** Wave kinematics decay with depth (via $\cosh(kz)/\cosh(kd)$). For tall cylinders, integrate the forces over depth, do not assume surface values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Force Regimes** classify wave loads into drag, inertia, and diffraction regimes based on **KC** and **D/L**.
- [x] **KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number:** $KC = U_m T / D$ (API/DNV form).
- [x] **Diffraction Parameter:** $\lambda = D / L$.
- [x] **Drag-dominated** if $KC > 20$, $\lambda < 0.2$; use $C_D \sim 1.0$ŌĆō$1.2$.
- [x] **Inertia-dominated** if $KC < 3$, $\lambda < 0.2$; $C_M \approx 2.0$ in potential flow.
- [x] **Transition regime** $3 < KC < 20$; full Morison with both terms.
- [x] **Diffraction-dominated** if $\lambda > 0.2$; Morison invalid ŌåÆ MacCamyŌĆōFuchs or BEM.
- [x] **Inertia force formula** (per unit length): $F_I = \rho C_M (\pi D^2/4)\,\dot{u}$.
- [x] **Drag force formula** (per unit length): $F_D = (1/2)\rho C_D D\, u \vert u \vert$.
- [x] Drag and inertia maxima are **90┬░ out of phase** in linear waves.
- [x] For tall cylinders, integrate $u(z)$ and $\dot{u}(z)$ over depth using $\cosh(kz)/\cosh(kd)$ factors.
- [x] KTU examiners frequently test regime classification by giving you numbers ŌĆö practice identifying $KC$ and $\lambda$ first, before plugging into Morison.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morison Equation - Fixed Cylinder in Waves
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Morison Equation - Fixed Cylinder in Waves

# ­¤īŖ Morison Equation ŌĆö Fixed Cylinder in Waves

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Morison Equation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Morison Equation** is an empirically-derived semi-analytical formulation used to estimate the horizontal wave force exerted by fluid flow on a *fixed, slender, vertical cylindrical member* whose diameter **D** is small compared to the wavelength **╬╗** (typically **D/╬╗ < 0.2**). The total inline force is the *linear superposition* of an **inertia force** (proportional to fluid acceleration) and a **drag force** (proportional to the square of fluid velocity).

Mathematically, the **force per unit length** on a fixed vertical cylinder in waves is:

$$f_x(z,t) = f_I(z,t) + f_D(z,t)$$

where:
- $f_I$ = Inertia force per unit length (N/m)
- $f_D$ = Drag force per unit length (N/m)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Seaweed-on-a-Pole Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** Imagine a thin wooden pole stuck vertically into a flowing river. As the water rushes past, you feel *two distinct pushes*:
> 1. **The "Push-from-Front" (Drag):** A steady push because the water physically hits and slides around the pole ŌĆö like wind hitting a flagpole. This depends on **how fast** the water is moving.
> 2. **The "Push-from-Acceleration" (Inertia):** A *jerky* push when the water suddenly changes speed (accelerates or decelerates) ŌĆö like the lurch you feel in a car when the driver hits the gas. This depends on **how quickly the water's speed is changing**.
>
> The Morison Equation simply **adds these two pushes together** to give the total horizontal force on the cylinder at any instant.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seawater density | **Žü** | **1025 kg/m┬│** | KTU standard value |
| Kinematic viscosity of seawater | ╬Į | **1.0 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬▓/s** | At 20┬░C |
| Drag Coefficient | $C_D$ | **0.6 ŌĆō 1.2** (smooth cyl.) | Depends on Re & KC |
| Inertia Coefficient | $C_M$ | **1.2 ŌĆō 2.0** (smooth cyl.) | Depends on $C_D$, Re & KC |
| Added Mass Coefficient | $C_a$ | $C_M - 1$ | Theoretical: $C_a = 1$ for circular cyl. |
| Diameter | D | meters | Cylinder cross-section |
| Wavelength | ╬╗ | meters | For validity: $D/\lambda < 0.2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö When to use Morison Equation:**
> The Morison Equation is **valid only when the cylinder does not significantly disturb the incoming wave field**. This happens when the diameter **D** is much smaller than the wavelength **╬╗**. The classical validity limit is:
>
> $$\frac{D}{\lambda} \leq 0.2 \quad \text{(Diffraction effects are negligible)}$$
>
> For larger structures (like gravity-based oil platforms), **Diffraction Theory** must be used instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Reasoning ŌĆö Why Two Force Components?

The Morison Equation is built on the following physical logic:

1. **The cylinder is slender and rigid** ŌĆö it neither flexes with the flow nor breaks the wave.
2. **The flow field around the cylinder can be decomposed** into:
   - A *potential flow* (inviscid, irrotational) component that gives the inertia effect.
   - A *viscous, separated flow* (boundary layer + wake) component that gives the drag effect.
3. **Superposition principle applies** because the two effects are considered *independent* and *linearly additive* (this is an empirical assumption, not a strict physical truth).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Breakdown of Each Component

#### (a) Drag Force Component ŌĆö $f_D$

The drag force arises from:
- **Pressure drag** (form drag) due to flow separation at the rear of the cylinder.
- **Viscous (skin friction) drag** along the cylinder surface.

$$f_D(z,t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, u(z,t)\,\vert u(z,t) \vert$$

- The term $u \vert u \vert$ (not $u^2$) preserves the **sign of velocity** so that the drag always pushes in the *direction of flow*.
- $C_D$ is **dimensionless** and is determined empirically.
- This is a **non-linear** term in velocity ŌåÆ makes analytical solutions hard.

#### (b) Inertia Force Component ŌĆö $f_I$

The inertia force arises from:
- The **pressure gradient** in the undisturbed flow (Froude-Krylov force).
- The **added mass effect** ŌĆö the cylinder must also accelerate a *slug of fluid* surrounding it.

$$f_I(z,t) = \rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\, \frac{\partial u(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

- $C_M$ is the **inertia coefficient** (also called the force coefficient).
- The area $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ is the **cross-sectional area** of the cylinder.
- This is a **linear** term in acceleration ŌåÆ easier to integrate.

### 2.3 The Morison Equation ŌĆö Complete Form

$$\boxed{f_x(z,t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, u\,\vert u\vert \;+\; \rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\, \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Check (KTU Favourite Trick Question):**
> - $f_x$ ŌåÆ N/m (force per unit length)
> - $\rho$ ŌåÆ kg/m┬│
> - $C_D, C_M$ ŌåÆ dimensionless
> - $D$ ŌåÆ m
> - $u$ ŌåÆ m/s
> - $\partial u / \partial t$ ŌåÆ m/s┬▓
> - Verification: $\rho \cdot D \cdot u^2$ ŌåÆ (kg/m┬│)(m)(m┬▓/s┬▓) = kg┬Ęm/s┬▓/m = **N/m** Ō£ō

### 2.4 The Coefficients $C_D$ and $C_M$ ŌĆö What Controls Them?

Two **dimensionless parameters** govern the values of $C_D$ and $C_M$:

#### (i) Reynolds Number (Re) ŌĆö Inertial vs. Viscous Flow

$$Re = \frac{u_{\max}\, D}{\nu}$$

- $u_{\max}$ = maximum horizontal particle velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = cylinder diameter (m)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of fluid (m┬▓/s)
- For ocean engineering, $Re \sim 10^4$ to $10^6$ ŌåÆ **turbulent flow regime**

#### (ii) KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter Number (KC) ŌĆö Oscillatory vs. Steady Flow

$$KC = \frac{u_{\max}\, T}{D}$$

- $T$ = wave period (s)
- $KC$ measures the **ratio of the oscillatory displacement amplitude to the cylinder diameter**.
- For a fixed cylinder in regular waves, the **particle motion** is oscillatory; the higher the KC, the more the flow separates.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö it covers 90% of the numerical questions on Morison Equation.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_x = f_I + f_D$ | Total inline force (per unit length) | Standard starting equation |
| 2 | $f_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u \vert u\vert$ | Drag force per unit length | Velocity-squared term |
| 3 | $f_I = \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ | Inertia force per unit length | Linear in acceleration |
| 4 | $F = \int_0^{\eta} f_x(z,t)\, dz$ | Total force on cylinder (integrate from seabed to instantaneous water surface) | Final answer for total force |
| 5 | $Re = \frac{u_{\max} D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | For selecting $C_D, C_M$ |
| 6 | $KC = \frac{u_{\max} T}{D}$ | KeuleganŌĆōCarpenter number | For selecting $C_D, C_M$ |
| 7 | $C_M = 1 + C_a$ | $C_a$ = added mass coefficient | Theoretical $C_a = 1$ for circular cylinder |
| 8 | $\frac{D}{\lambda} \leq 0.2$ | Validity limit for Morison | **Always quote this** in derivations |
| 9 | $u = \frac{H g T}{2 L} \cdot \frac{\cosh kz}{\cosh kd} \cos(\omega t - kx)$ | Linear wave (Airy) velocity | Required for $\partial u/\partial t$ |
| 10 | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{H g \pi}{L} \cdot \frac{\cosh kz}{\cosh kd} \sin(\omega t - kx)$ | Linear wave acceleration | Used in inertia term |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Morison Equation is the **backbone of offshore structural design** for:
- **Jacket platforms** (oil & gas) ŌĆö hundreds of slender tubular members.
- **Offshore wind turbine monopiles** ŌĆö modern renewables engineering.
- **Bridge piers** in coastal zones ŌĆö wave force estimation.
- **Mooring lines and risers** in deep water.
- **Submarine pipelines** laid on the seabed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this so critical for KTU?**
> Almost every problem in the Ocean Wave Hydrodynamics module 2 concludes with: *"Calculate the total wave force on the cylinder"* ŌĆö and this is **always solved using the Morison Equation**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Force on a Fixed Vertical Cylinder

We will derive the **total horizontal wave force** on a vertical cylinder of diameter **D** extending from the seabed ($z = -d$) to the still water level ($z = 0$), in a regular Airy wave.

#### Step 1: Start with the Morison Equation (Force per Unit Length)

$$f_x(z,t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, u(z,t)\,\vert u(z,t)\vert \;+\; \rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\, \frac{\partial u(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute the Airy (Linear) Wave Kinematics

For a linear Airy wave of height **H**, period **T**, water depth **d**, the horizontal particle velocity at depth **z** is:

$$u(z,t) = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

The horizontal particle acceleration is the time derivative of velocity:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

#### Step 3: Write the Inertia Component

Substituting $\partial u / \partial t$ into the inertia term:

$$f_I(z,t) = \rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \left[ -\frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx) \right]$$

Simplifying:

$$f_I(z,t) = -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

#### Step 4: Write the Drag Component

The drag term contains $u \vert u \vert$ which is **non-sinusoidal** (it produces higher harmonics when expanded):

$$f_D(z,t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, u(z,t) \cdot \vert u(z,t) \vert$$

This non-linearity means we must compute **RMS or peak values** rather than relying on simple sinusoidal integration.

#### Step 5: Integrate to Get Total Force

The total inline force is obtained by integrating $f_x$ from the seabed ($z = -d$) to the instantaneous free surface ($z = \eta(t)$). For a *fixed cylinder whose top is below the SWL*, we integrate to $z = 0$.

$$F_x(t) = \int_{-d}^{0} f_x(z,t)\, dz$$

##### Inertia Force Integral

$$\begin{aligned}
F_I(t) &= \int_{-d}^{0} f_I(z,t)\, dz \\[4pt]
&= -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 T^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx) \cdot \int_{-d}^{0} \cosh k(d+z)\, dz
\end{aligned}$$

Evaluate the integral using $\int \cosh(k(d+z))\, dz = \frac{1}{k}\sinh k(d+z)$:

$$\int_{-d}^{0} \cosh k(d+z)\, dz = \frac{1}{k}\left[ \sinh k(d+0) - \sinh k(d-d) \right] = \frac{\sinh kd}{k}$$

Substitute back:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_I(t) &= -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 T^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sinh kd} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx) \cdot \frac{\sinh kd}{k} \\[4pt]
&= -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 k T^2} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ and $T = 2\pi / \omega$:

$$\boxed{F_I(t) = -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^2 D^2 H}{4} \cdot \frac{\omega^2}{k} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx) \cdot \frac{1}{?}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Compact Form for Inertia Force (KTU 2024 ESE expected form):**
>
> $$F_I(t) = -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{1}{k} \cdot \tanh kd \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

This can be simplified further using dispersion $L = gT^2 \tanh kd / (2\pi)$:

$$F_I(t) = -\rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 T^2 k} \cdot \tanh kd \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$$

The **maximum inertia force** (peak) occurs when $\sin(\omega t - kx) = \pm 1$:

$$\boxed{F_{I,\max} = \rho\, C_M\, \frac{\pi^3 D^2 H}{2 T^2 k} \cdot \tanh kd}$$

##### Drag Force Integral (RMS Value)

Since $u \vert u \vert$ produces a non-sinusoidal force, we work with **maximum amplitude** $u_{\max}$ at $z = 0$:

$$u_{\max}(z) = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd}$$

The peak drag force at depth z is:

$$f_{D,\max}(z) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, u_{\max}^2(z) = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, \left[ \frac{\pi H}{T} \right]^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{\cosh k(d+z)}{\sinh kd} \right]^2$$

The total peak drag force:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{D,\max} &= \int_{-d}^{0} f_{D,\max}(z)\, dz \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, \left[ \frac{\pi H}{T \sinh kd} \right]^2 \cdot \int_{-d}^{0} \cosh^2 k(d+z)\, dz
\end{aligned}$$

Using $\int \cosh^2 x\, dx = \frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sinh 2x}{4}$:

$$\int_{-d}^{0} \cosh^2 k(d+z)\, dz = \left[ \frac{(d+z)}{2} + \frac{\sinh 2k(d+z)}{4k} \right]_{-d}^{0} = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{\sinh 2kd}{4k}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{F_{D,\max} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, \left[ \frac{\pi H}{T} \right]^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{d}{2\sinh^2 kd} + \frac{\sinh 2kd}{4k \sinh^2 kd} \right]}$$

This simplifies to:

$$F_{D,\max} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_D\, D\, \left[ \frac{\pi H}{T} \right]^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{kd \cdot \sinh kd + \cosh kd}{2 k \sinh^2 kd} \right] \cdot \text{(approximation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For deep water** ($kd \to \infty$): the drag term simplifies because $\cosh kd \approx \sinh kd \approx \frac{1}{2} e^{kd}$, and the result is dominated by surface effects.
> **For shallow water** ($kd \to 0$): the velocity becomes nearly uniform with depth, simplifying the integration significantly.

#### Step 6: Total Force Superposition

$$\boxed{F_{total}(t) = F_I(t) + F_D(t)}$$

The **maximum total force** is NOT simply the sum of maxima because the two components are 90┬░ out of phase (one is sine, the other is squared-cosine).

$$F_{total,\max} \neq F_{I,\max} + F_{D,\max}$$

Instead:

$$\boxed{F_{total,\max} = \sqrt{F_{I,\max}^2 + F_{D,\max}^2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often add the peak values directly. This is **WRONG** because the inertia and drag components reach their peaks at *different times* during the wave cycle. The correct combination uses the **square root of the sum of squares** when the two are 90┬░ out of phase.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When Does Drag Dominate vs. Inertia?

The relative importance of drag and inertia is governed by the ratio:

$$\frac{F_{D,\max}}{F_{I,\max}} = \frac{\pi H}{2 D \cdot \tanh kd} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_M} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

A simpler form using the **Keulegan-Carpenter number**:

$$\frac{F_D}{F_I} \propto \frac{KC}{\pi} \cdot \frac{C_D}{C_M}$$

| Regime | KC Range | Dominant Force | Engineering Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very small KC | KC < 3 | **Inertia dominant** | Small members, large waves, deep water |
| Moderate KC | 3 < KC < 30 | Both comparable | Typical offshore jacket members |
| Large KC | KC > 30 | **Drag dominant** | Large diameter piles, low-frequency swell |
