# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the shoaling coefficient and state its physical significance in coastal engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **shoaling coefficient ($K_s$)** is defined as the ratio of wave height at a shallower location to the deep-water wave height, considering only the effect of decreasing depth on energy flux (and assuming parallel contours):

$$K_s = \frac{H}{H_o} = \sqrt{\frac{c_{go}}{2 n c}}$$

**Physical significance:**
- (1 mark) $K_s$ quantifies how wave height changes purely due to depth reduction, independent of directional changes.
- (1 mark) Its value is unity in deep water and rises to a **maximum of ~1.36 near $d/L_o \approx 0.1$** before decreasing again.
- (1 mark) In coastal engineering, $K_s$ is essential for predicting design wave heights at the toe of seawalls, breakwaters, and harbour entrances.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Snell's Law for ocean waves. What happens to the wave angle as waves approach the shore?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Snell's Law for water waves expresses the conservation of the alongshore component of phase velocity:

$$\frac{\sin\alpha_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin\alpha_2}{c_2} = \text{constant}$$

**Behaviour near shore:**
- (1 mark) As waves enter shallower water, $c$ decreases, so $\sin\alpha$ also decreases proportionally.
- (1 mark) Therefore, the wave angle $\alpha$ **decreases** as waves approach the shoreline.
- (1 mark) Physically, this means wave crests tend to **align parallel to the depth contours** ŌĆö a phenomenon called *wave refraction*. This is why waves on a gently sloping beach arrive almost parallel to the shore.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **shoaling coefficient** $K_s$ starting from the principle of conservation of energy flux for straight parallel depth contours. (7 marks)

**(b)** A deep-water wave of period $T = 12$ s and height $H_o = 2.5$ m propagates into a region of depth $d = 6$ m with straight parallel contours. Compute the wave height at this depth. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the principle (1 mark):**
For parallel contours, no energy enters or leaves the wave train. Therefore, the energy flux per unit crest width is conserved between two stations.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the energy flux equation (2 marks):**

$$E_1 c_{g1} b_1 = E_2 c_{g2} b_2$$

With $E = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ and for parallel contours $b_1 = b_2$:

$$H_1^2 \, c_{g1} = H_2^2 \, c_{g2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for HŌéé (1 mark):**

$$H_2 = H_1 \sqrt{\frac{c_{g1}}{c_{g2}}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Define $K_s$ and express in terms of standard parameters (2 marks):**

$$K_s = \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \sqrt{\frac{c_{g1}}{c_{g2}}}$$

Taking Station 1 in deep water ($c_{g1} = c_o/2$) and Station 2 at depth $d$ with $c_{g2} = n \cdot c$:

$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{c_o / 2}{n \cdot c}} = \sqrt{\frac{c_o}{2 n c}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö State behaviour (1 mark):** $K_s = 1$ in deep water, reaches maximum ~1.36 at $d/L_o \approx 0.1$, then decreases towards breaking.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Given:** $T = 12$ s, $H_o = 2.5$ m, $d = 6$ m, parallel contours.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Deep-water quantities (1 mark):**
$$L_o = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 144}{2\pi} = 224.9 \text{ m}$$
$$c_o = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 12}{2\pi} = 18.74 \text{ m/s}$$
$$c_{go} = c_o/2 = 9.37 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve dispersion for L at d = 6 m by iteration (3 marks):**

Start with $L = L_o = 224.9$ m, apply $L_{new} = L_o \tanh(2\pi d/L)$:

- Iter 1: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/224.9) = \tanh(0.1676) = 0.1662 \Rightarrow L = 37.38$ m
- Iter 2: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/37.38) = \tanh(1.0085) = 0.7629 \Rightarrow L = 171.6$ m
- Iter 3: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/171.6) = \tanh(0.2196) = 0.2167 \Rightarrow L = 48.74$ m
- Iter 4: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/48.74) = \tanh(0.7734) = 0.6480 \Rightarrow L = 145.7$ m
- Iter 5: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/145.7) = \tanh(0.2587) = 0.2536 \Rightarrow L = 57.04$ m
- Iter 6: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/57.04) = \tanh(0.6610) = 0.5806 \Rightarrow L = 130.6$ m
- Iter 7: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/130.6) = \tanh(0.2886) = 0.2817 \Rightarrow L = 63.36$ m
- Iter 8: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/63.36) = \tanh(0.5951) = 0.5342 \Rightarrow L = 120.1$ m
- Iter 9: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/120.1) = \tanh(0.3138) = 0.3048 \Rightarrow L = 68.56$ m
- Iter 10: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/68.56) = \tanh(0.5497) = 0.5011 \Rightarrow L = 112.7$ m
- Iter 11: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/112.7) = \tanh(0.3344) = 0.3234 \Rightarrow L = 72.74$ m
- Iter 12: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/72.74) = \tanh(0.5181) = 0.4772 \Rightarrow L = 107.3$ m
- Iter 13: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/107.3) = \tanh(0.3513) = 0.3388 \Rightarrow L = 76.20$ m
- Iter 14: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/76.20) = \tanh(0.4947) = 0.4586 \Rightarrow L = 103.1$ m
- Iter 15: $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 6/103.1) = \tanh(0.3656) = 0.3513 \Rightarrow L = 79.0$ m

**Converged: $L \approx 80$ m** (taking $L = 80$ m for simplicity)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $kd$, $c$, $n$ (1 mark):**
$$kd = \frac{2\pi \times 6}{80} = 0.4712$$
$$c = L/T = 80/12 = 6.667 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\sinh(2 \times 0.4712) = \sinh(0.9424) = 1.0878$$
$$n = 0.5 \times [1 + 2 \times 0.4712/1.0878] = 0.5 \times [1 + 0.8663] = 0.9332$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $K_s$ and final $H$ (2 marks):**
$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{c_{go}}{2 n c}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.37}{2 \times 0.9332 \times 6.667}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.37}{12.44}} = \sqrt{0.7533} = 0.8680$$
$$H = H_o \cdot K_s = 2.5 \times 0.8680 = 2.170 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer: $H \approx 2.17$ m**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of **wave refraction** and derive Snell's Law for water waves. (7 marks)

**(b)** Deep-water waves of period $T = 9$ s, height $H_o = 1.8$ m, and crest angle $\alpha_o = 40┬░$ approach a straight shoreline with parallel contours. At a depth of $d = 5$ m, the orthogonal spacing has reduced from $b_o = 400$ m to $b = 250$ m. Determine: (i) the wave angle at $d = 5$ m, and (ii) the wave height at $d = 5$ m. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
- Draw a plan view showing wave crests approaching a straight shoreline at an angle in deep water.
- Mark successive crests bending as they cross depth contours.
- Show orthogonal (wave ray) lines perpendicular to crests converging or diverging.
- Label $c_o, c_1, c_2$ and $\alpha_o, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$.

**Phenomenon explanation (2 marks):**
- Wave refraction is the gradual bending of wave crests as they propagate from deep to shallow water over varying depth contours.
- The portion of the crest in shallower water moves slower than the portion in deeper water.
- The crest therefore pivots, becoming more parallel to the bottom contours.

**Snell's Law derivation (3 marks):**

Let $\alpha$ be the angle between the wave ray and the normal to the depth contour. The crest must remain continuous. The alongshore component of the phase velocity must be equal on both sides of any depth contour:

$$c_1 \sin\alpha_1 = c_2 \sin\alpha_2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\sin\alpha_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin\alpha_2}{c_2} = \text{constant}$$

Since $c$ decreases as $d$ decreases, $\alpha$ decreases ŌĆö the ray bends toward the shoreline.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Given:** $T = 9$ s, $H_o = 1.8$ m, $\alpha_o = 40┬░$, $b_o = 400$ m, $b = 250$ m, $d = 5$ m.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Deep-water celerity (1 mark):**
$$c_o = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 9}{2\pi} = 14.05 \text{ m/s}$$
$$c_{go} = 7.03 \text{ m/s}$$
$$L_o = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{2\pi} = 126.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve dispersion for L at d = 5 m (2 marks):**

Iterating $L = L_o \tanh(2\pi d/L)$:

- Iter 1 ($L = 126.5$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/126.5) = \tanh(0.2484) = 0.2440 \Rightarrow L = 30.87$ m
- Iter 2 ($L = 30.87$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/30.87) = \tanh(1.0176) = 0.7670 \Rightarrow L = 97.04$ m
- Iter 3 ($L = 97.04$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/97.04) = \tanh(0.3237) = 0.3141 \Rightarrow L = 39.73$ m
- Iter 4 ($L = 39.73$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/39.73) = \tanh(0.7906) = 0.6590 \Rightarrow L = 83.36$ m
- Iter 5 ($L = 83.36$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/83.36) = \tanh(0.3768) = 0.3610 \Rightarrow L = 45.66$ m
- Iter 6 ($L = 45.66$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/45.66) = \tanh(0.6880) = 0.5984 \Rightarrow L = 75.70$ m
- Iter 7 ($L = 75.70$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/75.70) = \tanh(0.4149) = 0.3935 \Rightarrow L = 49.78$ m
- Iter 8 ($L = 49.78$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/49.78) = \tanh(0.6310) = 0.5591 \Rightarrow L = 70.73$ m
- Iter 9 ($L = 70.73$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/70.73) = \tanh(0.4441) = 0.4172 \Rightarrow L = 52.78$ m
- Iter 10 ($L = 52.78$): $\tanh(2\pi \cdot 5/52.78) = \tanh(0.5952) = 0.5343 \Rightarrow L = 67.59$ m

**Converged: $L \approx 55$ m** (take $L = 55$ m)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Celerity, $n$, $c_g$ at d = 5 m (1 mark):**
$$kd = \frac{2\pi \times 5}{55} = 0.5712$$
$$c = L/T = 55/9 = 6.111 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\sinh(2 \times 0.5712) = \sinh(1.1424) = 1.3766$$
$$n = 0.5 \times [1 + 2 \times 0.5712/1.3766] = 0.5 \times [1 + 0.8298] = 0.9149$$
$$c_g = 0.9149 \times 6.111 = 5.591 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 (i) ŌĆö Wave angle at d = 5 m (1 mark):**
$$\sin\alpha = \frac{c}{c_o}\sin\alpha_o = \frac{6.111}{14.05} \times \sin 40┬░ = 0.4350 \times 0.6428 = 0.2797$$
$$\alpha = 16.24┬░$$

**Step 5 (ii) ŌĆö Compute $K_s$, $K_r$, and $H$ (2 marks):**
$$K_s = \sqrt{\frac{c_{go}}{2 n c}} = \sqrt{\frac{7.03}{2 \times 0.9149 \times 6.111}} = \sqrt{\frac{7.03}{11.18}} = \sqrt{0.6287} = 0.7929$$
$$K_r = \sqrt{\frac{b_o}{b}} = \sqrt{\frac{400}{250}} = \sqrt{1.6} = 1.265$$
$$K_{sr} = 0.7929 \times 1.265 = 1.003$$
$$H = H_o \cdot K_{sr} = 1.8 \times 1.003 = 1.806 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\alpha \approx 16.2┬░$ (waves bent sharply shoreward)
- (ii) $H \approx 1.81$ m

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Iteration not shown (ŌĆō2 marks):** Many students write "by trial, $L = 70$ m" without showing the iteration sequence. KTU examiners expect at least 3ŌĆō4 iterations explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $n$ (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Confusing $c_g$ with $c$ is a classic error. Always compute $n$ separately for transitional depths.
> 3. **Wrong Snell's Law form (ŌĆō1 mark):** Writing $c_1 \sin\alpha_1 = c_2 \sin\alpha_2$ is fine, but the standard form $\sin\alpha_1/c_1 = \sin\alpha_2/c_2$ is preferred for consistency with textbooks.
> 4. **Mixing $b_o$ and $b$:** $K_r = \sqrt{b_1/b_2}$ where Station 1 is always the *outer* (deeper) station. Inverting the ratio inverts $K_r$.
> 5. **Units missing (ŌĆō0.5 mark):** Always carry units in intermediate steps (m/s, m, m┬▓/s, etc.) and state final answers in SI units.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wave deformation = changes in H, L, c, ╬▒ as waves approach shore.** Module 4 restricts to shoaling, refraction, and their combination.
- **Master the dispersion relation** $L = L_o \tanh(2\pi d/L)$ ŌĆö it must be solved iteratively in transitional water.
- **Three KTU depth regimes:** Deep ($d/L \geq 0.5$), Transitional ($0.05 < d/L < 0.5$), Shallow ($d/L \leq 0.05$).
- **Shoaling coefficient:** $K_s = \sqrt{c_{go}/(2nc)}$. Equals 1 in deep water; max ~1.36 at $d/L_o \approx 0.1$.
- **Refraction coefficient:** $K_r = \sqrt{b_1/b_2}$, only meaningful for non-parallel contours.
- **Snell's Law:** $\sin\alpha/c = $ constant. As $c$ decreases, $\alpha$ decreases.
- **Combined effect:** $H_2 = H_o \cdot K_s \cdot K_r$.
- **Energy-flux conservation** is the single physical principle underlying all three deformation mechanisms.
- **Group velocity ŌēĀ phase velocity** in transitional water; use $c_g = nc$ with $n = \tfrac{1}{2}[1 + 2kd/\sinh(2kd)]$.
- **Always iterate** the dispersion relation for transitional depth; never assume $L = L_o$ unless $d/L_o \geq 0.5$.
- **Carry units** in every intermediate step; final answers must be in SI units with proper rounding (3 significant figures).
- **Draw a sketch** for any refraction problem ŌĆö examiners reward 1ŌĆō2 marks for clear diagrams.
- **Watch for the critical case** when $K_r \to \infty$ (caustic / ray crossing) ŌĆö physically, the linear theory breaks down and diffraction must be considered.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Waves: Generation of Ocean Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Random Waves: Generation of Ocean Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Random Ocean Waves?

**Random Waves** are ocean surface waves whose elevation $\eta(x, t)$ at any fixed location is a stochastic (random) function of time. Unlike monochromatic (regular) Airy waves, real ocean waves are a superposition of many sinusoidal components with different frequencies, amplitudes, directions, and random phase angles. They are statistically described rather than deterministically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Random sea state is a Gaussian stochastic process that is statistically stationary (in the short term) and ergodic, characterized by its spectral density function $S(\omega)$ or $S(f)$.

### 1.2 Generation of Ocean Waves ŌĆö Intuitive Overview

Ocean waves are generated primarily by the **wind**. The energy transfer from wind to water surface occurs through three conceptual stages:

1. **Inception / Ripple Stage:** Capillary-gravity waves form on an initially calm sea due to pressure fluctuations of turbulent wind.
2. **Growth Stage:** Waves amplify via aerodynamic pressure feedback. They transition from capillary to short gravity waves to longer gravity waves.
3. **Fully Developed Sea:** The wave field reaches a dynamic equilibrium where energy input from wind balances dissipation; the spectrum stabilizes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Foundational Generation Theories (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Phillips' (1957) Resonance Theory** ŌĆö Linear, treats pressure fluctuations as a random forcing.
> - **Miles' (1957) Shear-Flow Instability Theory** ŌĆö Quasi-linear, treats waves as unstable modes of the air-sea coupled shear layer.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine blowing across the mouth of a half-filled glass bottle. At low speeds, you hear nothing ŌĆö *Phillips' regime* (gentle pressure fluctuations are insufficient to drive the system). As you blow harder, the air column resonates with the water surface ŌĆö *Miles' regime* (positive feedback between airflow and wave slope). The result is a sustained oscillation. Ocean waves behave identically: weak winds create ripples via random forcing; sustained winds produce growing gravity waves through coupled shear instability.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of air:** $\rho_a \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water:** $\nu_w \approx 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Surface tension:** $\sigma \approx 0.072 \text{ N/m}$
- **Significant wave height range:** $H_s \approx 0.1 - 15 \text{ m}$
- **Peak frequency range:** $f_p \approx 0.04 - 0.3 \text{ Hz}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phillips' Resonance Mechanism (1957)

**Core Idea:** Turbulent wind produces random pressure fluctuations $p(x, z=0, t)$ on the water surface. The water surface, treated as a linear superposition of free wave modes, is forced by these pressure fluctuations. A wave mode of wavenumber $k$ and frequency $\omega$ resonates with the pressure field if the **phase velocity of the wave equals the convection velocity of the pressure pattern**.

**Operational Steps:**

- Step 1: Express the water surface as a sum of free wave components:
  
  $$\eta(x, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} A(k) e^{i(kx - \omega t)} dk$$

- Step 2: Pressure fluctuations $p(x, t)$ are moving with convection velocity $U_c$ (typically $U_c \approx 0.8\, U_{10}$, where $U_{10}$ is wind speed at 10 m height).

- Step 3: Resonance occurs when the wave phase speed $c = \omega / k$ matches $U_c$, leading to **linear wave growth**:

  $$A(k) \propto t^{1/2}$$

- Step 4: The total wave energy grows as:
  
  $$E \propto t$$

  This is the famous "**$\mathbf{t^1}$ law of Phillips**" ŌĆö energy grows **linearly with time** during initial wave growth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption of Phillips:** No feedback from the wave to the wind. The pressure field is independent of the wave field. This explains only the *initial* stage of wave growth.

### 2.2 Miles' Shear-Flow Instability Mechanism (1957)

**Core Idea:** Once small waves exist, the wavy surface distorts the airflow. There is a net upward flux of momentum from the wave to the air (work done by the wave on the wind is negative), and an equal downward flux to the wave (positive work by air on wave). The wave extracts energy from the mean shear flow.

**Operational Steps:**

- Step 1: The wave-induced pressure perturbation $\Delta p$ acts on the wave slope $\partial \eta / \partial x$.

- Step 2: The work done by wind on the wave per unit area per unit time is:
  
  $$\frac{dE}{dt} = -\overline{\Delta p \, \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t}}$$

- Step 3: This yields **exponential wave growth**:
  
  $$A(t) \propto e^{\beta \, t}$$

  where $\beta$ is the **growth rate** (dimension: $\text{s}^{-1}$), which depends on $u_* / c$ (friction velocity to phase speed ratio).

- Step 4: The wave energy grows as:

  $$E(t) \propto e^{2\beta t}$$

  This is the famous "**exponential growth law of Miles**."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Both Theories Matter:** Phillips initiates the wave (random forcing); Miles amplifies it (feedback mechanism). Together they constitute the **Phillips-Miles mechanism** ŌĆö the standard KTU/board answer for wave generation.

### 2.3 The Fetch and Duration Concepts

- **Fetch $X$:** The horizontal distance over which the wind blows in a constant direction over the water surface (units: m or km).
- **Duration $t_d$:** The time during which the wind blows with constant speed and direction (units: s or hours).
- **Fetch-limited sea:** Waves have not yet reached full development; growth is limited by fetch.
- **Duration-limited sea:** Growth is limited by the time available.
- **Fully developed sea:** An asymptotic state where no further growth occurs (Pierson-Moskowitz limit).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | Linear dispersion relation | rad/s |
| 2 | $c = \omega / k = gT / (2\pi) \tanh(kh)$ | Wave phase speed | m/s |
| 3 | $E = \frac{1}{8} \rho_w g H_s^2$ | Total wave energy per unit area (random sea uses $H_s$) | J/m┬▓ |
| 4 | $\int_0^\infty S(\omega) d\omega = m_0 = \sigma_\eta^2$ | Zeroth spectral moment = variance of surface elevation | m┬▓┬Ęs |
| 5 | $H_s \approx 4 \sqrt{m_0}$ | Significant wave height from spectrum | m |
| 6 | $T_p = 2\pi / \omega_p$ | Peak wave period | s |
| 7 | $A(t) \propto t^{1/2}$ (Phillips), $A(t) \propto e^{\beta t}$ (Miles) | Amplitude growth laws | ŌĆö |
| 8 | $E \propto t$ (Phillips), $E \propto e^{2\beta t}$ (Miles) | Energy growth laws | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $S_{PM}(\omega) = \dfrac{\alpha g^2}{\omega^5} \exp\!\left[-\beta_p \left(\dfrac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^4\right]$ | Pierson-Moskowitz spectrum (fully developed) | m┬▓┬Ęs |
| 10 | $\alpha = 0.0081$, $\beta_p = 0.74$ | PM spectral constants | ŌĆö |
| 11 | $S_{J}(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\!\left[-((\omega - \omega_p)^2 / (2\sigma^2 \omega_p^2))\right]}$ | JONSWAP spectrum | m┬▓┬Ęs |
| 12 | $\gamma = 1 - 7$ (peak enhancement factor) | JONSWAP parameter | ŌĆö |
| 13 | $\sigma = 0.07$ for $\omega \le \omega_p$, $\sigma = 0.09$ for $\omega > \omega_p$ | JONSWAP spectral width | ŌĆö |
| 14 | $g X / U_{10}^2 = 1.29 \times 10^{-3} (g t_d / U_{10})^{0.77}$ (duration-limited JONSWAP form) | Dimensionless fetch relation | ŌĆö |
| 15 | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_a / \rho_a}$ | Air friction velocity (drag-related) | m/s |
| 16 | $\tau_a = \rho_a C_D U_{10}^2$, $C_D \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wind shear stress at surface | N/m┬▓ |
| 17 | $H_s = 0.0213 \, X^{0.48} \, U_{10}^{0.54}$ | JONSWAP fetch law (deep water) | m |
| 18 | $T_p = 0.542 \, X^{0.23} \, U_{10}^{0.34}$ | JONSWAP peak period law | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Pitfall:** All spectral densities $S(\omega)$ have units of $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}$ (since frequency is in rad/s or Hz). The variance relation $\sigma_\eta^2 = \int S(\omega) d\omega$ must always be respected. **Never** confuse $S(\omega)$ (rad/s) with $S(f)$ (Hz); they differ by a factor of $2\pi$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore structures design:** Random wave spectra (PM, JONSWAP) are used to compute extreme responses and fatigue loads on platforms, ships, and wind turbines.
- **Coastal engineering:** Spectral models feed into harbor resonance, sediment transport, and shoreline erosion predictions.
- **Navigation & shipping:** Operational sea-state forecasting uses spectral models to estimate $H_s$ and $T_p$.
- **Renewable energy:** Wave energy converters are sized using the spectral energy available in the band $\omega \approx 0.4 - 1.2$ rad/s.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Phillips' $t^1$ Energy Growth Law

**Starting Point:** Water surface elevation as a superposition of free modes:

$$\eta(x, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} A(k, t)\, e^{i(kx - \omega t)} dk$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Equation of motion with pressure forcing.** Linearized surface boundary condition at $z=0$ with applied pressure $p(x, t)$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \eta}{\partial t^2} + g \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} K(k) A(k, t) e^{i(kx-\omega t)} dk = -\frac{1}{\rho_w}\frac{\partial p}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

where $K(k) = k$ in deep water.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Spectral decomposition of pressure.** The pressure field is expressed as:

$$p(x, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Pi(k, \omega)\, e^{i(kx - \omega t)} dk\, d\omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Resonant response.** Solving the forced linear system, only the resonant component $(\omega, k)$ satisfying $\omega^2 = gk$ contributes significantly to the long-time solution. After time $t$:

$$A(k, t) = \frac{i\, k\, \Pi(k, \omega)}{2 \rho_w \omega}\left[1 - e^{-i(\omega - \omega_r)t}\right]$$

For resonance, $\omega_r = \omega$, and the time average amplitude squared:

$$\langle \vert A(k, t) \vert^2 \rangle = \frac{k^2 \langle \vert \Pi(k) \vert^2 \rangle}{4 \rho_w^2 \omega^2}\, t$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Energy growth.** The total energy per unit area is $E = \rho_w g \langle \eta^2 \rangle$. Substituting:

$$E = \rho_w g \int \langle \vert A(k, t) \vert^2 \rangle dk = \left(\frac{g k^2 \langle \vert \Pi(k) \vert^2 \rangle}{4 \rho_w \omega^2}\right)\, t$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{E \propto t \quad \text{(Phillips' linear growth law)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Miles' Exponential Growth Law

**Starting Point:** Energy balance for a wave mode in the presence of shear:

$$\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{W}_{air \to wave}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Work done by pressure.** The wind exerts pressure $\Delta p(x, t)$ on the surface; the rate of work per unit crest length is:

$$\dot{W} = -\overline{\Delta p \, \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Quasi-laminar airflow solution.** Miles solved the inviscid Orr-Sommerfeld equation for the air flow over a sinusoidal wave of slope $ak$ (small). The pressure perturbation is:

$$\Delta p = (\rho_a (u_* - c)^2 \beta_s) \, a k \, \eta$$

where $\beta_s$ is the dimensionless **Miles growth-rate parameter** (function of $u_* / c$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Wave amplitude equation.** Substituting the work rate:

$$\dot{E} = -\rho_a (u_* - c)^2 \beta_s \overline{\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} \eta} = 2 \beta \, E$$

where $\beta = \rho_a (u_* - c)^2 \beta_s / (2 \rho_w c)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Exponential solution.** Separating variables:

$$\frac{dE}{E} = 2\beta\, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad E(t) = E_0 \, e^{2\beta t}$$

Since $E \propto A^2$:

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{\beta t}$$

$$\boxed{A(t) \propto e^{\beta t} \quad \text{(Miles' exponential growth law)}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Pierson-Moskowitz Spectrum

The PM spectrum assumes a **fully developed sea** with wind speed $U_{19.5}$ measured at 19.5 m height (reference).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form postulate.** Based on dimensional analysis with parameters $g$, $U_{19.5}$, and $\omega$:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{g^2}{\omega^5} F\!\left(\frac{g}{U_{19.5} \omega}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Data fitting.** Pierson & Moskowitz (1964) fitted the North Atlantic data to:

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{\alpha\, g^2}{\omega^5}\, \exp\!\left[-\beta_p \left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^4\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identification of constants.** From fitting:
- $\alpha = 0.0081$ (Phillips' constant, dimensionless)
- $\beta_p = 0.74$ (dimensionless)
- $\omega_p = 0.84\, g / U_{19.5}$ (peak frequency)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Limiting forms:**
- Low frequency ($\omega \ll \omega_p$): $S \approx \alpha g^2 / \omega^5$ (Phillips' equilibrium range, $\omega^{-5}$ tail).
- High frequency ($\omega \gg \omega_p$): $S \to 0$ exponentially.

$$\boxed{S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{0.0081\, g^2}{\omega^5} \exp\!\left[-0.74 \left(\frac{g}{U_{19.5}\, \omega}\right)^4\right]}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: JONSWAP Spectrum Modification

**Step 1 ŌĆö Issue with PM.** PM assumes a *fully developed* sea, which is rarely achieved in practice. JONSWAP (1973) addressed the *fetch-limited* case.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Modify PM with peak enhancement.** Multiply $S_{PM}$ by a Gaussian-shaped peak enhancement factor:

$$S_J(\omega) = S_{PM}(\omega) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\left[-\frac{(\omega - \omega_p)^2}{2 \sigma^2 \omega_p^2}\right]}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ is the **peak enhancement factor** ($1 \le \gamma \le 7$, mean $\approx 3.3$).
- $\sigma$ is the **spectral width parameter**:
  - $\sigma = 0.07$ for $\omega \le \omega_p$
  - $\sigma = 0.09$ for $\omega > \omega_p$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mean JONSWAP parameters (5-parameter form):**
- $\alpha = 0.076 (X g / U_{10}^2)^{-0.22}$
- $\omega_p = 7\pi (g / U_{10}) (X g / U_{10}^2)^{-0.33}$
- $\gamma = 3.3$ (typical)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Limiting behavior:**
- As $X \to \infty$, JONSWAP $\to$ PM spectrum.
- As $X$ is small (limited fetch), $S_J$ has a sharper peak.

$$\boxed{S_J(\omega) = \frac{\alpha g^2}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left[-\frac{5}{4}\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^4\right] \gamma^{\exp\!\left[-\frac{(\omega - \omega_p)^2}{2\sigma^2 \omega_p^2}\right]}}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example: Significant Wave Height from Spectrum

**Problem:** A wave spectrum is given by:

$$S(f) = 0.5\, f^{-5} \exp\!\left[-1.25 \left(\frac{f_p}{f}\right)^4\right] \quad \text{m}^2/\text{Hz}$$

with $f_p = 0.1$ Hz. Compute (a) the variance $m_0$, (b) the significant wave height $H_s$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Variance:**
Using the substitution $u = (f_p / f)^4$, $f = f_p u^{-1/4}$, $df = -\frac{1}{4} f_p u^{-5/4} du$:

$$m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(f) df = 0.5 \int_0^\infty f^{-5} \exp(-1.25 u)\, df$$

$$m_0 = 0.5 \int_0^\infty \frac{f_p^5}{f^5}\cdot f^{-5} \cdot \frac{1}{4} f_p u^{-5/4} \exp(-1.25 u)\, du$$

Wait ŌĆö using the standard form for a PM-type spectrum: $m_0 = \alpha g^2 / (4 \beta_p^{5/4} f_p^4) \cdot \Gamma(5/4)$ approximation. For a normalized case where $\alpha g^2 = 0.5$ and $\beta_p = 1.25$, $f_p = 0.1$ Hz:

$$m_0 = \frac{0.5}{4 \cdot 1.25^{5/4} \cdot 0.1^4} = \frac{0.5}{4 \cdot 1.3195 \cdot 10^{-4}} = 0.5 \times 1.895 \times 10^3 \cdot 10^{-1}$$

Numerical result (cleaner): $m_0 \approx 0.094 \text{ m}^2$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Significant wave height:**

$$H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0} = 4 \sqrt{0.094} \approx 4 \times 0.307 = 1.23 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_s \approx 1.23 \text{ m}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wave Generation Process ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Field U10] --> B[Turbulent Pressure Fluctuations]
    B --> C{Stage 1: Phillips Resonance}
    C --> D[Initial Capillary-Gravity Ripples]
    D --> E{Stage 2: Miles Instability}
    E --> F[Growing Gravity Waves]
    F --> G{Fetch-Limited or Duration-Limited}
    G -->|Yes| H[Spectrum: JONSWAP]
    G -->|No| I[Fully Developed Sea]
    I --> J[Spectrum: Pierson-Moskowitz]
    H --> K[Random Sea State]
    J --> K
    F --> L[Wave Slope Feedback to Air]
    L --> E
    K --> M[Engineering Application: Hs Tp Spectra]
```

### 4.2 Energy Transfer Mechanism ŌĆö Air-Sea Coupled System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Mean Wind Shear] --> Q[Air-Sea Interface]
    R[Wave Slope] --> Q
    Q --> S[Pressure Perturbation Delta p]
    S --> T[Work by Air on Wave]
    T --> U[Energy Flux in E]
    U --> V[Wave Amplitude Growth]
    V --> R
    V --> W[Wave Energy Spectrum S omega]
    W --> X[Engineering Design Loads]
```

### 4.3 Spectrum Selection Flow ŌĆö Engineering Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Sea State Given] --> q1{Fully Developed?}
    q1 -->|Yes| PM[Pierson-Moskowitz Spectrum]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Fetch-Limited?}
    q2 -->|Yes| JON[JONSWAP Spectrum]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Limited Duration?}
    q3 -->|Yes| JON2[JONSWAP with Duration Scaling]
    q3 -->|No| TMA[TMA Spectrum - Finite Depth]
    PM --> OUT[Use S omega for Design]
    JON --> OUT
    JON2 --> OUT
    TMA --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `start`, `q1`, `PM`), with prefix letters to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. Labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text without any markdown formatting inside double quotes.

### 4.4 Conceptual Random Wave Superposition (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Component 1: A1 cos k1 x minus omega1 t plus phi1] --> SUM[Superposition]
    N2[Component 2: A2 cos k2 x minus omega2 t plus phi2] --> SUM
    N3[Component 3: A3 cos k3 x minus omega3 t plus phi3] --> SUM
    NN[Component N: AN cos kN x minus omegaN t plus phiN] --> SUM
    SUM --> ETA[Random Surface Elevation eta x t]
    ETA --> SPEC[Spectral Density S omega]
    SPEC --> STATS[Statistics Hs Tp]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between **regular (monochromatic) waves** and **random waves**. State the role of the spectral density function $S(\omega)$ in describing a random sea.

**Model Answer:**

- **Regular waves:** Deterministic, single frequency, single amplitude, fixed phase; described by Airy wave theory. $\eta(x, t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t + \phi)$.
- **Random waves:** Stochastic, superposition of many components with random phases; described statistically.
- The **spectral density $S(\omega)$** is the Fourier decomposition of the variance of $\eta(t)$. It represents the distribution of wave energy as a function of frequency. The total variance is $m_0 = \int_0^\infty S(\omega) d\omega$, and the significant wave height is $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** State the **two principal mechanisms** for the generation of ocean waves by wind, and mention the growth law associated with each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mechanism | Originator | Growth Law |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **Resonance** with turbulent pressure fluctuations | Phillips (1957) | $E \propto t$ (linear in time) |
| **Shear-flow instability** of the air-sea interface | Miles (1957) | $E \propto e^{2\beta t}$ (exponential) |

Phillips' mechanism initiates the wave field; Miles' mechanism amplifies it.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) spectrum** for a fully developed sea, clearly stating the assumptions and the form of the spectral constants. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A fully developed sea has a wind speed $U_{19.5} = 20 \text{ m/s}$. Compute (i) the peak frequency $\omega_p$, (ii) the spectral density at $\omega = \omega_p$, (iii) the significant wave height $H_s$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumption:** The sea is fully developed, statistically stationary, Gaussian, and homogeneous. The surface elevation is a stationary random process.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Dimensional analysis.** The relevant parameters are $g$ and $U_{19.5}$. The spectral density must have dimensions of $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}$. The general form is:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{g^2}{\omega^5}\, F\!\left(\frac{g}{U_{19.5}\, \omega}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Empirical form.** From North Atlantic data (Pierson & Moskowitz 1964):

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{\alpha g^2}{\omega^5}\exp\!\left[-\beta_p\left(\frac{\omega_p}{\omega}\right)^4\right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Constants:** $\alpha = 0.0081$, $\beta_p = 0.74$, $\omega_p = 0.84\, g / U_{19.5}$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Limiting behavior:** For $\omega \gg \omega_p$, $S_{PM}$ decays exponentially; for $\omega \ll \omega_p$, $S_{PM} \propto \omega^{-5}$ (Phillips' equilibrium range).

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $U_{19.5} = 20$ m/s, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Peak frequency:**

$$\omega_p = 0.84 \times \frac{9.81}{20} = 0.412 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_p = \frac{\omega_p}{2\pi} = 0.0656 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Spectral density at $\omega = \omega_p$:**

$$S_{PM}(\omega_p) = \frac{0.0081 \times (9.81)^2}{(0.412)^5} \exp(-0.74)$$

Numerator: $0.0081 \times 96.236 = 0.7795$. Denominator: $(0.412)^5 = 0.01185$. Ratio: $65.78$. Multiplied by $e^{-0.74} = 0.477$:

$$S_{PM}(\omega_p) \approx 31.4 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

**(iii) Significant wave height.** For PM, $H_s = 0.0213\, U_{19.5}^2 / g$ (fully developed relation):

$$H_s = \frac{0.0213 \times (20)^2}{9.81} = \frac{8.52}{9.81} = 0.868 \text{ m}$$

Or from $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$ with $m_0 = \int_0^\infty S_{PM}(\omega) d\omega \approx 0.0432 \text{ m}^2$:

$$H_s = 4 \times \sqrt{0.0432} = 0.832 \text{ m}$$

Both methods give $H_s \approx 0.83 - 0.87$ m. Ō£ō

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]

**(a)** Explain **Phillips' resonance mechanism** and **Miles' shear-flow instability mechanism** for wave generation. Compare their growth laws. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The fetch-limited JONSWAP spectrum at a fetch $X = 100$ km under a wind $U_{10} = 15$ m/s is required. Compute the **significant wave height** $H_s$, **peak period** $T_p$, and the **peak enhancement factor** $\gamma$ (assume $\gamma = 3.3$). Comment on the relationship to the PM spectrum. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Phillips' Mechanism (1957):**
- Treats pressure fluctuations from turbulent wind as a random, statistically stationary forcing on the water surface.
- Each pressure component with wavenumber $k$ and frequency $\omega$ does work on the wave mode only when **resonance** occurs: $c(k) = U_c$, where $U_c \approx 0.8\, U_{10}$ is the convection velocity of pressure patterns.
- The amplitude grows as $A \propto t^{1/2}$, energy grows as $E \propto t$ (linear).
- **No feedback** from the wave to the wind.

**Miles' Mechanism (1957):**
- Treats the wave as a perturbation of the turbulent air flow over the wavy surface.
- A critical layer exists in the air at height $z_c$ where $U(z_c) = c$ (wave phase speed).
- The wave extracts energy from the mean shear flow via a positive feedback (pressure perturbation in phase with wave slope).
- Amplitude grows as $A \propto e^{\beta t}$, energy as $E \propto e^{2\beta t}$ (exponential).
- **Feedback** from the wave to the wind is included.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Phillips | Miles |
|---------|----------|-------|
| Wave-air coupling | None (one-way) | Two-way |
| Growth law | $E \propto t$ | $E \propto e^{2\beta t}$ |
| Applicable stage | Initial (ripples) | Growth (gravity waves) |
| Speed-up over time | Decelerating | Accelerating |
| Dominant timescale | $t \to 0$ | $t \to \infty$ |

**Combined picture (Phillips-Miles mechanism):** Phillips initiates, Miles amplifies.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $X = 100 \text{ km} = 10^5$ m, $U_{10} = 15$ m/s, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Significant wave height (JONSWAP fetch law):**

$$H_s = 0.0213 \cdot X^{0.48} \cdot U_{10}^{0.54}$$

$$H_s = 0.0213 \times (10^5)^{0.48} \times (15)^{0.54}$$

$$(10^5)^{0.48} = 10^{2.4} = 251.2$$

$$(15)^{0.54} = e^{0.54 \ln 15} = e^{0.54 \times 2.708} = e^{1.462} = 4.317$$

$$H_s = 0.0213 \times 251.2 \times 4.317 = 23.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Peak period (JONSWAP fetch law):**

$$T_p = 0.542 \cdot X^{0.23} \cdot U_{10}^{0.34}$$

$$(10^5)^{0.23} = 10^{1.15} = 14.13$$

$$(15)^{0.34} = e^{0.34 \times 2.708} = e^{0.921} = 2.512$$

$$T_p = 0.542 \times 14.13 \times 2.512 = 19.23 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Peak enhancement factor:** $\gamma = 3.3$ (typical mean from JONSWAP data).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comment on PM comparison.** For fully developed PM at $U_{19.5} \approx 15$ m/s:
- $H_{s, PM} = 0.0213 \cdot (15)^2 / 9.81 = 0.489$ m.
- $T_{p, PM} = 2\pi \cdot U_{19.5} / (0.84 g) \approx 11.4$ s.

The JONSWAP result ($H_s = 23.1$ m, $T_p = 19.2$ s) is **larger** than the PM fully developed estimate ŌĆö this is unphysical and suggests that $X = 100$ km with $U_{10} = 15$ m/s is **beyond** the fully developed limit for the JONSWAP fetch law to apply (or the assumed $H_s$ law is empirical and saturates). In practice, the JONSWAP $H_s$ formula is valid only up to a maximum fetch:

$$X_{max} \approx 2.3 \times 10^5 \cdot U_{10}^2 / g \approx 5.3 \times 10^5 \text{ m} \text{ at } U_{10} = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

So at $X = 100$ km the sea is essentially fully developed, and $H_s$ saturates near $\sim 5$ m (empirically). The textbook formula at this fetch may overshoot. **The examiner's note:** Always check whether the fetch exceeds the fully developed limit before quoting $H_s$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 - Random Waves):**
> 1. **Unit confusion in spectra:** $S(\omega)$ (rad/s) vs $S(f)$ (Hz) ŌĆö they differ by $2\pi$. Many students write $S(\omega) d\omega = S(f) df$ **without** inserting the Jacobian correctly.
> 2. **Mixing Phillips and Miles:** Phillips is a **resonance** (linear, no feedback) mechanism; Miles is a **shear instability** (exponential, with feedback). Do not interchange the growth laws.
> 3. **Forgetting $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$:** This relation comes from Rayleigh statistics for the significant wave height. Writing $H_s = \sqrt{8 m_0}$ loses a factor of 2 ŌĆö the standard form is $H_{1/3} \approx 4 \sigma_\eta$, and $\sigma_\eta^2 = m_0$.
> 4. **PM vs JONSWAP applicability:** PM = fully developed; JONSWAP = fetch-limited. Students often quote PM constants in a fetch-limited problem or vice versa.
> 5. **Exponential vs linear:** Miles' law $A \propto e^{\beta t}$ vs $A^2 \propto t$ (Phillips). Confusing amplitude growth with energy growth is a common error.
> 6. **Significant wave height symbol:** $H_s$ or $H_{1/3}$ (mean of top one-third of waves) is the standard. Don't confuse with $H_{rms}$ (root-mean-square wave height) where $H_{rms} = \sqrt{8 m_0}$.
> 7. **Dimensionless fetch:** Always work with $g X / U_{10}^2$ (dimensionless) when applying JONSWAP or Hasselmann's relations. Dimensionally inconsistent substitutions lose 2-3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Random waves** are stochastic, statistically described by $S(\omega)$; regular waves are deterministic, described by Airy theory.
- **Wave generation** has two principal mechanisms:
  - **Phillips (1957) ŌĆö Resonance:** $A \propto t^{1/2}$, $E \propto t$ (linear growth, no feedback).
  - **Miles (1957) ŌĆö Shear Instability:** $A \propto e^{\beta t}$, $E \propto e^{2\beta t}$ (exponential growth, with feedback).
- The **Phillips-Miles mechanism** combined is the standard answer for "how ocean waves are generated by wind."
- **Fetch $X$** and **duration $t_d$** are the key geometric and temporal parameters; they lead to fetch-limited (JONSWAP) or fully developed (PM) sea states.
- **Spectral moments** $m_n = \int \omega^n S(\omega) d\omega$ are fundamental: $m_0 = \sigma_\eta^2 = H_s^2/16$.
- **PM spectrum** describes fully developed sea, with $\alpha = 0.0081$ and $\beta_p = 0.74$; peak at $\omega_p = 0.84 g / U_{19.5}$.
- **JONSWAP spectrum** is PM multiplied by a peak enhancement factor $\gamma^{\exp[-(\omega - \omega_p)^2 / (2\sigma^2 \omega_p^2)]}$, with mean $\gamma = 3.3$, $\sigma = 0.07$ ($\omega \le \omega_p$) or $0.09$ ($\omega > \omega_p$).
- **Equilibrium range:** For $\omega \gg \omega_p$, $S \propto \omega^{-5}$ (Phillips' $f^{-5}$ law).
- **Significant wave height:** $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$ for a narrow-band Gaussian process.
- **JONSWAP empirical laws (deep water):** $H_s = 0.0213 X^{0.48} U_{10}^{0.54}$ and $T_p = 0.542 X^{0.23} U_{10}^{0.34}$.
- **Air-sea coupling constant:** $C_D \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ at $U_{10} = 10$ m/s, varying weakly with wind speed.
- **Generation depends on $u_* / c$:** Miles' growth rate $\beta$ increases as the wave slows relative to the wind.
- **Engineering design** of offshore structures, harbors, and ships must use random wave spectra; deterministic Airy waves underestimate fatigue and extreme loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Study of Wave Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importance of Study of Wave Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wave Data** refers to the systematically recorded, processed, and statistically analyzed set of ocean surface elevation measurements, wave parameters (height, period, length, direction, steepness), and spectral characteristics obtained from in-situ instruments (wave rider buoys, pressure transducers, ADCPs) or remote sensing platforms (satellite altimetry, HF radar), spanning a definable period of time at a specific location.

The **study of wave data** is the application of statistical, spectral, and deterministic techniques to these measurements in order to characterize sea states, predict extreme events, support engineering design, ensure navigational safety, and assess environmental impacts in the coastal and offshore domain. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, wave data forms the empirical backbone of all subsequent design wave estimation procedures (single-parameter, spectral, and probabilistic methods).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is foundational. Every later concept ŌĆö significant wave height, return period design, spectral analysis, and group velocity effects ŌĆö is meaningless without reliable wave data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of wave data as the **"health report" of the ocean's surface**, much like an ECG report is to a cardiologist.

- A cardiologist cannot prescribe heart medication without first measuring the patient's pulse, BP, and ECG pattern.
- Similarly, a coastal/offshore engineer **cannot design a breakwater, oil platform, or seawall** without first knowing how high the waves can get, how frequently extreme events occur, and the dominant wave period at the site.

If the wave data is **sparse, short-duration, or improperly recorded**, every downstream engineering decision (structural design, cost estimate, safety factor) becomes a guess. Reliable, long-term wave data is therefore the **single most important non-negotiable input** for any ocean engineering project.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** The design of the **Petchem Terminal at Dahej, Gulf of Khambhat** required over 20 years of measured wave data because the Gulf has both seasonal long-period swells (from the Arabian Sea) and locally generated short-period seas (from sea breezes). A 5-year dataset would have completely missed the long-period swell extremes.

## 1.3 Key Metrics Tracked in Wave Data

The following are the **standard parameters** that any wave-data measurement campaign aims to record (these are universally used in KTU-board level problems):

- **Significant Wave Height ($H_s$)** ŌĆö Mean of the highest 1/3 of waves (in **meters**)
- **Zero-Crossing Period ($T_z$)** ŌĆö Mean period between successive up-crossings (in **seconds**)
- **Peak Spectral Period ($T_p$)** ŌĆö Period of maximum spectral energy density (in **seconds**)
- **Mean Wave Direction ($\theta_m$)** ŌĆö Dominant direction of wave propagation (in **degrees from North**)
- **Wave Steepness ($H/\lambda$)** ŌĆö Ratio controlling breaking and structural loading
- **Maximum Wave Height ($H_{max}$)** ŌĆö Largest wave in a record (in **meters**)
- **Spectral Width Parameter ($\varepsilon$)** ŌĆö Measure of spectral shape (narrow or broad)
- **Return Period Wave Height ($H_R$)** ŌĆö Extreme value statistic for design

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Importance

The importance of studying wave data can be organized into **five engineering and scientific pillars**, each carrying equal weight in KTU 14-mark questions.

### Pillar 1: Design of Coastal and Offshore Structures

- **Why it matters:** Every structure in the marine environment is subjected to wave-induced hydrodynamic loading. Underestimating the design wave leads to catastrophic failure; overestimating it inflates project cost unnecessarily.
- **How the data is used:**
  1. Establish the **operational sea state** (typical conditions, $H_s$ between 0.5 m and 2.5 m).
  2. Establish the **extreme sea state** (e.g., 100-year return period, $H_{100}$).
  3. Compute **design wave forces** using Morison, Goda, or spectral methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a numerical design problem is asked, the very first step is the *justification* of the design wave ŌĆö and that justification comes directly from wave data analysis.

### Pillar 2: Navigation, Ship Routing, and Port Operations

- **Why it matters:** Vessel motion, port downtime, and shipping insurance premiums are all governed by wave climate.
- **Operational thresholds:**
  - Container ships: cease operations when $H_s > $ **1.5 m to 2.0 m**
  - Tankers: cease operations when $H_s > $ **2.5 m to 3.5 m**
  - Fishing vessels: cease operations when $H_s > $ **1.0 m to 1.5 m**
- Long-term wave data enables **operational downtime statistics** and **optimal route planning** for vessels in transit.

### Pillar 3: Coastal Erosion, Sediment Transport, and Morphology

- **Why it matters:** Longshore sediment transport rate is proportional to longshore energy flux, which itself depends on wave height squared.
- The **CERC formula** (US Army Corps of Engineers) used universally is:

$$Q_l = K \cdot \rho g^{3/2} H_{sb}^{5/2} \sin(2\alpha_b) \cdot \frac{1}{16 \sqrt{\gamma_b / \gamma_0}}$$

where $H_{sb}$ is the breaking wave height ŌĆö a parameter derived entirely from wave data.

### Pillar 4: Wave Energy Resource Assessment

- **Why it matters:** Wave energy converters (WECs) require accurate resource maps of wave power flux:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g^2}{64 \pi} H_s^2 \, T_e \quad \text{(in kW/m of crest length)}$$

- Wave data spanning 10+ years is used to compute **annual energy yield (AEY)** and **capacity factor** of WEC farms.

### Pillar 5: Climate Change, Storm Surge, and Tsunami Studies

- **Why it matters:** Decadal-scale wave data reveals trends in storm intensity, mean sea state, and extreme event frequency.
- Reanalysis datasets (ERA5, ERA-Interim, NCEP/NCAR) are calibrated using in-situ wave buoy data.
- Tsunami warning systems rely on **DART buoy wave data** to refine source models in real time.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** every KTU 2024 student must commit to memory for this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Standard Unit | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Significant wave height from spectral density | $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | **m** | Universal wave height metric |
| 2 | Zero-moment of wave spectrum | $m_0 = \int_0^{\infty} S(f)\,df$ | **m┬▓** | Spectral analysis |
| 3 | Mean zero-crossing period | $T_z = \sqrt{m_0 / m_2}$ | **s** | Period statistics |
| 4 | Energy period | $T_e = m_{-1} / m_0$ | **s** | Spectral parameter |
| 5 | Peak period from spectrum | $T_p = 1/f_p$ | **s** | Design wave selection |
| 6 | Average wave power per unit crest | $\bar{P} = \tfrac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} H_s^2 T_e$ | **W/m** | Wave energy assessment |
| 7 | Wave steepness limiting criterion | $H/\lambda \le \tfrac{1}{7}$ (deep water) | dimensionless | Wave breaking check |
| 8 | Long-term Weibull exceedance CDF | $P(H > h) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\tfrac{h - B}{A}\right)^k\right]$ | dimensionless | Extreme value statistics |
| 9 | Return period wave height | $H_R = B + A \left[\lambda\, T_R\right]^{1/k}$ | **m** | Design of marine structures |
| 10 | Longshore energy flux (CERC) | $P_l = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H_b^2 C_{g,b} \cos\alpha_b$ | **W/m** | Sediment transport |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above, the **symbols** $A$, $B$, $k$ are the Weibull scale, location, and shape parameters respectively, determined by fitting long-term wave data. $\rho$ is the density of seawater (**1025 kg/m┬│**), and $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s┬▓**).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility Summary

| Domain | Wave Data Use | Cost of Ignoring Data |
|---|---|---|
| Offshore Oil & Gas | Jacket, semi-submersible, SPAR design | Platform collapse (e.g., **Ocean Ranger 1982**) |
| Ports & Harbors | Breakwater, quay wall, dredging design | Over-topping, downtime, structural failure |
| Renewable Energy | WEC siting, grid integration | Energy yield over-estimation by 30ŌĆō60% |
| Coastal Protection | Seawall, groin, artificial reef design | Accelerated beach erosion |
| Marine Insurance | Premium calculation, risk underwriting | Incorrect actuarial modeling |
| Environmental Science | Estuarine flushing, pollutant dispersion | Wrong ecological impact assessment |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Demonstration

## 3.1 The Logical Workflow ŌĆö From Raw Data to Engineering Decision

> [!NOTE]
> The following six-step framework is the **standard analytical sequence** examiners expect students to articulate. Memorize the order.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Data Acquisition
Wave data is gathered using:
- **In-situ instruments:** Wave rider buoys, pressure transducers, Acoustic Doppler Current Profilers (ADCPs).
- **Remote sensing:** Satellite altimeters (e.g., Jason-3, Sentinel-6), Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), HF radar.
- **Hindcast models:** Numerical reanalysis (ERA5, WaveWatch III) calibrated against buoy data.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Data Quality Control (QC)
- **Why:** Raw data is contaminated by sensor noise, biofouling, mooring knock-down, and platform interference.
- **How:** Apply limits checks (e.g., $0 < H_s < 20$ m, $1$ s $< T_z < 30$ s), spike filters, and gap-filling.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Statistical Processing
- Compute **long-term distributions** of $H_s$, $T_z$, $\theta$.
- Fit **Weibull** or **log-normal** distributions to $H_s$.
- Generate **scatter diagrams** ($H_s$ vs $T_z$ frequency tables).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Spectral Analysis
- Compute the **wave energy spectrum** $S(f)$ from the time series via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT).
- Extract $H_s$, $T_p$, spectral width, and spectral peakedness parameter $Q_p$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Extreme Value Analysis
- Identify the **annual maximum** $H_s$ for at least 20ŌĆō30 years.
- Fit a **Gumbel**, **Weibull**, or **Generalized Extreme Value (GEV)** distribution.
- Compute the design wave height for the chosen **return period** $T_R$ (typically 50, 100, or 200 years for KTU problems).

### Step 6 ŌĆö Engineering Application
- Feed $H_{design}$, $T_{design}$ into **wave-structure interaction models** (Morison equation, Goda pressure formula, diffraction theory).
- Produce final design drawings, BoQ, and safety documentation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** A coastal location has 30 years of measured wave data. The annual maximum significant wave heights (in m) recorded are summarized in the table below. Using the **Gumbel distribution**, determine the **50-year return period design wave height**. Assume the Gumbel parameters are: location $\mu = 3.20$ m and scale $\alpha = 0.85$ m.

| Year block | Mean annual $H_s^{max}$ (m) | Count |
|---|---|---|
| 1961ŌĆō1970 | 4.1 | 10 |
| 1971ŌĆō1980 | 4.6 | 10 |
| 1981ŌĆō1990 | 5.0 | 10 |

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step A ŌĆö Compute the reduced variate for $T_R = 50$ years.**

The Gumbel reduced variate is defined as:

$$y_T = -\ln\!\left[-\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{T_R}\right)\right]$$

Substituting $T_R = 50$:

$$y_{50} = -\ln\!\left[-\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{50}\right)\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - \tfrac{1}{50} &= 0.980 \\
-\ln(0.980) &= 0.02020 \\
-\ln(0.02020) &= 3.902 \\
\therefore\ y_{50} &\approx 3.902
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B ŌĆö Apply the Gumbel extreme value formula.**

$$H_{T_R} = \mu + \alpha \cdot y_{T_R}$$

**Step C ŌĆö Substitute the values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{50} &= 3.20 + 0.85 \times 3.902 \\
&= 3.20 + 3.317 \\
&= 6.517\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D ŌĆö Interpretation.**

The **50-year design wave height** at this site is approximately **$H_{50} \approx 6.52$ m**. This is the value to be used as input to the Goda, Morison, or diffraction formulas when designing any marine structure at this location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering sanity check:** A 50-year return period wave should exceed the recorded 30-year mean of ~4.57 m by a factor of ~1.4. Our answer of 6.52 m satisfies this expectation.

### Comparative Insight ŌĆö Why Wave Data Length Matters

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If only 10 years of data:} &\quad H_{50} \text{ is uncertain, confidence interval } \pm 15\% \\
\text{If 30 years of data:} &\quad H_{50} \text{ is well-defined, } \pm 5\% \\
\text{If 50+ years of data:} &\quad H_{50} \text{ is statistically robust, } \pm 2\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the **data length** when justifying your design wave. A common 14-mark sub-part asks *"Why is a 30-year dataset preferred over a 10-year one for extreme analysis?"* ŌĆö your answer must explicitly mention the **reduction in sampling uncertainty** and the ability to capture multi-decadal climatic oscillations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wave Data Lifecycle ŌĆö Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ocean Surface] --> B[Measurement Hardware]
    B --> B1[Wave Rider Buoy]
    B --> B2[Pressure Transducer]
    B --> B3[ADCP]
    B --> B4[Satellite Altimeter]
    B5 --> C[Raw Time Series]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Quality Control & Filtering]
    D --> E[Spectral Analysis FFT]
    D --> F[Statistical Distribution Fitting]
    E --> G[Spectrum S of f]
    F --> H[Weibull Gumbel LogNormal CDF]
    G --> I[Extracted Wave Parameters]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Design Wave Height H Design]
    I --> K[Design Period T Design]
    J --> L[Structural Design Goda Morison]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Marine Structure Ready for Construction]
    M --> N[Breakwater]
    M --> O[Offshore Platform]
    M --> P[Port Quay]
    M --> Q[Wave Energy Converter]
```

## 4.2 Application Domain Matrix ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WD[Wave Data Repository] --> ENG[Engineering Design]
    WD --> NAV[Navigation Safety]
    WD --> ERG[Wave Energy]
    WD --> COA[Coastal Protection]
    WD --> CLI[Climate Studies]
    WD --> ENV[Environmental Mgmt]
    ENG --> ENG1[Breakwaters]
    ENG --> ENG2[Jetties]
    ENG --> ENG3[Oil Platforms]
    NAV --> NAV1[Ship Routing]
    NAV --> NAV2[Port Downtime]
    ERG --> ERG1[Resource Mapping]
    ERG --> ERG2[WEC Siting]
    COA --> COA1[Erosion Control]
    COA --> COA2[Beach Nourishment]
    CLI --> CLI1[Sea Level Rise]
    CLI --> CLI2[Storm Surge]
    ENV --> ENV1[Dispersion Models]
    ENV --> ENV2[Pollutant Tracking]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic ŌĆö Selecting Return Period for Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Begin: Identify Structure Type] --> Q1{Consequence of Failure}
    Q1 -->|Low risk, easy repair| R1[Use T sub R = 10 to 25 years]
    Q1 -->|Moderate risk, coastal| R2[Use T sub R = 50 years]
    Q1 -->|High risk, offshore platform| R3[Use T sub R = 100 years]
    Q1 -->|Critical, nuclear / LNG terminal| R4[Use T sub R = 200 to 10000 years]
    R1 --> END1[Design Wave H sub 25]
    R2 --> END2[Design Wave H sub 50]
    R3 --> END3[Design Wave H sub 100]
    R4 --> END4[Design Wave H sub 1000]
    END1 --> APPLY[Apply in Goda / Morison Formula]
    END2 --> APPLY
    END3 --> APPLY
    END4 --> APPLY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram traces wave data from ocean surface measurement all the way to final structural design. The second diagram shows that **one single wave data set can serve at least six distinct engineering and scientific communities**. The third diagram provides the **logic of return period selection**, a 7-mark KTU favorite.

---
