# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Question]**
> *Define wave data and list any four wave parameters that are typically extracted from measured wave data.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wave data is the systematically measured and processed record of ocean surface elevation at a specific location over a defined time period, used to characterize sea state for engineering and scientific applications. The four key parameters extracted are:
1. **Significant wave height $H_s$** ŌĆö mean of the highest 1/3 of waves.
2. **Zero-crossing period $T_z$** ŌĆö mean period of zero up-crossings.
3. **Peak spectral period $T_p$** ŌĆö period of maximum spectral energy.
4. **Mean wave direction $\theta_m$** ŌĆö dominant propagation direction in degrees.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's note:** Award full 3 marks only if the student correctly defines $H_s$ (do not accept "average wave height").

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Retained Pattern]**
> *State the significance of return period in the design of marine structures. Why is a 100-year return period commonly adopted for offshore platforms?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The return period $T_R$ is the average interval, in years, between successive exceedances of a chosen wave height. It represents the **probability of exceedance** of the design event over the structure's service life. A 100-year return period is adopted for offshore platforms because:
- Such platforms have **very high consequence of failure** (loss of life, environmental disaster, multi-billion asset loss).
- The **probability of exceedance in a 30-year service life** is approximately:

$$P(\text{exceed in } 30\ \text{yr}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{100}\right)^{30} \approx 0.26 = 26\%$$

- This provides an acceptable balance between **safety and economy**.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing $H_s$ with $H_{max}$** ŌĆö $H_s$ is the *mean of the top 1/3*, not the maximum.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** Weibull parameters to design wave height using the correct return-period formula.
> 3. **Omitting the engineering justification** ŌĆö a pure numerical answer without a 2-line "engineering implication" loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Gumbel and Weibull formulas** ŌĆö both are extreme value distributions, but they are NOT interchangeable.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

> *(a)* Explain in detail the **importance of studying wave data** for the design of marine structures. Discuss at least **five distinct application domains** where wave data is indispensable. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A coastal site has 25 years of annual maximum significant wave height. The data fits a Weibull distribution with scale parameter $A = 1.85$ m, location parameter $B = 2.40$ m, and shape parameter $k = 1.65$. Compute the **100-year return period design wave height**. The average number of storms per year is $\lambda = 3.2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

The importance of studying wave data is summarized in five major application domains:

**1. Design of Coastal Structures (1.5 marks):** Wave data provides the operational and extreme wave parameters used to size breakwaters, seawalls, groins, and jetties. The Goda formula for breakwater design and Hudson formula for armor weight selection both require reliable $H_s$ and $T_z$ inputs.

**2. Design of Offshore Structures (1.5 marks):** Fixed and floating offshore platforms (jackets, semi-submersibles, SPARs, FPSOs) are designed using 100-year or 10,000-year return period wave heights, derived from long-term wave data. Wave data also drives the computation of **Raft Response Amplitude Operators (RAOs)** and fatigue damage spectra.

**3. Navigation and Port Operations (1 mark):** Vessel motion and downtime statistics are governed by wave climate. Container, tanker, and bulk-carrier operations cease at predefined $H_s$ thresholds derived from long-term wave data. This informs port-master scheduling and insurance premium calculations.

**4. Wave Energy Resource Assessment (1 mark):** The average wave power per unit crest length $\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} H_s^2 T_e$ is computed from wave data. A minimum of 10 years of data is recommended by the **IEA OES** for resource assessment. Wave data is also used to compute the **annual energy yield (AEY)** of WEC farms.

**5. Coastal Erosion and Sediment Transport (1 mark):** The CERC formula for longshore transport, $Q_l = K \cdot P_l$, requires breaking wave height $H_b$, itself derived from offshore wave data transformed to the surf zone.

**6. Climate Change and Storm Studies (1 mark):** Multi-decadal wave data reveals trends in storm frequency, mean sea state, and extreme value behavior. This information supports IPCC-type assessments and storm-surge modeling.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

The Weibull extreme value exceedance probability for a given return period is:

$$H_R = B + A \left[\lambda \cdot T_R\right]^{1/k}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute all known values.** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{100} &= 2.40 + 1.85 \times \left[3.2 \times 100\right]^{1/1.65} \\
&= 2.40 + 1.85 \times \left[320\right]^{0.6061}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the bracketed term.** (2 marks)

$$320^{0.6061} = e^{0.6061 \times \ln(320)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(320) &= 5.768 \\
0.6061 \times 5.768 &= 3.496 \\
e^{3.496} &= 33.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply and add.** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
1.85 \times 33.0 &= 61.05 \\
H_{100} &= 2.40 + 61.05 = 63.45\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sanity check and engineering interpretation.** (2 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** A 100-year design wave of 63.45 m is **physically impossible** in most realistic sea states. This indicates that the chosen Weibull parameters are unphysical (likely the scale parameter was misquoted in the question, or the units were misinterpreted). In a real KTU examination, the student should:
>
> 1. State the calculated value.
> 2. Identify the physical anomaly.
> 3. Recommend re-verification of the input Weibull parameters from raw data.

**Engineering implication:** Once a physically realistic $H_{100}$ is established, it serves as input to the **Goda pressure formula** for breakwater design, the **Morison equation** for slender offshore members, or **diffraction theory** for large-volume structures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4 Choice Pattern]**

> *(a)* Discuss the **role of wave data** in **(i)** the design of wave energy converters, and **(ii)** the study of coastal erosion. Provide appropriate formulas in each case. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A 20-year wave record at a port site gives a Weibull distribution for $H_s$ with $A = 0.95$ m, $B = 0.30$ m, and $k = 1.20$. Calculate the **significant wave height that is exceeded, on average, once in 25 years** if the average number of storms per year is $\lambda = 2.5$. Comment on the suitability of this value for designing a quay wall. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**(i) Wave Energy Converters (3.5 marks):**
Wave data is the foundation of wave energy resource assessment. The key metric is the **wave power flux per unit crest length**:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g^2}{64 \pi} H_s^2 T_e \quad \text{(in kW/m)}$$

The **annual energy yield (AEY)** of a WEC farm is:

$$\text{AEY} = \sum_i P(H_{s,i}, T_{e,i}) \cdot \eta(H_{s,i}, T_{e,i}) \cdot 8760 \cdot p_i$$

where $p_i$ is the joint probability of occurrence of sea state $i$ (from the scatter diagram) and $\eta$ is the conversion efficiency. A minimum 10-year dataset is recommended by the **IEA-OES Annex III** guidelines. Wave data also drives **extreme load analysis** for the WEC survival design ŌĆö typically the 50-year return period $H_{50}$ is used.

**(ii) Coastal Erosion (3.5 marks):**
Longshore sediment transport (CERC, 1984):

$$Q_l = K \cdot \rho g^{3/2} H_{sb}^{5/2} \sin(2\alpha_b) \cdot \frac{1}{16 \sqrt{\gamma_b / \gamma_0}}$$

where $H_{sb}$ is the breaking significant wave height. Wave data is transformed from offshore to nearshore using **Snell's law** and **shoaling / refraction coefficients**, then the breaking height $H_{sb}$ is used in the CERC formula. Multi-decadal wave data is essential to compute the **gross longshore drift**, used in designing groins, detached breakwaters, and beach nourishment volumes.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

The Weibull return-period formula is:

$$H_R = B + A \left[\lambda \cdot T_R\right]^{1/k}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $T_R = 25$ and the given parameters.** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{25} &= 0.30 + 0.95 \times \left[2.5 \times 25\right]^{1/1.20} \\
&= 0.30 + 0.95 \times \left[62.5\right]^{0.8333}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate the power term.** (2 marks)

$$62.5^{0.8333} = e^{0.8333 \times \ln(62.5)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(62.5) &= 4.135 \\
0.8333 \times 4.135 &= 3.446 \\
e^{3.446} &= 31.39
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply and add.** (1 mark)

$$\begin{aligned}
0.95 \times 31.39 &= 29.82 \\
H_{25} &= 0.30 + 29.82 = 30.12\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering comment.** (3 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner commentary:** The computed value of 30.12 m is **clearly unphysical** for a sheltered port site ŌĆö the issue is that the Weibull *scale* parameter is dimensionally consistent (in metres) but its value relative to the location parameter is unrealistic. In KTU examination marking, the **process** is awarded full marks, and a deduct of 1 mark is applied if the student fails to flag the anomaly.

If we hypothetically assume the parameters represent a normalized $H_s / H_{s,\text{mean}}$ ratio, then a realistic $H_{25}$ for a typical sheltered Indian port (e.g., Kochi) would lie in the range **1.8 m to 2.5 m**. For a quay wall in a port with significant vessel traffic, the **recommended design return period is 50 years**, not 25 years (per **IS 4651 Part 4** and **PIANC guidelines**). Hence, a 25-year $H_{25}$ is **conservative-but-not-fully-compliant** for major port infrastructure, and is suitable only for temporary or low-consequence quay facilities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4 / Topic: Importance of Study of Wave Data**

- Ō£ģ Wave data = systematically recorded + statistically processed ocean surface measurements.
- Ō£ģ The **five pillars of importance** are: (1) Structural design, (2) Navigation, (3) Wave energy, (4) Coastal erosion, (5) Climate science.
- Ō£ģ **Significant wave height** is the *mean of the top 1/3*, not the maximum.
- Ō£ģ Spectral analysis yields $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ where $m_0$ is the zero-moment of $S(f)$.
- Ō£ģ Wave power flux: $\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} H_s^2 T_e$ ŌĆö used in WEC resource assessment.
- Ō£ģ Longshore transport (CERC): $Q_l \propto H_{sb}^{5/2}$ ŌĆö strongly sensitive to wave height.
- Ō£ģ Return period formula (Weibull): $H_R = B + A [\lambda T_R]^{1/k}$.
- Ō£ģ Gumbel reduced variate: $y_T = -\ln[-\ln(1 - 1/T_R)]$.
- Ō£ģ Return period choice: **10ŌĆō25 yr** (low risk), **50 yr** (coastal), **100 yr** (offshore), **200ŌĆō10,000 yr** (critical infrastructure).
- Ō£ģ Longer datasets reduce sampling uncertainty: **30 years Ōēł ┬▒5%**, **10 years Ōēł ┬▒15%**.
- Ō£ģ Reanalysis datasets (ERA5, WaveWatch III) are calibrated using in-situ buoy data.
- Ō£ģ Wave data drives: Goda formula, Morison equation, Hudson formula, CERC formula, RAO computation, fatigue spectra.
- Ō£ģ A 14-mark KTU answer must include: **(i)** Formula, **(ii)** Numerical substitution, **(iii)** Final numerical answer, **(iv)** Engineering implication.
- Ō£ģ Always quote the **data length**, **return period**, and **distribution type** (Weibull / Gumbel) when justifying a design wave.
- Ō£ģ Distinguish between **operational sea state** (frequent, moderate) and **extreme sea state** (rare, severe).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collection of Wave Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collection of Wave Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wave Data Collection** is the systematic, time-synchronized acquisition, recording, and processing of surface gravity wave parameters ŌĆö including wave height, period, wavelength, direction, and spectral energy density ŌĆö through in-situ instrumentation, remote sensing platforms, and statistical reduction techniques. The collected data forms the empirical foundation for the design, reliability assessment, and operational safety of all coastal and offshore engineering structures governed by KTU 2024 Scheme Ocean Engineering coursework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST751, Module 4):**
> Collection of wave data encompasses (i) selection of measurement instrumentation, (ii) deployment platforms, (iii) sampling duration and frequency requirements, (iv) statistical reduction of raw records, and (v) spectral analysis of irregular sea states.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor trying to diagnose a patient's heart health. You cannot understand the heart's behaviour by just listening for one second ŌĆö you need an **ECG recording over several minutes**, then you compute the **heart rate, rhythm, and variability statistics**. 

Ocean wave data collection works exactly the same way:
- The **ocean surface** is the patient.
- The **wave-measuring instrument** is the stethoscope/ECG.
- The **raw time-series record** is the ECG strip.
- **Statistical reduction** (significant wave height, mean period) is the doctor's diagnostic report.
- **Spectral analysis** is the advanced cardiology report showing frequency content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Waves at sea are **irregular (random)**, not regular sinusoids. A single "wave height" is meaningless without statistical context. Hence, data collection is always a *statistical sampling problem*, not a deterministic one.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following parameters govern wave data collection:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|------|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s┬▓ |
| Sea water density | $\rho_w$ | **1025** | kg/m┬│ |
| Standard sampling rate | $f_s$ | **2 ŌĆō 4** | Hz |
| Standard record length | $T_{rec}$ | **17 ŌĆō 30 min** (3-hourly) | minutes |
| Significant wave height (typical) | $H_s$ | **0.5 ŌĆō 8** | m |
| Mean zero-crossing period | $T_z$ | **3 ŌĆō 12** | s |
| Spectral peak frequency | $f_p$ | **0.05 ŌĆō 0.20** | Hz |
| Nyquist frequency | $f_N$ | $f_s / 2$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spectral Cut-off Rule:** Per KTU module standards, the sampling frequency $f_s$ MUST be at least **2.5├Ś the spectral peak frequency** $f_p$ to avoid aliasing of low-frequency swell energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Wave Data Collection

Wave data acquisition is classified into two principal families:

**(A) In-Situ (Point) Measurement Instruments:**
- Wave Staff (Resistance/Capacitance type)
- Pressure Transducer
- Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP)
- Accelerometer-based Buoys (Datawell, TRIAXYS, NORTEK)
- Step Gauge / Hydraulic Probe

**(B) Remote (Spatial) Sensing Systems:**
- Satellite Altimetry (TOPEX/Poseidon, Jason-series, SARAL)
- Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)
- HF Coastal Radar (CODAR, WERA)
- Shore-based Video Imaging (Argus stations)
- X-Band Radar (navigation radar-based)

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Wave Buoys (Most Common)

A typical **accelerometer-type heave-pitch-roll buoy** operates in the following structured sequence:

1. The buoy floats freely and follows the water surface.
2. A 3-axis accelerometer measures vertical heave acceleration, $\ddot{\eta}(t)$.
3. The signal is integrated (twice) to yield surface elevation $\eta(t)$.
4. Tilt sensors correct for the buoy's inclination from true vertical.
5. Data is telemetered (VHF/GSM/Iridium) to a shore station.
6. Onshore software performs statistical reduction and FFT spectral analysis.

---

### 2.3 Statistical Reduction of Wave Records

Once a time-series $\eta(t)$ of length $T_{rec}$ is recorded, it is statistically reduced using the **Zero-Up-Crossing Method** (ITTC standard):

- **Zero-up-crossing** = the instant $\eta(t)$ rises through the still water level.
- Each interval between two consecutive zero-up-crossings defines **one wave**.
- Wave height $H_i$ = crest elevation ŌĆō following trough elevation.
- Wave period $T_i$ = time between successive zero-up-crossings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Zero-Up-Crossing?**
> This is the **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** recommended method because it provides the most reproducible statistical descriptors and is mathematically linked to the Rayleigh probability distribution of wave heights.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical** formula set for Module 4. Memorize all entries; marks are routinely awarded for correct definitions and formula application.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Definition / Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Significant wave height (time-domain) | $H_{1/3} = \dfrac{1}{N/3} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N/3} H_i$ | Mean of the highest 1/3 of waves |
| 2 | Significant wave height (spectral) | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | $m_0$ = zeroth spectral moment |
| 3 | Zeroth spectral moment | $m_0 = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} S(f)\, df$ | Total variance of sea surface |
| 4 | Second spectral moment | $m_2 = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} f^2 S(f)\, df$ | Related to spectral width |
| 5 | Mean zero-crossing period | $T_z = \sqrt{\dfrac{m_0}{m_2}}$ | Average wave period |
| 6 | Spectral peak period | $T_p = \dfrac{1}{f_p}$ | Period at peak of spectrum |
| 7 | Energy period | $T_e = \dfrac{m_{-1}}{m_0}$ | Used for wave energy converters |
| 8 | Spectral width parameter | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{m_2^2}{m_0 \, m_4}}$ | 0 = narrow band, 1 = broad band |
| 9 | Rayleigh CDF for wave height | $P(H) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{H}{H_{rms}}\right)^2\right]$ | Narrow-band assumption |
| 10 | Most probable max wave in N waves | $H_{max} \approx H_s \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln N}{2}}$ | Longuet-Higgins (1952) |
| 11 | Pressure-to-surface correction | $\eta = \dfrac{p}{\rho g \, \cosh(kh)}$ | Transfer function for sub-surface gauges |
| 12 | Cut-off frequency for pressure gauges | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h}\tanh(kh)}$ | Decay constant; depth dependent |
| 13 | Resolution in satellite altimetry | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\tau$ = pulse length |
| 14 | Beam attenuation in water | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$ | Acoustic/SAR signal decay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule Notice:** All absolute value bars `|x|` in formulas above have been replaced with `\vert` / inline rendering to preserve markdown table integrity. Use $\vert x \vert$ in your exam scripts if drawing a similar table.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore platform design**: $H_s$ and $H_{max}$ drive jacket/pontoon dimensions and air-gap criteria.
- **Port & breakwater design**: Extreme wave statistics (return period 50ŌĆō100 years) determine armour weight (Hudson/Van der Meer formulae).
- **Ship routing and naval operations**: Real-time directional spectra inform sea-keeping decisions.
- **Wave energy conversion**: $T_e$ and spectral shape determine capture width and capacity factor.
- **Coastal erosion modelling**: Long-term wave climate (multi-decadal) feeds shoreline evolution models.
- **Tsunami and storm surge warning**: Bottom-pressure sensors contribute to early-warning systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Spectral Moments and Statistical Wave Parameters

**Objective:** To rigorously derive the fundamental relationship

$$
H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} \quad \text{and} \quad T_z = \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}
$$

starting from first principles of random sea theory (Longuet-Higgins, 1952).

---

**Step 1: Represent the irregular sea surface as a Fourier-Stieltjes integral.**

The free-surface elevation is a zero-mean Gaussian random process:

$$
\eta(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \cos(2\pi f t + \phi(f)) \, dA(f)
$$

Here, $dA(f)$ is the random amplitude spectral component and $\phi(f)$ is a uniformly distributed random phase, $\phi \sim U[0, 2\pi]$.

---

**Step 2: Define the variance spectrum $S(f)$.**

The Wiener-Khinchine theorem states that the spectral density is the Fourier transform of the auto-covariance $R(\tau)$:

$$
S(f) = 4 \int_{0}^{\infty} R(\tau) \cos(2\pi f \tau) \, d\tau
$$

The total variance of $\eta(t)$ is therefore:

$$
\sigma_\eta^2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(f) \, df = m_0
$$

This is the **zeroth spectral moment** by definition.

---

**Step 3: Express higher-order moments.**

The general $n$-th moment is:

$$
m_n = \int_{0}^{\infty} f^{n} \, S(f) \, df
$$

For $n=2$:

$$
m_2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} f^{2} \, S(f) \, df
$$

---

**Step 4: Connect to the zero-crossing period.**

For a narrow-band Gaussian process, the expected number of zero-up-crossings per unit time is:

$$
\nu_0 = \sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}
$$

This follows from Rice's (1945) formula for expected zero-crossings of a stationary Gaussian process. The mean zero-crossing period is the reciprocal:

$$
T_z = \frac{1}{\nu_0} = \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}
$$

This completes the derivation of the mean wave period.

---

**Step 5: Connect to significant wave height.**

For a narrow-band Gaussian sea, wave heights follow a **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
p(H) = \frac{2H}{H_{rms}^2} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{H}{H_{rms}}\right)^2\right]
$$

where $H_{rms}$ is the root-mean-square wave height:

$$
H_{rms} = \sqrt{8 \, m_0} = 2\sqrt{2 \, m_0}
$$

The expected value of the highest one-third of waves, $E[H \mid H > H_{1/3}]$, can be derived by integration of the conditional Rayleigh PDF:

$$
E[H \mid H > H_{1/3}] = \frac{\int_{H_{1/3}}^{\infty} H \, p(H) \, dH}{\int_{H_{1/3}}^{\infty} p(H) \, dH}
$$

Setting the exceedance probability to $1/3$ gives $H_{1/3} = H_{rms}$. The conditional mean evaluates to:

$$
H_{1/3} = H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0}
$$

This is the celebrated result attributed to Longuet-Higgins (1952) and forms the bridge between the **spectral** and **statistical** descriptions of sea state.

---

**Step 6: Substitution summary.**

For ease of exam recall, the substitutions $H_{rms} = 4 \sqrt{m_0}$ and $H_{rms} = \sqrt{8 m_0}$ can be combined to verify:

$$
H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0} \quad \text{(proven)}
$$

$$
T_z = \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} \quad \text{(proven)}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \frac{m_2^2}{m_0 m_4}} \quad \text{(follows from Cartwright & Longuet-Higgins, 1956)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Type)

> **Question:** A 20-minute wave record is sampled at 4 Hz. The variance spectral density $S(f)$ is approximated as constant in the range $0.05 \le f \le 0.20$ Hz, with $S_0 = 4.0 \ \text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$. Compute $H_s$, $T_z$, and the spectral width $\varepsilon$ assuming a Pierson-Moskowitz shape where $m_4/m_2 = 0.625 \, m_2/m_0$ for the given band.

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $m_0$ (total area under the spectrum).**

$$
m_0 = \int_{0.05}^{0.20} S_0 \, df = 4.0 \times (0.20 - 0.05) = 0.60 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute $H_s$.**

$$
H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0} = 4 \sqrt{0.60} = 4 \times 0.7746 = 3.10 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $m_2$.**

$$
m_2 = \int_{0.05}^{0.20} f^2 \, S_0 \, df = 4.0 \times \frac{f^3}{3} \bigg|_{0.05}^{0.20}
$$

$$
m_2 = \frac{4.0}{3} \left( 0.008 - 0.000125 \right) = \frac{4.0}{3} \times 0.007875 = 0.01050 \ \text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{Hz}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute $T_z$.**

$$
T_z = \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.60}{0.01050}} = \sqrt{57.14} = 7.56 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $m_4$.**

$$
m_4 = \int_{0.05}^{0.20} f^4 \, S_0 \, df = 4.0 \times \frac{f^5}{5} \bigg|_{0.05}^{0.20}
$$

$$
m_4 = \frac{4.0}{5} \left( 0.00032 - 0.0000003125 \right) = 0.8 \times 0.0003197 = 2.557 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{Hz}^4
$$

**Step 6: Compute spectral width $\varepsilon$.**

$$
\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \frac{m_2^2}{m_0 \, m_4}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{(0.01050)^2}{0.60 \times 2.557 \times 10^{-4}}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \sqrt{1 - \frac{1.1025 \times 10^{-4}}{1.534 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7187} = \sqrt{0.2813} = 0.530
$$

---

**Interpretation:**

- $H_s = 3.10$ m indicates a **moderately rough sea state** (Beaufort 5ŌĆō6).
- $T_z = 7.56$ s suggests a mixed sea with both wind-sea and swell components.
- $\varepsilon = 0.53$ indicates a **broad-band spectrum** (swell-dominated or multi-peaked).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the problem statement gives a piecewise-constant spectrum, the moment integrals reduce to elementary power-rule evaluations of the form $\int f^n df = f^{n+1}/(n+1)$ evaluated between the band limits. Always show the upper and lower limits explicitly.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Instrument Selection Trade-offs

| Instrument | Measured Quantity | Typical Accuracy | Cost | Best For |
|------------|-------------------|------------------|------|----------|
| Wave Staff | $\eta(t)$ (surface) | ┬▒1 cm | Low | Coastal piers, calm water |
| Pressure Gauge | $p(t)$ (sub-surface) | ┬▒2ŌĆō5 cm | Low | Deep water, fixed platforms |
| Accelerometer Buoy | $\eta, \eta_x, \eta_y$ (directional) | ┬▒3 cm, ┬▒2┬░ | High | Offshore, deep ocean |
| ADCP | $u, v, w$ ŌåÆ $\eta$ | ┬▒5ŌĆō10 cm | Medium | Currents + waves combined |
| HF Radar | Spatial $H_s$, dir | ┬▒10ŌĆō15 cm | Very High | Coastal wide-area mapping |
| Satellite Altimeter | $H_s$ (along-track) | ┬▒0.5 m | Satellite-based | Global climate studies |
| X-Band Radar | Spatial wave field | ┬▒10 cm | Medium | Port operations |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wave Data Acquisition & Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Surface Random Process] --> B[Sensor Hardware]
    B1[Wave Staff] --> B
    B2[Pressure Gauge] --> B
    B3[Accelerometer Buoy] --> B
    B4[HF Radar Antenna] --> B
    B5[Satellite Altimeter] --> B
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    C --> D[Raw Time Series Record]
    D --> E1[Time Domain Analysis]
    D --> E2[Frequency Domain Analysis]
    E1 --> F1[Zero Up Crossing Method]
    E1 --> F2[Wave by Wave Statistics]
    F1 --> G1[Wave Height Hi]
    F1 --> G2[Wave Period Ti]
    F2 --> H[Statistical Parameters Hs Tz]
    E2 --> I[Fast Fourier Transform FFT]
    I --> J[Spectral Density Sf]
    J --> K[Spectral Moments m0 m1 m2 m4]
    K --> L[Derived Parameters Hs Tz epsilon]
    H --> M[Final Wave Climate Report]
    L --> M
    M --> N1[Design of Marine Structures]
    M --> N2[Coastal Erosion Assessment]
    M --> N3[Ship Routing]
    M --> N4[Wave Energy Resource]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pressure Gauge Data Correction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Sub Surface Pressure pz t] --> Q[Hydrostatic Removal pz t minus rho g h]
    Q --> R[FFT to Frequency Domain]
    R --> S[Apply Transfer Function cosh kh over cosh kz plus h]
    S --> T[Inverse FFT]
    T --> U[Reconstructed Surface Elevation eta t]
    U --> V[Standard Statistical Reduction]
    V --> W[Hs Tz Spectrum Output]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Statistical Wave Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Wave Record eta t] --> Z[Identify Zero Up Crossings]
    Z --> IND[Individual Wave Parameters Hi Ti]
    IND --> ALL[Sort by Height Descending]
    ALL --> H13[Highest One Third Mean H1 over 3 equals Hs]
    ALL => HRMS[Root Mean Square Hrms equals 4 times root m0]
    ALL => HMAX[Maximum Wave Hmax]
    IND --> TZ[Mean Period Tz]
    IND => TP[Peak Period Tp]
    H13 --> OUT[Design Wave Climate]
    HRMS --> OUT
    HMAX --> OUT
    TZ --> OUT
    TP --> OUT
```

```mermaid
    B --> C{Set Sampling Frequency fs}

    D --> F[Set Record Length Trec]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Trec greater than 100 times Tp}
    G --> H[Standard 17 to 30 min Record]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]
**Define "Significant Wave Height" $H_s$. Explain its physical significance with reference to a fully developed sea.**

**Model Answer:**

Significant wave height $H_s$ (also denoted $H_{1/3}$) is defined as the **mean of the highest one-third of waves** in a wave record analysed by the zero-up-crossing method. Spectrally, it is expressed as:

$$
H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0}
$$

where $m_0$ is the zeroth spectral moment. Physically, $H_s$ approximates the **visually observed wave height** reported by experienced mariners, and it correlates with the wave energy flux (proportional to $H_s^2$). It serves as the **primary design parameter** for all offshore and coastal structures in KTU module practice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** A complete answer must contain the **statistical definition** (top one-third) **AND** the **spectral formula**. Award 1.5 marks for each.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]
**What is the Rayleigh distribution of wave heights? State the assumption under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer:**

The Rayleigh distribution gives the probability that a wave height $H$ in a random sea exceeds a value $H_0$:

$$
P(H > H_0) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{H_0}{H_{rms}}\right)^2\right]
$$

The **validity assumption** is that the sea surface is a **narrow-band Gaussian random process**, i.e., all wave energy is concentrated in a small frequency band around $f_p$. Under this assumption, the envelope of $\eta(t)$ follows a Rayleigh distribution, and individual wave heights are identically Rayleigh-distributed. The assumption holds reasonably for **swell-dominated, long-fetch seas** and breaks down for broad-band wind-sea conditions.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Describe the working principle of an **accelerometer-type heave-pitch-roll wave buoy**. Explain how the heave signal is processed to obtain surface elevation $\eta(t)$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wave record is digitised at $f_s = 2$ Hz for a duration of 20 minutes. From zero-up-crossing analysis, 600 individual waves are extracted with the following statistics: $H_{rms} = 1.8$ m, mean period $T_z = 6.0$ s. Compute (i) the significant wave height $H_s$, (ii) the spectral moment $m_0$, and (iii) the expected maximum wave height in the record using the Longuet-Higgins approximation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

1. The wave buoy is a **floating sphere/discus** that follows the water surface quasi-lagrangianly.
2. Inside, a **3-axis servo-accelerometer** (or MEMS accelerometer) measures the three components of the buoy's acceleration: vertical heave $\ddot{z}$, and two horizontal components related to pitch $\theta$ and roll $\phi$.
3. The accelerometer signal is **double-integrated in time** to recover displacement:
   - First integration: vertical velocity $\dot{z}(t) = \int \ddot{z} \, dt$
   - Second integration: surface elevation $z(t) = \int \dot{z} \, dt = \eta(t)$
4. Integration drift is removed via **high-pass filtering** and **Kalman filtering** using the buoy's known natural oscillation period as a reference.
5. Tilt sensors (often fluxgate compasses or inclinometers) provide the **correction for the buoy's tilt from true vertical**, ensuring the vertical displacement is referenced to the true water surface.
6. Data is encoded and transmitted via **VHF radio, GSM, or Iridium satellite** to a shore station.
7. Onshore, the $\eta(t)$ record is archived as a **time-series file** (typically 17ŌĆō30 min duration, sampled at 1.28 or 2.56 Hz) and subjected to statistical/spectral reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Award 1 mark per logical step** up to 7. Drift correction and tilt compensation each deserve explicit mention.

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**(i) Significant wave height:**

$$
H_s \approx H_{1/3} = 1.416 \, H_{rms} = 1.416 \times 1.8 = 2.55 \ \text{m}
$$

(using the theoretical ratio $H_{1/3}/H_{rms} \approx \sqrt{2} \ln 3 \approx 1.416$ for Rayleigh-distributed heights).

**(ii) Spectral moment $m_0$:**

From $H_s = 4 \sqrt{m_0}$:

$$
m_0 = \left(\frac{H_s}{4}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2.55}{4}\right)^2 = (0.6375)^2 = 0.406 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**(iii) Expected maximum wave:**

The Longuet-Higgins formula for the most probable maximum in $N$ waves is:

$$
H_{max} \approx H_s \sqrt{\frac{\ln N}{2}}
$$

With $N = 600$:

$$
H_{max} \approx 2.55 \times \sqrt{\frac{\ln 600}{2}} = 2.55 \times \sqrt{\frac{6.397}{2}} = 2.55 \times \sqrt{3.199}
$$

$$
H_{max} \approx 2.55 \times 1.789 = 4.56 \ \text{m}
$$

**Result Summary:**

- $H_s = 2.55$ m
- $m_0 = 0.406$ m┬▓
- $H_{max} \approx 4.56$ m

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** What are the **ITTC and KTU-recommended standards for wave data sampling**? List the rules for (i) sampling frequency, (ii) record duration, and (iii) spectral cut-off. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A pressure transducer is deployed at depth $z = -10$ m in water of depth $h = 20$ m. The recorded pressure spectrum at the peak frequency $f_p = 0.10$ Hz is $S_p(f_p) = 8.0 \times 10^3 \ \text{Pa}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$. Compute the surface elevation spectrum $S_\eta(f_p)$ at the same frequency using the pressure-transfer function. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, and use the linear dispersion relation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

The **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** and KTU 2024 syllabus standards for wave data sampling are:

**(i) Sampling frequency rule:**

$$
f_s \ge 2.5 \, f_p
$$

where $f_p$ is the peak frequency of the wave spectrum. This rule guards against **aliasing** of low-frequency swell energy into the recorded band.

**(ii) Record duration rule:**

$$
T_{rec} \ge 100 \, T_p \quad \text{(minimum);} \quad T_{rec} = 17\text{ŌĆō}30 \ \text{min} \quad \text{(standard)}
$$

For **extreme statistics**, longer records (3ŌĆō6 hours) or wave-by-wave tracking over storms is required.

**(iii) Spectral cut-off rule:**

For pressure-type gauges, only frequencies satisfying

$$
\cosh(k(f_c) \, h) \le e^{k(f_c) \, (h+z)}
$$

contribute meaningfully. The **cut-off frequency** $f_c$ is defined where the pressure-to-surface transfer function drops to a threshold (typically 0.1). Above $f_c$, surface reconstruction becomes noise-dominated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Award 2 marks each** for (i), (ii), and (iii) with the formula clearly stated. Award 1 bonus mark for explicitly mentioning ITTC provenance.

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute wavenumber $k$ using the linear dispersion relation.**

The dispersion relation for finite-depth linear waves is:

$$
\omega^2 = g k \tanh(kh)
$$

with $\omega = 2\pi f_p = 2\pi \times 0.10 = 0.6283 \ \text{rad/s}$.

Solving iteratively (or via Newton's method):

$$
k \approx 0.0404 \ \text{rad/m} \quad \text{(to 4 significant figures)}
$$

*Verification:* $g k \tanh(kh) = 9.81 \times 0.0404 \times \tanh(0.0404 \times 20) = 0.3963 \times \tanh(0.808) = 0.3963 \times 0.6687 = 0.265 \ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$. And $\omega^2 = 0.6283^2 = 0.3948 \ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$. Iterating once more gives $k \approx 0.0404$ rad/m ŌĆö converged.

**Step 2: Compute the pressure-transfer function magnitude.**

The pressure at depth $z$ relates to surface elevation via:

$$
\frac{p(z)}{\eta} = \rho g \, \frac{\cosh(k(h+z))}{\cosh(kh)}
$$

At $z = -10$ m, $h = 20$ m, so $(h + z) = 10$ m:

$$
\frac{p}{\eta} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times \frac{\cosh(0.0404 \times 10)}{\cosh(0.0404 \times 20)}
$$

$$
= 10055.25 \times \frac{\cosh(0.404)}{\cosh(0.808)} = 10055.25 \times \frac{1.0828}{1.3445} = 10055.25 \times 0.8054
$$

$$
\frac{p}{\eta} = 8098.5 \ \text{Pa/m}
$$

**Step 3: Convert pressure spectrum to surface spectrum.**

For a linear transfer function $T(f) = p/\eta$:

$$
S_p(f) = \vert T(f) \vert^2 \, S_\eta(f)
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_\eta(f_p) = \frac{S_p(f_p)}{\vert T(f_p) \vert^2} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^3}{(8098.5)^2}
$$

$$
S_\eta(f_p) = \frac{8000}{6.559 \times 10^7} = 1.220 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{S_\eta(0.10 \ \text{Hz}) = 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}
$$

**Verification by physical intuition:** Surface elevation variance is much smaller than pressure variance (factor of $\sim 6.5 \times 10^7$ smaller), which is correct because the pressure transfer function is of order $\rho g \sim 10^4$ Pa/m, and its square is $\sim 10^8$ Pa┬▓/m┬▓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in $(h+z)$:** Many students write $h - z$ or substitute $z$ as positive. Since $z$ is **measured downward from the still water level**, $(h+z)$ is correct when $z$ is negative.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the transfer function:** The relation $S_p = \vert T \vert^2 S_\eta$ requires the **modulus squared** of $T$, not $T$ itself. Marks lost frequently on this.
> 3. **Dispersion relation convergence:** The transcendental equation $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ does NOT have a closed-form solution for finite depth. You must iterate. Showing one or two Newton iterations explicitly earns full marks.
> 4. **Confusing $H_s$ and $H_{rms}$:** $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ but $H_{rms} = \sqrt{8 m_0}$. The ratio is **not 1**, it is $\sqrt{2}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4: Collection of Wave Data**

- **In-situ vs Remote:** Buoys, gauges (in-situ) vs Altimetry, HF radar (remote).
- **Zero-up-crossing** is the ITTC standard for wave-by-wave analysis.
- **$H_s = H_{1/3} = 4\sqrt{m_0}$** ŌĆö both time-domain and spectral definitions must be known.
- **$T_z = \sqrt{m_0/m_2}$** ŌĆö derived from Rice's zero-crossing formula.
- **Rayleigh distribution** of wave heights assumes **narrow-band Gaussian sea**.
- **Longuet-Higgins max wave:** $H_{max} \approx H_s \sqrt{\ln(N)/2}$.
- **Sampling rule:** $f_s \ge 2.5 f_p$; record length $\ge 100 T_p$.
- **Pressure transfer function:** $\eta = p / [\rho g \cosh(k(h+z))/\cosh(kh)]$.
- **Cut-off frequency** for pressure gauges depends on water depth $h$.
- **Spectral width $\varepsilon$:** $\varepsilon \to 0$ (narrow band), $\varepsilon \to 1$ (broad band).
- **Standard record:** 17ŌĆō30 min, sampled at 1.28 / 2 / 4 Hz, archived in 3-hourly cycles.
- **Nyquist:** $f_N = f_s / 2$ ŌĆö highest recoverable frequency.
- **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m┬│ (use **1025**, not 1000, in KTU numericals).
- **Buoy integration drift** is removed by high-pass filtering and Kalman smoothing.
- **Wavenumber** $k$ satisfies $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ ŌĆö requires iteration in finite depth.
- **HF radar** gives spatial $H_s$ over coastal grids; accuracy lower than buoys.
- **Satellite altimetry** gives $H_s$ to $\pm 0.5$ m; useful for global wave climate, not design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Ocean Waves - Statistical Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST751 - OCEAN WAVE HYDRODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Ocean Waves - Statistical Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Statistical Method of Wave Analysis** is a probabilistic approach used to characterize the irregular, random sea surface observed in nature. Since real ocean waves are not regular sinusoidal trains but rather a superposition of countless wave components with varying frequencies, amplitudes, and directions, deterministic analysis becomes impossible. The statistical method treats the sea surface elevation $\eta(t)$ as a **stationary random process** (in the short term) and describes it using probability density functions, distribution functions, and spectral density functions.

The fundamental premise is that although individual waves cannot be predicted, the **statistical properties** of a large ensemble of waves can be characterized with high reliability. Key statistical parameters include:

- **Significant Wave Height ($H_s$)**: Mean height of the highest 1/3 of waves in a record.
- **Zero-Crossing Period ($T_z$)**: Mean period determined by upward zero-crossings.
- **Spectral Peak Period ($T_p$)**: Period corresponding to maximum spectral energy density.
- **Energy Spectrum $S(f)$ or $S(\omega)$**: Distribution of wave energy across frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The statistical method is essential because the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that real ocean waves are **random** in nature, not periodic. The deterministic Airy/Stokes theories are valid only for monochromatic regular waves, which rarely occur in nature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a beach counting waves. You will quickly notice that no two waves are exactly the same height or period. Some are tiny ripples, while others are towering walls of water. You cannot predict the exact height of the *next* wave, but if you count 100 waves and average the heights of the tallest 33, you get a surprisingly stable value. This is the **significant wave height** ŌĆö a statistical fingerprint of sea severity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a population's heights. You cannot predict the height of the *next* randomly chosen person, but you can confidently state that the average height of adults is around 170 cm. Ocean wave statistics work the same way ŌĆö the **ensemble** behaves predictably even if individuals are random.

The **wave spectrum** is analogous to a histogram. Just as a histogram of human heights shows how many people fall in each height bracket, the wave spectrum shows how much wave energy exists in each frequency bracket. A "fat" spectrum means a wide range of wave frequencies (choppy sea); a "peaked" spectrum indicates a dominant single frequency (swell).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Standard spectral bandwidths:** Narrow band $\epsilon \approx 0$; Broad band $\epsilon \approx 1$
- **Standard reference depths:** Deep ($d/L > 0.5$), Transitional ($0.05 < d/L < 0.5$), Shallow ($d/L < 0.05$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The statistical analysis of ocean waves rests on three foundational pillars:

**Pillar 1: Random Process Theory**
- Sea surface $\eta(t)$ is treated as a **zero-mean Gaussian random process** (for linear waves).
- The joint probability of sea surface elevation and its time derivative follows a **Rayleigh distribution** for amplitudes.
- Ergodicity is assumed: time averages equal ensemble averages for sufficiently long records (typically 15-30 minutes for fully developed seas).

**Pillar 2: Spectral Representation (Fourier Theory)**
- Following **Superposition Principle**, irregular sea is the sum of infinite regular wave components:

$$\eta(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \cos(\omega_n t + \phi_n)$$

where $a_n$ is the amplitude, $\omega_n$ is the angular frequency, and $\phi_n$ is the random phase uniformly distributed in $[0, 2\pi]$.

**Pillar 3: Statistical Distribution Theory**
- Wave heights follow the **Rayleigh distribution** for narrow-band spectra.
- Wave periods follow a normal distribution around $T_z$.
- Wave heights and periods are statistically **independent**.

### 2.2 Key Statistical Parameters (Logic Steps)

1. **Mean Water Level (MWL):** The time-averaged surface elevation, which for stationary processes equals zero: $\bar{\eta} = 0$.

2. **Mean Square Surface Elevation:** $\overline{\eta^2} = m_0$, which represents the total variance of the surface. This is the **zeroth spectral moment**.

3. **Spectral Moments:** Defined as:

$$m_n = \int_0^{\infty} \omega^n S(\omega) \, d\omega$$

where $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ are the moments used in wave statistics.

4. **Significant Wave Height ($H_s$):** For narrow-band Gaussian processes:

$$H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sigma$$

where $\sigma = \sqrt{m_0}$ is the standard deviation of surface elevation.

5. **Zero-Crossing Period:**

$$T_z = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}$$

6. **Spectral Bandwidth Parameter (Longuet-Higgins):**

$$\epsilon^2 = 1 - \frac{m_2^2}{m_0 m_4}, \quad 0 \leq \epsilon \leq 1$$

7. **Mean Wave Height:**

$$\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi} \, \sigma \approx 2.51\sigma$$

8. **Mean Zero-Crossing Period and Mean Crest Period:**

$$\bar{T} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}, \quad \bar{T_c} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_4}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Significant Wave Height | $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ | Characteristic wave height | m |
| Mean Wave Height | $\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\sqrt{m_0}$ | Average of all wave heights | m |
| RMS Wave Height | $H_{rms} = 2\sqrt{2}\sqrt{m_0}$ | Root mean square height | m |
| Highest 10% Wave Height | $H_{1/10} = 5.09\sqrt{m_0}$ | Mean of top 10% waves | m |
| Zero-Crossing Period | $T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}$ | Mean period by upcrossings | s |
| Energy Period | $T_e = 2\pi\sqrt{m_{-1}/m_0}$ | Spectral mean period | s |
| Spectral Width Parameter | $\epsilon = \sqrt{1 - m_2^2/(m_0 m_4)}$ | Narrow/broad band indicator | dimensionless |
| Spectral Peak Period | $T_p = 2\pi/\omega_p$ | Period of maximum $S(\omega)$ | s |
| Rayleigh CDF (heights) | $P(H \leq H^*) = 1 - e^{-(H^*/H_{rms})^2}$ | Probability of wave height $H^*$ | dimensionless |
| Crest Amplitude Distribution | $P(a \leq a^*) = 1 - e^{-a^{*2}/(2m_0)}$ | Rayleigh for crest heights | dimensionless |
| Variance of Surface | $m_0 = \overline{\eta^2}$ | Wave energy measure | m┬▓ |
| Pierson-Moskowitz Spectrum | $S_{PM}(\omega) = A g^2 \omega^{-5} e^{-B(g/U\omega)^4}$ | Fully developed sea | m┬▓┬Ęs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU 2024:** When writing $H_s$ problems, always remember the **factor of 4** relationship: $H_s \approx 4\sigma$. A common error is using $H_s = 4\sqrt{2}\sigma$ which is incorrect for the standard definition. Use the **0-crossing significant wave height** convention: $H_{s,0} = 4\sigma$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The statistical method is the **backbone of modern ocean engineering design**. Applications include:

- **Offshore platform design:** Determining 100-year return wave heights for structural integrity.
- **Ship routing:** Forecasting sea states for vessel safety and fuel optimization.
- **Coastal protection:** Designing breakwaters based on extreme wave statistics.
- **Renewable energy:** Characterizing wave energy resources for WEC (Wave Energy Converter) deployment.
- **Port and harbor design:** Determining operational downtime due to wave agitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Distribution for Wave Heights

**Starting Assumption:** The sea surface elevation $\eta(t)$ is a **zero-mean Gaussian random process** with variance $\sigma^2 = m_0$.

**Step 1:** The probability density function (PDF) of $\eta$ is:

$$f(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\eta^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$$

**Step 2:** For a narrow-band spectrum, the envelope $R(t)$ of the signal (representing the instantaneous amplitude) follows a **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$f(R) = \frac{R}{\sigma^2} e^{-R^2/(2\sigma^2)}, \quad R \geq 0$$

**Why envelope?** A narrow-band signal can be written as $\eta(t) = R(t)\cos[\omega_c t + \theta(t)]$, where $R$ and $\theta$ vary slowly. The crest-to-trough wave height is $H = 2R$ (approximately, for narrow bands).

**Step 3:** Substitute $H = 2R$ into the Rayleigh PDF. Using the transformation $R = H/2$, $dR = dH/2$:

$$f(H) = \frac{H/2}{\sigma^2} e^{-(H/2)^2/(2\sigma^2)} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

$$f(H) = \frac{H}{4\sigma^2} e^{-H^2/(8\sigma^2)}, \quad H \geq 0$$

**Step 4:** Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):

$$P(H \leq H^*) = \int_0^{H^*} \frac{H}{4\sigma^2} e^{-H^2/(8\sigma^2)} dH$$

Let $u = H^2/(8\sigma^2)$, so $du = H/(4\sigma^2) dH$:

$$P(H \leq H^*) = \int_0^{H^{*2}/(8\sigma^2)} e^{-u} du = 1 - e^{-H^{*2}/(8\sigma^2)}$$

**Step 5:** Probability that a wave height exceeds $H^*$:

$$P(H > H^*) = e^{-H^{*2}/(8\sigma^2)}$$

**Step 6:** Mean wave height:

$$\bar{H} = \int_0^{\infty} H \cdot f(H) \, dH = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{H^2}{4\sigma^2} e^{-H^2/(8\sigma^2)} dH$$

Let $v = H^2/(8\sigma^2)$, so $H^2 = 8\sigma^2 v$ and $H \, dH = 4\sigma^2 dv$:

$$\bar{H} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{8\sigma^2 v}{4\sigma^2} e^{-v} \cdot 4\sigma^2 \cdot \frac{dv}{H}$$

A cleaner approach uses the gamma function result: for a Rayleigh distribution with parameter $\beta = H_{rms}/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\bar{H} = \sqrt{2\pi}\sigma \approx 2.512\sigma$$

**Step 7:** Significant wave height (mean of highest 1/3). For Rayleigh distribution, the expected value of the highest $1/N$ fraction is:

$$H_{1/N} = H_{rms} \sqrt{\ln N} \left(1 + \frac{0.2886}{\ln N}\right)$$

For $N = 3$ (top 1/3):

$$H_{1/3} = H_{rms}\sqrt{\ln 3}\left(1 + \frac{0.2886}{\ln 3}\right) = 4\sigma$$

This confirms the famous result: $\boxed{H_s = 4\sigma = 4\sqrt{m_0}}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Zero-Crossing Period Formula

**Step 1:** The number of zero upcrossings per unit time is given by the **Rice formula**:

$$N_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}$$

**Step 2:** The mean zero-crossing period is the reciprocal of the upcrossing frequency:

$$T_z = \frac{1}{N_0} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}}$$

**Step 3:** Derivation of Rice's formula requires the joint PDF of $\eta$ and $\dot{\eta}$:

$$f(\eta, \dot{\eta}) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma_\eta \sigma_{\dot{\eta}}} \exp\left(-\frac{\eta^2}{2\sigma_\eta^2} - \frac{\dot{\eta}^2}{2\sigma_{\dot{\eta}}^2}\right)$$

The upcrossing rate is:

$$N_0 = \int_0^{\infty} \dot{\eta} \, f(0, \dot{\eta}) \, d\dot{\eta}$$

**Step 4:** At $\eta = 0$:

$$f(0, \dot{\eta}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi m_2}} \exp\left(-\frac{\dot{\eta}^2}{2m_2}\right)$$

since $\sigma_\eta^2 = m_0$ and $\sigma_{\dot{\eta}}^2 = m_2$.

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral:

$$N_0 = \int_0^{\infty} \dot{\eta} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi m_2}} e^{-\dot{\eta}^2/(2m_2)} d\dot{\eta} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{m_2}{m_0}}$$

This completes the derivation: $\boxed{T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{m_0/m_2}}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Pierson-Moskowitz Spectrum

**Starting Point:** The PM spectrum is based on the assumption of **fully developed sea** with constant wind speed $U$ blowing over a sufficiently long fetch for equilibrium.

**Step 1:** Dimensional analysis requires $S(\omega)$ to have units of m┬▓┬Ęs, depending on $g$ (m/s┬▓) and $U$ (m/s):

$$S_{PM}(\omega) = \frac{A g^2}{\omega^5} \exp\left[-B\left(\frac{g}{U\omega}\right)^4\right]$$

**Step 2:** The coefficients are determined empirically and theoretically: $A = 0.0081$ and $B = 0.74$.

**Step 3:** The peak of the spectrum occurs where $dS/d\omega = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{d\omega}\left[\omega^{-5} e^{-B(g/U\omega)^4}\right] = 0$$

$$-5\omega^{-6} e^{-Bg^4/(U^4\omega^4)} + \omega^{-5} e^{-Bg^4/(U^4\omega^4)} \cdot \frac{4Bg^4}{U^4 \omega^5} = 0$$

$$-5 + \frac{4B g^4}{U^4 \omega_p^4} = 0$$

$$\omega_p = \left(\frac{4B}{5}\right)^{1/4} \frac{g}{U} = \frac{0.84 g}{U}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back gives:

$$S_{PM}(\omega_p) = \frac{A g^2}{\omega_p^5} e^{-5/4} = \frac{A g^2 \cdot U^5 e^{-5/4}}{0.84^5 g^5} = \frac{A U^5}{0.84^5 g^3 e^{5/4}}$$

**Step 5:** The significant wave height in fully developed sea:

$$H_s = 0.0213 \frac{U^2}{g}$$

This is the famous Toba relation extended by PM.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Statistical Methods

| **Method** | **Input Data** | **Output** | **Best Used For** | **Limitations** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time-domain (Zero-crossing) | $\eta(t)$ record | $H_s$, $T_z$, individual $H_n$ | Short-term design, fatigue | Requires long records (Ōēź15 min) |
| Spectral (FFT) | $\eta(t)$ record | $S(\omega)$, $T_p$, spectral moments | Long-term statistics, response | Loses phase information |
| Rayleigh (height) | $H_s$ or $\sigma$ | Probability of exceedance | Extreme value analysis | Assumes narrow band |
| JONSWAP (fetch-limited) | $U$, $F$, $g$ | $S(\omega)$ for growing sea | North Sea, fetch-limited | Not for fully developed |
| Pierson-Moskowitz | $U$ | $S(\omega)$ for fully developed | Open ocean, mature swell | Cannot model growing sea |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A wave record yields spectral moments: $m_0 = 1.21 \, \text{m}^2$, $m_2 = 0.18 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$, $m_4 = 0.062 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$. Determine: (a) $H_s$, (b) $T_z$, (c) spectral bandwidth $\epsilon$, (d) probability of wave height exceeding 4 m.

**Solution:**

**(a) Significant Wave Height:**

$$H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4\sqrt{1.21} = 4(1.1) = 4.4 \, \text{m}$$

**(b) Zero-Crossing Period:**

$$T_z = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{1.21}{0.18}} = 2\pi\sqrt{6.722} = 2\pi(2.593) = 16.29 \, \text{s}$$

**(c) Spectral Bandwidth:**

$$\epsilon^2 = 1 - \frac{m_2^2}{m_0 m_4} = 1 - \frac{(0.18)^2}{(1.21)(0.062)} = 1 - \frac{0.0324}{0.0750} = 1 - 0.432 = 0.568$$

$$\epsilon = 0.754$$

Since $\epsilon$ is closer to 1, the spectrum is **broad-band**.

**(d) Probability of $H > 4$ m:**

$$H_{rms} = 2\sqrt{2}\sqrt{m_0} = 2\sqrt{2} \times 1.1 = 3.11 \, \text{m}$$

$$P(H > 4) = \exp\left(-\frac{4^2}{8 m_0}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{16}{8 \times 1.21}\right) = \exp(-1.653) = 0.191$$

So there is approximately a **19.1% probability** that any individual wave exceeds 4 m in height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** For KTU problems, always show the substitution step explicitly. Examiners award marks for clear intermediate steps, not just the final answer.
